# Interpretation

## Defintitions -- "inclusive"

## Ordinary meaning:

In the absence of a statutory definition it is necessary to give the word its ordinary meaning, and that means "the ordinary meaning conveyed by a provision having regard to its context in the Act and to the purpose or object of the Act (Acts interpretation Act 1931, 8B(3). Recourse may be made to the dictionary meaning "to confirm the interpretation conveyed by the ordinary meaning of the provision" (8B(1)(c) Acts interp Act.

[Can't look at subordinate legislation to interpret powering legislation]{.mark} (***Attorney General v Barnes Tassc 2023)***

# Reckoning of time:

Tasmanian statutory interpretation requires you to *exclude* the day the period is dated from when you reckon a period "dated or reckoned from a given day". *Section 29 of the Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas)* states that a period reckoned from a given day "shall be reckoned exclusively of that day" --- so the first day is excluded from the calculation.

NOTE sentencing orders usually say "commencing on" so it would be inclusive of the same day, as it is express by the court. If it were to say "from x date" then it would be the next day. Confusing. Look at the Acts interp Act.

And Def's are usually given full credit for parts of days served.

Supported by consistent authority in the reckoning of time cases: *Prowse v McIntyre* \[1961\] Tas SR 9 (re exclusion of first day unless statute indicates otherwise).

General principle of law that the law is not concerned with divisions of a day, and fractions of a day are disregarded: *Prowse v McIntyre *[\[1961\] HCA 79](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1961/79.html); [(1961) 111 CLR 264](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281961%29%20111%20CLR%20264); *Forster v Jododex Australia Pty Ltd *[\[1972\] HCA 61](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1972/61.html); [(1972) 127 CLR 421](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281972%29%20127%20CLR%20421) at 446, per Gibbs J. In *Forster*,* *Gibbs J referred to Sir William Grant MR in *Lester v Garland *(1808) 15 VES Jun, at 257 (33 ER, at 752):

# Commissioner for declarations:

Defined in the Oaths Act Tas -- includes Police and Legal Practitioners. Allows us to take stat decs.

Section 13 Oaths Act

A commissioner for declarations signing in that capacity is to add after his or her signature --

\(a\) \"commissioner for declarations\"; and

\(b\) his or her title, if qualified as a commissioner under [section 12(2)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/whole/html/inforce/current/act-2001-077#GS12@Gs2@EN) .

Execution of Warrant of arrests Governed by section 301 of the CODE. No issue so long as reasonable belief warrant eists and copy given to def as soon as practicable. Note necessary to have it at time of arrest if above complied with.

## Power of arrest without warrant

### Need to state reason

 The *Criminal Code, *s301(2), provides as follows:

\"**(2)** It is the duty of a person arresting another, whether with or without warrant, to give notice, if practicable, of the process or warrant under which he is acting, or of the cause of the arrest.\"

if it is pointless (i.e. they would not be able to hear the cops) to inform or attempt to inform an arrested person of the cause of the arrest, it is not \"practicable\" to do so within the meaning of s301(2) **Johnson v Reeves \[2004\] TASSC 110 (24 September 2004)**

Very hi standard!!!!! Even the threat of violence against police, or a volitaile sitiaution does not warrant the failure of an explanation for not stating the reasons see **Wilkie v Patterson \[2016\] TASMC 6**

\+

### "Found offending"

**Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan, Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan, Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden, David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse Files \[1997\] TASSC 52 (20 May 1997)**

*48. The Act, s34B(3) provides the authority for a police officer to arrest without warrant a person committing certain offences, including the offences of resisting and assaulting a police officer in the execution of his duty. Some suggestion was made during the course of argument that this power of arrest is governed by the provisions of the Act, s55. I do not accept that submission because s34B is not a section in respect of which it is \"specially provided\" that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person found offending against that provision. What is provided for by s34B(3) is that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person \"committing\" an offence.*

*49. In the course of argument, considerable reference was made to Lynch v Hargrave [\[1971\] VicRp 11](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1971/11.html); [\[1971\] VR 99.](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1971%5d%20VR%2099?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) Relevantly, that case concerned the expression \"found offending\", and, in particular, whether, before it could be said that a person was \"found offending\", it needed to be established objectively that that person in fact was offending or whether it was sufficient that the person finding the alleged offender believed on reasonable grounds that the person was offending. Relying on Trebeck v Croudace [\[1918\] 1 KB 158](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1918%5d%201%20KB%20158?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), McInerney J held at 109 - 110:*

*\"Accordingly, I am of the view the power of arrest of a person \'found offending\' is to be regarded as exercisable according to the facts as then known to the member of the police force. Whether or not the person arrested was in fact and in law offending in the relevant time is a matter for subsequent determination of the court.\"*

*50. There is no reason why the same principle should not be applied to the word \"offending\". I do apply it, so that the power conferred by s34B(3) is exercisable whenever a police officer believes, on reasonable grounds, that the relevant person is committing a relevant offence created by s34B. It follows that the lawfulness of the arrests of Leigh Phegan and Hulse is not determined by whether they actually assaulted Lee. The arrests may have been lawful and, accordingly, Rowlands may have been acting in the execution of his duty at relevant times even if the obstruction of Lee was not established.*

*51. Applying Lynch v Hargrave (supra) and the well-established meaning of \"committing\" (see Simmons v Millingen [\[1846\] EngR 275](http://www.worldlii.org/int/cases/EngR/1846/275.html); [(1846) 2 CB 524](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281846%29%202%20CB%20524?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), [135 ER 1051](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=135%20ER%201051?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13); Hanway v Boultbee [\[1830\] EngR 888](http://www.worldlii.org/int/cases/EngR/1830/888.html); [(1830) 1 M & Rob 15](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281830%29%201%20Macl%20%26%20R%2015?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), [174 ER 6](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=174%20ER%206?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13); R v Smith [(1876) 14 SCR (NSW) 419)](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281876%29%2014%20SCR%20%28NSW%29%20419?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) I construe s34B(3) as conferring a power of arrest in those cases:*

*1 where a person is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act;*

*2 where the arresting officer believes on reasonable grounds that a person taken is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act; and*

*3 where a person is taken immediately and in fresh pursuit after being found in either of those circumstances.*

*52. A police officer\'s power of arrest is to be exercised by him or her personally and on the facts known to him or her. The mere direction of a senior officer provides no lawful authority for the making of an arrest by the police officer to whom the direction is given. An arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. That liberty may not be infringed except by lawful authority. The direction of a senior officer does not provide that lawful authority. Any officer who complied with the direction given by Lee, did so at his peril unless there was some other basis which authorised that officer to make the arrest.*

### Custody defined:

Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995

**3.**   **Interpretation**

(1)  In this Act, unless the contrary intention appears --

(2)  For the purposes of this Act, a person is in custody if he or she is --

\(a\) under lawful arrest by warrant; or

\(b\) under lawful arrest under [[section 27 of the ](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2015-10-13/act-1924-069#GS27@EN)[Criminal Code](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2015-10-13/act-1924-069)]{.underline} or a provision of any other Act.

Lawful custody defined in 106 of the CODE

### The duty to give reasons for arrest 

- Except when not practicable see **Johnson v Reeves \[2004\] TASSC 110 for a discussion on this**

When making an arrest a police officer has a duty to inform the person being arrested of the reasons for that arrest. This duty exists at both common law (Christie v Leachinsky \[1947\] AC 573) (which applies to summary offences ((Gow v Davies \[1992\] 1 Tas R 1; Criminal Code Act s 4(3).

))28) and under s 301(2) of the Criminal Code (which applies to indictable offences). This section of the Code provides: (2) It is the duty of a person arresting another, whether with or without warrant, to give notice, if practicable, of the process or warrant under which he is acting or the cause of the arrest.

As can be seen from s 301(2), where it is not 'practicable', there is no duty to inform of the reasons for arrest. Examples of when it might be impracticable to give the reasons for arrest are if the arrestee is blind drunk, if the arrestee is deaf and cannot lip-read or if the arrestee responds with an immediate counterattack or by running away.

Another exception to the rule under the common law is that where the reason for the arrest is obvious, for example if a robber is caught red-handed, it may not be necessary to give the reason for arrest (Christie v Leachinsky \[1947\] AC 573, at 587) . This exception is not mentioned in the Code provision.

Christie v Leachinsky is the principal common law authority explaining this duty. This case discusses the history of the duty, dating it back to at least 1755, and details the two justifications for the duty. The first justification is that being arrested is such a substantial interference with a person's liberty, that it must be justified and that the person being arrested should be informed of that justification. Secondly, informing the arrestee of the reasons for the arrest gives the person the opportunity to offer an explanation to clear themselves and be released as soon as possible.

**The problem defined**

Protections provided by the Act

The rights of citizens who have been arrested are safeguarded by a number of statutory and common law

protections. In Tasmania, the majority of these protections are contained in the Criminal Law (Detention

and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas). They include:

\- The requirement that the arrestee be taken before a magistrate or justice as soon as practicable after

arrest unless released unconditionally or released on police bail: s 4(1);

\- The requirement that the arrestee be informed of the right to silence prior to questioning: s 4(5);

\- The requirement that the arrestee be informed of the right to communicate with a friend or relative

and a legal practitioner prior to questioning: s 6(1);

The requirement (subject to s 6(3) and (6)) that the police defer any questioning and investigation to

enable the arrestee to make or attempt to make the communication: s 6(2);

\- The requirement (subject to s 6(3) and (6)) that the police afford the arrestee reasonable facilities to

make the communication: s 6(7);

\- The provision of an interpreter when needed: s 5;

\- The requirement that the arrestee be questioned only for a reasonable time: s 4(2)(a); reasonableness

in this context is determined by the considerations set out in s 4(4);

\- The requirement that the arrestee be taken before a custody officer without delay and placed in the

custody of the custody officer: s 15(1);

\- The requirement that the custody officer perform the duties in relation to the arrestee imposed by s

15(2) and (4); and

\- The requirement that the custody officer ensure that the arrestee is treated in accordance with the

Act: s 16 (1).

**To whom do the Act's protections apply?**

'In custody' under the Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act

The Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act affords the protections contained in sections 4-6, 15

and 16 to people who are 'in custody'. Section 3(2) provides that a person is 'in custody' for the purposes

of the Act if he or she is:

\(a\) under lawful arrest by warrant; or

\(b\) under lawful arrest under section 27 of the Criminal Code or a provision of any other Act.

Because the application of the Act depends upon whether a person is under lawful arrest, it is critically

important to determine what exactly is meant by the term, 'under lawful arrest'. The term 'arrest' is not

defined in the Act. In practice, it can often be difficult to ascertain a suspect's precise custodial status.

The case law has distinguished three major custodial categories: (a) persons who are "voluntarily

assisting the police with their enquiries", (b) persons who are under arrest and, (c) in Tasmania, since the

decision of the Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal in Sammak2 (followed in Reid and Swan3

) persons

who are in the custody of the police though not under arrest.

**1. Volunteers**

A person who is "voluntarily assisting the police with their inquiries" is not under arrest, either at

common law or under the Act. In fact, such a person is, in the eyes of the law, not detained at all and is at

liberty to leave. Accordingly, it was felt, at the time that the Act was enacted, that volunteers did not need

the protections afforded by sections 4-6 of the Act and, therefore, those protections were not applied to

them.

**2. Under lawful arrest**

In contrast, a person who is under arrest is not free to leave and, where the arrest is lawful, must submit to

the authority of the arrestor. For an arrest to be lawful there must be a power to arrest and an arrest in fact.

Essentially, the latter requires that the police make it clear to the suspect that he or she is no longer free.4

There is no set formula for what must be said and done to effect a lawful arrest. Any form of words may

be used which, in the circumstances of the case, make it clear to the arrestee that he or she is under

compulsion. Where there has been a formal, lawful arrest the protections provided by the Act will clearly

apply to the arrestee. However, at common law, a person may also be found to be under arrest, even if

there has been no formal arrest, if the police behave in such a way as to give him or her reasonable

grounds for believing that he or she is not free to leave.5

It is not clear whether the protections provided

by the Act apply to a person who is under informal arrest in this sense although since the decisions of the

Court of Criminal Appeal in Sammak and of Slicer J in Reid and Swan, there is real doubt that they do.

# Power of entry

**Power to enter premises to effect power of arrest (s26A CODE)**

### Implied licence 

Implied licence to enter driveway or path if no signs to contrary

*Halliday v Nevill*, [\[1984\] HCA 80](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1984/80.html); the majority of the High Court held, and I read from the head note:

\"If the path or driveway leading to the entrance of a suburban dwelling-house is left unobstructed and with entrance gate unlocked and there is no notice or other indication that entry by visitors generally or particularly designated visitors is forbidden or unauthorized, the law will imply a licence in favour of any member of the public to go upon the path or driveway for any legitimate purpose that in itself involves no interference with the occupier\'s possession or injury to the occupier, his guests or his or their property.\"

### Implied licence limited though!

However, in **Tasmania v Lee \[2005\] TASSC 117** it was clarified the It was held that there was an implied licence on the part of the police officer to go onto the driveway or path leading up to the door of the house, and it is implicit from that that there was no power to enter the house itself. To do so without invitation would amount to a trespass.

# Power to seize, seizure 

Potentially a common law power to seize evidence or potential sources of evidence of 3^rd^ parties English case of Ghani v Jones \[1970\] 1 QB 693

Power to seize property from third parties It has become common practice for police to seize mobile phones from bystanders who are recording incidents in which they are not criminally involved. Phones are seized (and sometimes retained for long periods) for the purpose of preserving and downloading the footage for use as evidence. The personal inconvenience to the phone's owner, not to mention the potential interference with their privacy, can be significant.

In Ghani v Jones, Lord Denning held that seizure of property from non-suspects may be justified in some circumstances. However, in order to enliven this power, the person's refusal to hand over the property "must be quite unreasonable". He provided (at pp 1704 and 1705) some hypothetical examples of situations in which a third party would be unreasonable in refusing to allow the police to examine their property. For example, some bank robbers "borrow" a private car and then abandon it by the roadside. Police wish to examine the car for fingerprints; the owner of the car would be unreasonable in refusing to allow them to do so. The seizure of a phone from a bystander was recently considered by Her Honour Magistrate Swain in the NSW Local Court in Police v Ronald Sines; Police v Andrew Love (date of decision 7 July 2017; not published on NSW Caselaw). Mr Love was using his mobile phone to record an altercation between his friend Mr Sines and a police officer. Police asked him to hand over his phone, which he refused to do. Police then tried to seize the phone, and Mr Love threw it on the ground, from where it was retrieved by police. Applying Ghani v Jones, her Honour held (at paras 118-119 of her judgment) that the police officer had the power to seize the phone because: 3 Power to seize property (including from third parties) 61315787 Police powers to search and seize mobile phones page 8

• police had reasonable grounds to believe that the offence of assaulting police had been committed; • assaulting police in the execution of their duty is a serious offence; • "the police had reasonable grounds for believing that the phone contained material which could prove the serious offence alleged", as Mr Love appeared to have filmed the incident; • although Mr Love was not criminally involved in the assault police, "the police had reasonable grounds to believe that Mr Love's refusal was unreasonable"; and • the phone would not be kept for any longer than reasonably necessary, as the police officer told Mr Love that his phone would be returned after any footage was downloaded. Her Honour did not elaborate on why Mr Love's refusal to hand over his phone may have been unreasonable. It is also questionable whether she correctly applied the test set down by Lord Denning. Needless

# Search:

Not resisting or not saying no to a search does not amount to consent. **Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)**

Pre-condition to search powers on reasonable belief under MOD is "lawful" entry to private premise. Must have warrant etc. Private premises also held to include hotel room under occupancy in certain circumstances. **Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)**

# Search warrants:

**Re: Phone** -- Phone falls within *Evidence Act* definition of a "document." Can examine contents, but that likely wouldn't extend to external applications. General distinction is what can be examined while phone is in "flight mode." Not sure about changing passwords, but Police can take steps to prevent destruction of evidence (namely putting the phone in flight mode to prevent remote wiping).

### POLICE POWERS OF SEARCH AND SEIZURE

***Scope of the search warrant (i.e. seizing items not particularised in the warrant). *** 

Police can't use a warrant to go on a fishing exercise: ***Crowley v Murphy*.**

  

However, whether they can lawfully seize items not mentioned in the warrant will depend on whether the conditions from ***Ghani v Jones***, which are now embodied (mostly) in s. 6 of the *Search Warrants Act (tas)*, can be satisfied: 

1.  Must relate to a serious offence; 

<!-- -->

2.  Property must be the fruity of the crime or the means by which it was committed, or material evidence of its commission; and 

<!-- -->

3.  That the person in possession was involved in the commission of the crime either as a principal or accessory.  

In addition: 

4.  Police must keep them for no longer that necessary to complete investigations or to preserve as evidence; and 

<!-- -->

5.  The lawfulness of the police conduct must be judged at the time of the seizure, not by what happens later (hindsight or subsequent events cannot justify the seizure).  

Under s6(e) of the *Search Warrants Act* the executing officer can seize any material found on the premises that they believe on reasonable grounds to be either: 

- Evidential material in relation to the offence which the warrant relates; or 

<!-- -->

- Evidential material in relation to another offence. 

### 3.3 Common law rights

The only time that search and seizure is permitted is when pursuant to arrest; pursuant to a warrant or with consent.

***Coco v R*** (1994) 179 CLR 427 at pp 435-6 per Mason CJ, Brennan, Gaudron and McHugh JJ

***"Every unauthorized entry upon private property is a trespass, the right of a person in possession or entitled to possession of premises to exclude others from those premises being a fundamental common law right."*** (emphasis added) @ 435

Chapters 7 & 8 in Gans et al, ***Criminal Process and Human Rights*** (Federation Press, 2011)

#### When can you search at common law?

***R v Jefferies*** \[1994\] 1 **NZLR** 290 at 300-301

A police constable in an unmarked police car heard over the police radio that a light blue car had left the scene of an armed robbery. It was the escape vehicle, and believed to be heading in the P.C's direction. Presently, along came a light blue vehicle, which the PC stopped. Conversation between the four men and the PC made it clear that they were being stopped in relation to the armed robbery.

Without asking for consent the PC searched the boot of the car and found \$30,000 worth of marijuana. None of the men were involved in the armed robbery.

Was this search lawful under statute? How did the statute interact with the NZ Bill of Rights? What about at CL powers of search?

Section 60 of the Arms Act gave the PC grounds to search on the reasonable suspicion of weapons being on site. There was a procedural responsibility which was not adhered to -- namely identifying himself. Despite this is it was still held to be lawfully effected.

#### Search and seizure: common law and statute

A search is an examination of a person or property and a seizure is a taking of what is discovered. Seizure under the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution also extends to seizure of persons which in New Zealand might more readily \[\*33\] be dealt with under the arbitrary arrest and detention provisions of s 22.

The common law does not recognise a right to search anyone in the absence of an arrest. Nor does it recognise a general right of search on arrest. As an incident to an arrest a police officer may search an accused and take possession of any property tending to show that person\'s guilt (Barnett & Grant v Campbell (1902) 21 NZLR 484). As well as searching for such property the police may search for objects with which a person might do himself or herself or any others injury, or which might be employed to facilitate escape. As Donaldson LJ observed in Lindley v Rutter (1980) 72 Cr App R 1, 6:

\"It is the duty of any constable who lawfully has a prisoner in his charge to take all reasonable measures to ensure that the prisoner does not escape or assist others to do so, does not injure himself or others, does not destroy or dispose of evidence and does not commit further crime such, for example, as malicious damage to property. This list is not exhaustive, but it is sufficient for present purposes. What measures are reasonable in the discharge of this duty will depend upon the likelihood that the particular \[\*34\] prisoner will do any of these things unless prevented. That in turn will involve the constable in considering the known or apparent disposition and sobriety of the prisoner. What can never be justified is the adoption of any particular measures without regard to all the circumstances of the particular case.\"

A personal search is a restraint on freedom and an affront to human dignity. It is exercisable at common law only for good reason. The Courts will examine any claimed justification with great care and generally speaking no one should be required to submit to that restraint unless he or she knows in substance the reason for the search (Brazil v Chief Constable of Surrey \[1983\] 1 WLR 1155, 1162).

Special statutes authorise personal including body searches in particular cases and s 57A of the Police Act 1958 adds to the common law right of search on arrest. It allows for the search and seizure of all money and all or any other property found on or in the possession of a person taken into lawful custody and at a police station or in any other premises or vehicle used for police purposes.

The common law did not allow any entry and search of private property by officers of the \[\*35\] state without permission of the owner or occupier except under a warrant issued by a justice acting on evidence that there was strong cause to believe that stolen goods were concealed there.

#### When can you search at common law?

A District Court Judge, justice or Registrar may issue a search warrant if satisfied that there is reasonable ground for believing that there is present there:

\(a\) Any thing upon or in respect of which any offence punishable by imprisonment has been or is suspected of having been committed; or

\(b\) Any thing which there is reasonable ground to believe will be evidence as to the commission of any such of-fence; or

\(c\) Any thing which there is reasonable ground to believe is intended to be used for the purpose of committing any such offence . . .

Under other specific statutes conditions under which warrants may be issued or warrantless searches undertaken are expressed in quite different ways and there is no single test or criterion running through the legislation.

#### When is a search \"unreasonable\"

A search will be unreasonable if the circumstances giving rise to the search make the search itself unreasonable or if an otherwise initially reasonable search is carried out in an unreasonable manner.

Section 21 is a negative provision in that it is a restraint on governmental action and confers no powers on the government. In particular it does not em-power a \"reasonable\" search.

The test of unreasonableness requires consideration of the values underlying the right and a balancing of the relevant values and public interests involved. As apparent from Entick v Carrington, where the action was for trespass to property, the common law concern was for the protections of property rights: entry on to private property was a trespass and the seizure of property was a conversion. However both in America and Canada privacy values have become an accepted basis for the constitutional protection.

### 3.4 Grounds of the Search: Reasonable Grounds to Suspect or Believe

#### What is reasonable suspicion?

***Lindley v Rutter*** \[1981\] QB 128

A drunken woman was taken to a police station where her bra was taken off. She was arrested -- resisted search -- two officers searched her believing they were under a standing order to search every female prisoner and remove the bra for their own protection.

There is a duty of an officer to have a lawful purpose for search -- a standing order from a senior officer is not enough. There has to be a basis.

Held: If they officer's mind did not apply itself to having a lawful purpose for the search, than the search was unlawful (subjective mindset of officer required level of analysis). There was no legal basis to search her -- standing order not sufficient.

***George v Rockett*** (1990) 170 CLR 104

- *Reasonable suspicion is: the existence of facts which are sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person.' @ 112*

- *Suspicion is 'a state of conjecture or surmise where proof is lacking' @ 115*

- *'The facts which can reasonably ground a suspicion may be quite insufficient reasonably to ground a belief, yet some factual basis for the suspicion must be shown.' @ 115. (George v Rockett) -- so suspicion is less than a belief.*

See 111 of judgement. Reference and description of the search is the basis of est the boundaries the search powers. There must be specificity in the warrant -- the type of evidence soiught must be detailed

***\*Nguyen v Elliott*** (unreported, Supreme Court, Victoria, Hedigan J, No 7540 of 1994, 6 February 1995)

(Note Tasmanian has a higher standard of reasonable belief than this Vic example)

The act of a suspect kicking out and contacting a police officer , is not out of proportion to the threat of being searched against ones will, and that any bruising which may follow is justified in the circumstances where the it is an unwarranted search.

The subsequent charge for actions following an arrest on this basis is not grounds upon which the def can be held accountable. One cannot be guilty of resiting officer in course of duty when an unwarranted search is clearly not in the course of an officer's duty.

F. Police watching group of Asian youths in park (were known to have been involved in other offences). When they saw the police they behaved suspiciously (i.e. looking around etc). Eventually approached one of the boys and said they wanted to search. He objected. Police persisted and eventually he kicked a police officer (bit one during scuffle). Continued to resist and was then handcuffed and charged him with numerous offences relating to the resistance. No drugs found on him.

H. Accused defended the charges on the grounds that the police were not in the execution of their duty. They had no reasonable basis for the search. TJ and appeal judge agreed. Held that the police had no right to search the young men as there was no reasonable basis for the belief that the youths were in possession of drugs. Accused could thus not be prosecuted for the offences.

But, the only reason the Court of Criminal Appeal upheld the decision was that they said the TJ had a better basis to judge the demeanour of the witnesses. Clear they were reluctant to do this. The whole tenor of the decision is that they wanted to agree with the police.

\****MacLeod*** (1991) 61 A Crim R 465

Issuing officer must be satisfied on info given to them under oath that there is reasonable grounds - subjective standard of the actual officer. Not enough that someone else is satisfied that there is such a suspicion.

**Held**. ***George v Rockett*** was applied by Slicer J here. See para 8-9 for a summary of the law in this area:

• it is the state of mind of the issuing officer that is relevant.

• issuing officer must satisfy himself or herself that the necessary criteria are present

• issuing officer cannot rely merely on the suspicion of the informant

• need for particularity in relation to object and the offence; mere reference to the relevant section is not enough.

***Beneficial Finance Corp v AFP (1991) 31 FCR 523***

H. The issuing officer doesn't have an obligation to go behind the warrant and investigate further (even if incomplete). However, there is an obligation to clear up ambiguities or make enquiries if they have concerns. Also see Jackson v Mijovich (1991) 23 ALD 470.

**Sniffer Dogs: The Right to Security of the Person Chapter 7 in Gans et al, *Criminal Process and Human Rights* (Federation Press, 2011) pp 268 -- 270; 273 - 275**

In Qld, NT and SA, legislation expressly provides that an indication by a drug detection dog of the presence of a drug provides police with a reasonable ground for a search (@ 268)

- Tas doesn't have legislation on this

- Interesting statistics: some dogs have a 90-92% accuracy rate (Chevy, from Canada), whilst others, such as NSW dogs have a reliability rate of 26%.

- However, some behaviours + sniffer dogs will undoubtedly constitute a reasonable ground for a search: consider a man, who upon seeing police with a sniffer dog starts muttering 'fuck fuck fuck' to himself. Reasonable suspicion? (@ 275)

### 3.5 Statutory Powers of search -- premises and person

This section includes powers of search in non-arrest situations. Be careful with the distinction with the standards required: difference between suspicion and belief.

***Crimes Act 1914*** (Cth) ss 3ZE -- 3ZI

***Police Offences Act*** 1935 (Tas), ss 57, 58, 58A, 58B

Poison

Liquor

Accused persons in custody (incidental to arrest)

***Search Warrants Act*** 1997 (Tas) s 6(2)(a)

***Family Violence Act*** 2004 (Tas), s 10(3)

Where family violence in affect, search powers exist.

***Poisons Act 1971*** (Tas), ss 90A(1)(b), 90B & 90C

Premises; vehicle and personal searches conducted in the process of these two searches.

***Police Powers (Public Safety) Act 2005***, ss 18, 19, 22, 23

***Misuse of Drugs Act*** 2001 (Tas), ss 29, 30 (Reasonable belief).

### 3.6 Personal Search

***Lindley v Rutter*** \[1981\] QB 128

Standing orders to search any female person arrested and placed in a cell, and to remove their brassiere.

Was the PC acting in execution of her duty when she searched the defendant, and removed the defendant's brassiere?

Because she formed no state of mind -- no purpose in performing the search, it was unlawful

"So far as searches are concerned, he should appreciate that they involve an affront to the dignity and privacy of the individual. Furthermore, there are degrees of affront involved in such a search. Clearly going through someone's pockets or handbag is less of an affron than a body search. In every case a police officer ordering a search or depriving a prisoner of property should have a very good reason for doing so." Donaldson LJ @ 135

***\*Nguyen v Elliott*** (unreported, Supreme Court, Victoria, Hedigan J, No 7540 of 1994, 6 February 1995)

Two police officers -- one who was 'merely curious' as to whether an Asian youth had drugs on him, rather than having the reasonable suspicion necessary to exercise his statutory power

Unlawful search meant that resistance was permitted, and subsequent arrest was unlawful and resistance was permitted

### 3.7 Search of Premises

***Entick v Carrington*** (1765) 2 Wilson KB 275; (1765) 19 St.T 129

*"The poorest man may in his cottage bid Defiance to all the forces of the crown. It May be frail -- its roof may shake -- the Wind may blow through it -- the storm may Enter -- the rain may enter -- but the king of England cannot enter -- all his force Dares not cross the threshold of the Ruined tenement. So be it -- unless he has Justification by law."*

There is a conflict of authority between the UK and Australia

***Motherland***

***Dillon v O'Brien & Davies*** (1887) 16 Cox CC 245 -- warrant for arrest

Can search property where person is arrested:

"\... it is clear, and beyond doubt, that, at least in cases of treason and felony, constables \... are entitled, upon a lawful arrest by them of one charged \... to take and detain property found in his possession which will form material evidence in his prosecution for that crime\...' @ 249

Applied to misdemeanours as well

[***\*Ghani v Jones*** \[1970\] 1 QB 693]{.mark}

This case represents the 'high water mark'

**Held**. Per Lord Denning: it is settled law that where the police enter premises pursuant to search warrant or pursuant to power of arrest in relation to serious offence; they are entitled to search for and take any goods in relation to that offence. If during the search they find other goods relating to other events they are entitled to seize them as well provided they behave reasonably and detain the goods for no longer than is necessary.

Fivefold test of Lord Denning MR:

***Can seize item when legally on the premises, without the consent of the owner if:***

1\. Reasonable grounds for believing serious offence has been committed

2\. Reasonable grounds for believing the article in question is either the fruit of the crime, the instrument by which the crime is committed, or is material evidence

- Great Train Robbery -- a saucer of milk given to a cat on a farm where the robbers hid

3\. Reasonable grounds to believe that the person in possession of it has himself committed the crime, or is implicated, or is accessory or at any rate his refusal must be quite unreasonable

4\. Must not keep the article nor prevents its removal for any longer than is reasonably necessary to complete their investigations or to preserve it for evidence. If a copy will suffice, a copy.

5\. The lawfulness of the conduct of the police will be judged at the time and not by what happens afterwards

***\*Jeffrey v Black*** \[1977\] 3 WLR 895

Sandwich case: Police officers may not search premises of an arrestee without either a warrant or the consent of the accused, at least for evidence of matters unrelated to the reason for the previous arrest of the accused. There must be a connection with the evidence gathered/ search and the initial charge.

If related the reason for the previous arrest -- tickety boo.

So if search granted, they can search for and take things relating to that, but are not authorised to search for evidence which is unrelated to the offence.

Silent is not consent either.

Case also stands for the principle that whee there is a serious crime; the suspect can have their premises searched -- not for misdemeanours.

They ignored the right of the sandwich thief to refuse entry.

***R (on the application of Rottman) v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis*** \[2002\] 2 All ER 865; \[2002\] 2 WLR 1315 - arrest warrant for extra-jurisdictional offence.

Applies to extradition arrest warrants.

Dissent very interesting

Procedurally sound to search premises where individual arrested. This means that if they were arrested on site, it does not make sense that another search warrant is needed.

Factually: includes building on premises even where person arrested outside building structure -- another extension: extending Dillon by not being in the immediate vicinity of the arrest

#### Australia

***\*Challenge Plastics Pty Ltd v Collector of Customs*** (1993) 115 ALR 149

***Ghani*** and Lord Denning went too far.Common law powers of search are restricted to: Dillon v O'Brien - being in the possession of a person at the time of his arrest either for a felony or a misdemeanour

[***\*Laurens v Willers*** (2002) 131 A Crim R 281 per Heenan J at \[44\]]{.mark}

Seizure under a search warrant

Basis of common law rights with search: action of trespass based in exclusive possession rights

Here: applicants were in a caravan park to which they had no exclusive possession

Moreover, second common law power of P.O: to seize stolen goods if without violence or harm to the goods of the applicant

- Line of authority away from ***Ghani*** as a principle in Aus.

***\*Tasmania v Woodberry*** \[2012\] TASSC 89

Serious offence where woman stole \$30,000 from business she worked for. Visited by 2 police officers. Not cautioned or warned that she could speak to a lawyer so there was an arrest.

She was taken from her place of business and was told that she would need to accompany them to the house -- she claimed that she was compelled to comply with the search.

The court noted that there was no power of the police to compel her to submit to a search of her house -- in so doing, the police officer ***disregarded her right to refuse entry***. All evidence of the search was excluded. There were other means available to the officers to initiate the search such as consent or getting a search warrant.

***\*[Spyke & Alexander]{.mark}*** Tas Unreported Serial No 2 of 1981 BC8171022

Forged checks on typewriter.

Applied Ghani and also in s5 and s6 of Tas Search Warrant Acts (the aspect of allowing police to sieze items of other offences when entering pursuant to a search warrant under that Act) -- not a great deal of reason why they are applying Ghani (cf. ***Challenge Plastics*** which considered the law carefully)

**\**

### 3.8 Search Warrants

#### Search Warrants

**Common Law**

Purpose of a search warrant: Search warrant operates as a control design --imposes an independent judicial officer between official and citizen. That's why at CL you see the initial conservative approach to issue search warrant (and the jealously the CL protects rights of occupiers)

At CL: need to ensure that goods seized during the search were covered by the search warrant (if not, could be sued for trespass to private property)

CL rejected the idea of general warrants (i.e. unspecified warrants to search anytime/anywhere) --because they had been used by the Star Chamber in a very oppressive way.

***George v Rockett*** (1990) 170 CLR 104

HC explaining the purpose of a search warrant

**Statutory extensions**

Have expanded to any offence at all (s5 SW Act TAS), not just re: stolen property.

***Search Warrants Act 1997*** (Tas)

***Crimes Act 1914*** (Cth), s 3E

***Police Offences Act*** 1935 (Tas), s 60

General warrant provision, but confined to searches for stolen property (issued to all PO by the commissioner of police, not issued by an independent judicial officer). They are issued for 6months (and allow police to enter any premises where they have a reasonable believe there may be stolen property).

\*Not HR's compliant (as no safeguard of judicial oversight)

General warrant, couple with Spyke gives PO a pretty free hand at searching shit in Tas. All they need is reasonable belief

#### 3.8.1 Granting a warrant

***George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104***

F: PO had given an application for a warrant to a Qld Magistrate. The application just stated that reasonable grounds for believe that there were documents on premises re: ex-premier.

HC held: this was completely legit. Just need sufficient evidence placed before the magistrate or JP so that they themselves have a RB/Suspicion that the items were on the property.

Purpose of a search warrant: Search warrant operates as a control design --imposes an independent judicial officer between official and citizen. That's why at CL you see the initial conservative approach to issue search warrant (and the jealously the CL protects rights of occupiers)

This gives an indication of what a JO should do when decided whether to issue a warrant.

HC said warrants can only work effectively if the JO brings an independent mind to the application

***\*MacLeod (1991) 61 A Crim R 465***

Applied George v Rockett (Slicer J)

What is required for a valid warrant: not the mind of the applicant, but the mind of the issuing authority. Justice cannot rely on the suspicion/believe of the applicant, must form it themselves.

Applications are usually made on oath, or by affidavit, and must put before the authority sufficient evidence to enable them to form a reasonable believe/suspicion

This case is an example where warrant had no technical defect, however court could not be satisfied on the evidence it had (JPs notebook), that the JP actually did have sufficient evidence before him to form reasonable suspicion/belief.

#### 3.8.2 Disclosure -- what is required of the party seeking a warrant?

There is conflicting authority as to the duty of disclosure on the part of the applicant for the search warrant***. Karina Fisheries v Mitson*** (1990) 26 FCR 473 held that all matters must be disclosed that would affect the issuing of the warrant. However, in ***Lego Australia v Paraggio*** 25/8/94 it was held that only where there has been fraud or misrepresentation will a warrant be struck out for non-disclosure. Most prosecutors believe though that everything relevant should be disclosed in the interests of best practice and conservativeness.

***Karina Fisheries v Mitson*** (1990) 26 FCR 473

Full disclosure by the applicant is required that might effect the making of a decision

**This case focuses on greater privacy**

*"There is a duty of full disclosure of all material facts by the informant seeking a search warrant. This duty stems from the common law tradition of protecting the privacy of individuals against the arbitrary use of the power of entry and search\...." @ 473*

(emphasis added)

***Lego Australia v Paraggio*** (1994) 52 FCR 542

Only fraud or misrepresentation will invalidate a warrant (sooo mixed authority on this point)

This case spec rejects Katrina above --a warrant can be set aside because of fraud and an informant must act in good faith in making an application. The statemnets made must be sufficient to est statute -- the critical q. is not whether the ...

This case is more popular.

There is no general duty of disclosure in making an application for the grant of a search warrant' @ 542

Instead, the Court asked whether the material in the information was sufficient on its face for the purposes of s 10(1) of the Crimes Act?

Contrast approach Karina

Contrast facts ***George v Rockett***

#### 3.8.3 Wording of a Warrant

***NSW v Corbett*** (2007) 230 CLR 606

Sooo....don't need to be technical, but people need to know what the PO are searching for, and that they do have the power to search (which they did in that case)

Degree of precision required in identifying the offence

F. Warrant issued to search for firearms. The particular provision relied upon in the warrant had been repealed though. At first instance it was held that this misstatement of the offence invalidated the warrant. Upheld by the Court of Appeal (NSW) then appealed to HCA.

H. Appeal allowed. Statement of offence in search warrants does not have to have the precision that is required in an indictment. Only require a statement of the nature of the offence so that you can clearly see what the search is for and establish the boundaries of the search.

Here the warrant was clear in stating the nature of the offence and all other details. Repealed provision had been replaced by a very similar provision so you couldn't say that this statement could possibly have given rise to any misapprehension of the police power to search for firearms. So here the similarity of the provisions was crucial.

Faield suciiced of a police officer -- note said police will die -- taken as a threat and a warrant was issued.

The warrant was granted under repeal;ed legislation (valid when made but law reprealed before the warrant executed -- howver, new law had similar wording -- warrant was still ok because there was no error in the application or the process by which it was granted.

Nature of offernce and the object of the se3arch were both described appropriately in the warrant, so it was substantially OK.

***Winston Dean v Graeme Ross White*** \[1996\] TASSC 22

Also relevant for disclosure requirements in addition to wording.

Court found lack of specificity in the premises to be searched -- too broad in its description. The warrant was invalid because it was not limited to one premises and not certain as to which premises answered that description in the warrant,

#### 3.8.4 What is the extent of power granted under a warrant?

***Challenge Plastics v Collector of Customs*** (1993) 115 ALR 149

Read this case:

Concerned customs act warrant s 214.

The requirement of a warrant is prac safeguard against interference of individual rights. Officer must swear under oath to an independent judicial officer before such interference will be granted

s 6(1)(e) ***Search Warrants Act 1997*** (Tas)

Broad power - Allows for non-specified things to be collected and seized in a search.

#### 3.8.5 Execution of a warrant

***Baker v Campbell*** (1983) 153 CLR 52

Re. Legal pro privileged not restricted to judicial proceedings.

App were a firm of lawyers -- warrant said enter anytime and take any docs relating to 5 businesses. The terms of the warrant must be strictly adhered to. Authorised only to do no more than absolutely necessary to satisfy the warrant.

Search warrants and their application to documents protected by legal professional privilege. F. D (police) attempted to seize documents protected by privilege.

H. HCA held that the search warrant did not cover the documents as they were covered by LPP. Legal professional privilege is not simply a rule of evidence; rather it is a fundamental common law human right. So the HCA treat LPP in a very important fashion. Search warrants thus cannot apply to documents covered by LPP. This causes some significant problems in practice.

***Crowley v Murphy*** (1981) 34 ALR 496

Warrant to seize implies warrant to search (phrasing of particular legislative provision)

Lockhart J:

1\. good faith;

2\. power exercised for the purposes for which they're conferred; not ulterior

purpose;

3\. fairly in regard to the circumstances;

4\. exercised having regard to the persons affected by warrant's exercise, including

their rights

5\. officer must strictly follow the directions of the warrant and not exceed the limit of its authority (@ 521)

H. Police cannot go on a **'fishing exercise'** looking for documents when they don't really know what they are looking for.

***Oke v Commr Australian Federal Police*** (2007) 168 A Crim R 503

***\***
AFP searching a house and the issue was whether they gave an incomplete copy of the search warrant. There were parts missing -- name of the officer, guidleines as to the treatment of claims for legal pro privilege. Did not satisfy statutory requirements so the execution was invalid.

2 princples:

*When handing a warrant over it must be sufficient to satisdy the searchee so that they can acertain its authenticity -- what was supplied was only a draft one -- could not identify the date or anytone, or anything on oits surface to indicate that it was legal.*

Demonstrates the difficulties with the above process.

F. Oke contacted solicitor about police going through documents (including client/solicitor information). Solicitor then spent the rest of the day trying to negotiate with police to not look at documents he alleged were covered by privilege. Persuaded the police to desist in search and entered into written agreement. This solicitor really stood his ground. AFP hadn't exercised the warrant properly and tried to get out of the whole thing by returning the documents. He refused so they dumped them on his doorstep.

H. The warrant was invalid.

***Larsson v Commr of Police for NSW*** (1988) 16 NSWLR 173

Denial of pltf right to enter own basement. Lrg number of tems found in the basement some possibly related to drug use -- others that were not.

They prevented im form going down and locked it to sto\[p him. But they had no authorioty to do so. Warrqant doesn't permit the deprivation of ones home

s 9(2) ***Search Warrants Act 1997*** (Tas).

Does permit temporary cessation and return to searching under a warrant (but only allows one hour-so basically a lunch break), unless they have consent of the occupier

**Search and seizure (Search Warrant Question Template)**

**Valid entry to premises for purpose of search and Seizure**

Common law protection of property is extensive. Fundamental right to private property -- and to exclude others from it -- see for example HCA in *Coco v R*.

According to *Kuru v NSW*, the police have no special powers to gain lawful entry to premises beyond authorisation in the common law or statute. To lawfully enter premises they will require either:

- Consent; or

- A licence or lawful authorisation.

Further, the common law authorises the police to enter premises;

- to prevent a breach of the peace; or

- Make a lawful arrest and then enter and seize items in the possession of the arrestee.

To otherwise gain lawful entry; the police require a ***search warrant***.

**Search warrants**

**Application**

An application for a search warrant should be made in writing by the applicant (but this is not required by the *Search Warrants Act* in Tasmania). It must then be considered by a judicial officer (usually Justice of the Peace or Magistrate).

The general provision most commonly relied upon for an application for a search warrant is contained in s5(1) of the Search Warrants Act:

**5. When search warrants can be issued**

**(1)** An issuing officer may issue a warrant to search premises if the officer is satisfied by information on oath that ***there are reasonable grounds for suspecting that there is, or there will be within the next 72 hours, any evidential material at the premises***.

But, the *Search Warrants* *Act* does not limit or exclude the operation of any other law purporting to grant powers of search, seizure, arrest or detention (s4).

***Duties of issuing officer***

- A warrant can only be issued if the conditions in s5 (above) are satisfied; or the conditions imposed in any other enabling legislation being relied upon.

- Usually require ***reasonable grounds for suspecting*** that the property will be on the premises.

- The nature of the search warrant process is to ***interpose a judicial officer*** between the executive and citizens to act as a control on excessive executive action; the judicial officer acts as a 'fundamental safeguard': *George v Rocket*.

- To make this process effective; the judicial officer is therefore required to ***bring an independent mind to bear on the process***: *George v Rocket*.

- The ***judicial officer*** must therefore ***be satisfied*** that there are ***grounds for issuing the warrant***:

- *MacLeod* and *George v Rocket* confirm that this requires :

  - there be ***objectively reasonable grounds*** for the belief.

  - I.e. was the information ***sufficient to induce the requisite suspicion*** in a ***reasonable person***?

- A suspicion is more than a mere idle wondering (*George v Rocket)*.

- It is clear that the judicial officer cannot simply 'rubber stamp' the application by simply relying on the suspicions of the applicant: *MacLeod*.

- So importantly, it is the ***state of mind of the issuing officer*** which will be the Court's focus: *MacLeod*.

- However, judicial officer is not required to go 'behind the application' to investigate further or seek more information unless there are clear ambiguities or other questions raised by the application: *Beneficial Finance Corp v AFP* and *Jackson v Mijovich*.

***Duties of applicant -- extent of disclosure required***

- Competing authority on this point; argue both ways.

- Compare *Karina Fisheries* and *Lego Australia*.

- The applicant for a warrant should disclose all matters that would affect the issuing of the warrant otherwise it may be invalid: *Karina Fisheries.*

- The search warrant will only be invalid for non-disclosure where there has been some fraud, half-truth or misrepresentation on the part of the applicant: *Lego Australia.*

- But practically, it is good practice to disclose all information.

***Particularity in warrant***

There is a need for sufficient particularity in the application; state the place, name of applicant etc. Also need to describe the nature of the offence so that it can be established what the search is for and to establish boundaries for the search. Some minor errors will be fine though provided the nature of the offence is clear: *NSW v Corbet*.

Come to preliminary conclusion on whether warrant would be struck out as a result of the application.

**Execution of the warrant**

***Has a proper Announcement been made?***

The requirements to make a 'proper announcement' as contained in the arrest cases of *Lippl* and *O'Neil* have been embodied in s19 of the *Search Warrants Act*. As a result;

(1) The police must announce that they are authorised to enter the premises, and give opportunity for person to allow entry.

However, not required to give such an announcement if the police believe on reasonable grounds (see cases in relation to arrest *Rockett*) that immediate entry is required to ensure the safety of any person or the effective execution of the warrant (for instance if they believe evidence is about to be destroyed).

Also note, s8 of the *Search Warrants Act*; requirement to show particulars of the warrant to the occupier of the premises (if present).

***Scope of the search warrant (i.e. seizing items not particularised in the warrant).***

It is clear that the police cannot use a warrant to go on a fishing exercise: *Crowley v Murphy*.

However, whether they can lawfully seize items not mentioned in the warrant will depend on whether the conditions from *Ghani v Jones*, which are now embodied (mostly) in s6 of the *Search Warrants Act*, can be satisfied:

(1) Must relate to a serious offence;

(2) Property must be the fruity of the crime or the means by which it was committed, or material evidence of its commission; and

(3) That the person in possession was involved in the commission of the crime either as a principal or accessory.

In addition:

(4) Police must keep them for no longer that necessary to complete investigations or to preserve as evidence; and

(5) The lawfulness of the police conduct must be judged at the time of the seizure, not by what happens later (hindsight or subsequent events cannot justify the seizure).

Under s6(e) of the *Search Warrants Act* the executing officer can seize any material found on the premises that they believe on reasonable grounds to be either:

- Evidential material in relation to the offence which the warrant relates; or

- Evidential material in relation to another offence.

***Application to legal professional privilege***

Legal professional privilege is a fundamental common law right; therefore search warrants to not apply to documents covered by LPP (see *Baker v Campbell* and s22 *Search Warrants Act*). To override this; must be made ***very clear*** by legislation.

NB practical approach AFP and Law Reform Commission developed; documents bundled up and given to third party to determine if they are, in fact, privileged. Person alleging privileged has four days to establish privileged status. But see *Oke v Commr. AFP* for practical illustration of the difficulties with this process.

**Conclusion**

If warrant is invalid, then the entry is unlawful and all evidence obtained will be at the judge's discretion to exclude it on the basis it was improperly or illegally obtained evidence (s138 of *Evidence Act*).

CCP Part Three: POLICE POWERS OF INVESTIGATION

**[INTERROGATION]{.underline}**

**Everyone as a basic right not to assist the State in investigation of an offence against them**

**1. Right to Silence**

Art 14(3)(g) ICCPR

- right of a person not to be compelled to testify himself or herself or to confess guilt

- [One rationale:]{.underline} it acts as a curb upon the excesses of law enforcement agencies

- Thought to derive in CL from the excesses of the Star Chamber in UK which had the power to 'press' (torture) confessions out of people

- Linked to presumption of innocence and onus of proof BRD on the Crown

- [Scope:]{.underline} Considered in R v Director of Serious Fraud Office, ex parte Smith \[1993\] AC 1 at 30 by Lord Mustal: Right to silence does not cover a single right, rather it consists of a 'bundle of rights' which have a number of different historical sources and motivations:

- 1\. General immunity of everyone to be free from being compelled to answer questions on pain of punishment

- 2\. General immunity of everyone from being compelled to answer question on pain of punishment *that may incriminate them*.

- 3\. Specific immunity possessed by suspects --when being interviewed by law enforcement officials to not answer questions

- 4\. General immunity from being questioned at all when charged with a criminal offence (i.e. once charged, agents should not question them further at all)

- 5\. Trial immunity: from being compelled to answer on pain of punishment at trial

- 6\. Trial immunity for accused: from having adverse inferences drawn against them from refusing to answer questions on trial

- Based on the notion that we all have a right to tell other people to 'mind their own business'

- These 6 instances of Right to Silence are very rigid: only allow for circumstances of oral evidence, thus may not extend to other types of evidence (such as ID parade, photos etc)

> [Other immunities derived from people subject to investigation:]{.underline}

- Derive from CL dislike of the behavior of the Star Chamber (and 'pressing' confessions)

- Adherence to the idea that any evidence obtained by pressure is inherently unreliable

- Expanded to include notions of fair play and a fair trial

- And also desire to minimize risk of unfair trial

[Ground for arguing for the extension of the right to silence in non-oral areas:]{.underline}

*(Collins)*

F: PO expelled drugs from suspects mouth by squeezing his throat.

H: Canadian Court adopted an expanded view of the right to silence --said it infringed right to silence to do this.

*(R v Stillman)*

F: PO forcibly taken an imprint of suspects teeth

H: Conduct was held to infringe the Right to Silence

*(Jalloh v Germany)*

F:

H: Infrigement to force the suspect to swallow a pill to trigger vomiting to prove they have swallowed drugs

**[Right to Silence at Common Law (Rule 1 of the Judges Rule)]{.underline}**

Police have the power to question anyone (whether suspected of committing an offence or not) in relation to an offence. However, anyone who is questioned has the right not to answer

- As this is embodied in Judges Rules, it does not have the force of law. However it is a 'yard stick' by which to judge conduct. Thus any evidence obtained in breach of the Judges Rules can be challenged under s138 or s90 of the UEL

*(Rice v Connelly)*

F: A seen lurking suspiciously in area where many burglary's had occurred. He refused to tell them name, what he was doing etc. They asked him to accompany his to a Station, he refused. Then they arrested him on willful obstruction.

H: not an offence for a person to refuse to cooperate with police in their investigations (i.e. not giving name and address) --does not constitute the offence of obstruction. People probably have a moral or social duty to assist police, but no legal duty.

[Statutory Abrogations of the Right to Silence]{.underline}

*\*Need to read the provisions carefully to work out what their scope is (and what they allow the PO to compel a person to provide --i.e. an offence not to provide information in some circumstances). Usually, it is very narrow, and can only be compelled in narrow circumstances*

*All of the provisions below give police the power to demand name and address (sometimes with extra bits, or in different circumstances)*

*Crimes Act* *1914* (Cth), ss 3UC & 3V

In Cth place, give reason for being there, and proof of ID

*Police Offences Act 1935* (Tas), s 55A (only allows police to compel name and address when PO reasonably suspected of committing an offence under the POA. If refuse or give false, they are obstructing course of judges)

*Misuse of Drugs Act 2001* (Tas), s 31

*Road Safety (Alcohol & Drugs) Act 1970* (Tas), s15(1)

*Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979* (Cth), s 34L Unlimited provision. Requires people to provide info to proscribed authorities when answering question, pursuant to a questioning warrant. Don't need to be a suspect, and it is an offence not to supply info (max sentence, 5 years)

Some provisions allow police to demand other information/instructions:

*Road Safety (Alcohol & Drugs) Act 1979* (Tas), ss 7A & 8 (submit to a breath test)

*Firearms Act 1996* (Tas), s 134 (if found with a firearm in a public place, they can compel you to hand it over so they can inspect it)

*Misuse of Drugs Act 2001* (Tas), s 31(2) Self incrimination provision: if they find any drugs on your premises, they can demand you supply to them the name of your supplier (and an offence not to do so)

**\**

**\`. Detention for Interrogation**

*Williams* (1986) 161 CLR 278: no authorization of arrest for the purpose of interrogation. This has been problematic for police, and therefore each jurisdiction has enabled legislation which ensure that although not lawful at CL detention post arrest may be lawful under the *Detention and Interrogation Act Tas or Crimes Act (Cth)*.

Following this, each Australian jurisdiction enabled PO to detain lawfully arrested persons for a reasonable time for questioning

Tas provision is the ***Criminal Law (Detention & Interrogation) Act* *1995* (Tas), ss 4(2) & (9).** Gives noime limit, only a reasonable time (guidance as to what this is for relevant case)

***Crimes Act 1914* (Cth), ss 23C, CA, D & DA**

Sets time limit (2 hrs for Children and A &TS, 4 hrs for everyone else). But can only detain for a reasonable period of time, with 4 hours being the max.

*Reasonable time*: judged according to the facts of the case and the guidance of the legislation.

E.g. how long taken to transport the arrestee to police HQs, ID Parade time allowance, allowance to deal with intoxication of the suspect, for participations in forensic procedures.

Once the reasonable time has expired, the obligation to release on police bail, or bring before a justice re emerges.

If suspect is obtained beyond the reasonable time, the detention will be unlawful --thus any evidence obtained during unlawful detention will be deemed invalid (*Williams*) will then apply.

Interrogation to be valid:

1.  Need a properly grounded, lawful arrest

**[2. PROTECTIONS FOR DETAINEES:]{.underline}**

- Judges Rules

- Right to be cautioned (also now has statutory recognition --s139 UEL)

- Communication Rights (with a friend/relative AND a lawyer) s6 Tas, s23G Cth (see *Challander)*

- Interpreter

- Video-recording

- Crimes Act (Cth) protection for minors, Indigenous and TS Islanders.

**Caution**

*CARR* tells us that a right to caution does not exist as such, at CL. However the administrative guidelines of the judges rules require police to do so.

**Right to Communicate**

*R v Challender*

F: Suspect had requested to talk to relative and lawyer and was refused.

H: Info provided after the arrest was prohibited by the act.

*Crimes Act* *1914* (Cth), s 23G -- P

Friend or lawyer may be present in the interview (s23G) --but no equivalent allowance in Tas CL D&I Act

Interpreter

S23N (Cth)

Must not interview a A or TS I without an 'interview friend' present, and must allow suspect time to talk in private with the friend (caveat is something H....if police reasonably of the view that the persons level of education and understanding would mean that they would not be disadvantaged to be interviewed without them)

**Video-Recording**

Any admission of a serious offence must be recorded to be admissible

S85A --considered in *R v Kelly*

Issue: whether the police should have video statements made by the A (after he made off the cuff statements outside the interview room).

Were those statements made to the police during 'official questioning'

HC: (still applies in Tas) It is the period of time when the questioning commences and ceases. Relatively narrow concept in the sense that it only applies to confessions or admissions made during official questions, not to admissions made otherwise. Relatively broad as the requirement to record applies even if not in custody or under arrest. In this case, the questioning ceased when the Sgt said 'we will conclude the interview' and turned off the recording device.

Kirby J dissent: this interpretive approach enabled the police to undermine the purpose of the legislation

CF. *Carr v WA*

Considering the term 'interview'

H: any conversation between a member of the PO and a suspect, was covered by the term 'interview' when in relation to an offence. It didn't matter if the conversation was informal, or initiated by the suspect.

Said the legislation tries to prevent 'verballing' by the police, and this warranted a broad interpretation of 'interview'

*R v Challender*

F: A arrested early in morning, and interviewed late afternoon. Before interview, PO took her outside for smoke and a chat, and told her the types of things they would ask her in the interview. The A made a number of comments about the offences being investigated, and later on tape, the PO asked her about the things she had said earlier, she admitted making the comments

H: The early chat did not constitute 'official questioning' due to *Kelly* thus could not exclude the admissions on the basis they had not been recorded. But, applied s90 of the UEL instead, and said what the PO had done was unfair to the A (as the A may have thought that what she was saying was 'off the record'), and she may not have been aware of her right to silence, and not in a good position to decide whether to exercise the right to silence. Further, said the whole point of the chat was to try and catch the A unaware, and thus undermined her rights.

S85A UEL Exceptions (s85A b-d)

Considered in *R v Browne*

F: PO had not recorded the admissions of the A, but argued they had a reasonable explanation for this as it was not practicable to do so (as at the time the admissions were made, they didn't have a video recorded with them). PO were at A's house for the purpose of detaining him

H: CJ --that's not a reasonable excuse, you should be carrying recording equipment with you because there is a strong chance they will say something.

PO also tried to argue that 'exceptional circumstances' should apply on basis that the making of the admissions was not in dispute, and therefore should be accepted.

CJ: that is not an exceptional circumstance. The court should be very slow to apply the 'exceptional circumstances' exception, and there must be something in the case that takes it out of the ordinary, and that was not the case here.

\*Consider: is it a requirement that the A consent to being recorded??

*Carr*: HC held no implied requirement of consent to the recording. (A caught on surveillance camera making admissions). A had challenged the admissibility of the admissions on basis he had not consented to being recorded.

**[3. POLICE POWERS OF SEARCH AND SEIZURE]{.underline}**

**The Common Law and Human Rights**

CL is informed by decisions like *Coco*

*HC:* every unauthorized entry upon private property is a trespass, the right of a person in possession or entitled to possession of premises to exclude other from those premises being a fundamental common law right.

Thus these rights are considered in relation to the fundamental right of people to exclude others from their property

[Human Rights]{.underline}

**Right to Privacy** (Art 8 ECHR), (Art 17 ICCPR)

- Broad HR, and dynamic/evolving.

- Art 17: meaning of privacy generally is unclear

- Many people treat it as a bundle of different rights that people have:

- Right to control entry onto private premises (territorial privacy)

- Freedom from interference from your personal space (bodily privacy)

- Controlling personal info

- Freedom from surveillance and from interception of one' communications (privacy of communications)

- However, still remains a very contentious HR

- *R v Pohoretsky* \[1987\] 1 SCR 945 per Lamer J at \[5\]: bodily privacy --a violation of the sanctity of a persons body is much more serious than that of his office or home

- *R v Jefferies* \[1994\] 1 NZLR 290 Richardson J: personal search is a restraint on freedom, and an affront to human dignity.

- 

Right to Liberty and Security of the person (Art 9)

- Almost invariably attached to Right to Privacy in HR, but it is a distinct right

**Personal Search**

***Circumstances and extent of search and seizure: common law and statute***

[Common Law]{.underline}

**Right to Privacy**

- Fundamental right

- CL tend to treat territorial privacy as more important than personal (unlike HR, other way around)

- Delineate only 3 circumstances where police can conduct personal search:

- With the consent of the person

- Pursuant to a warrant

- As an incident to a lawful arrest

[Common Law]{.underline}

Personal search is restricted to:

- With consent

- Pursuant to a warrant

- As an incident of a lawful arrest

[Arrest]{.underline}

- *Lindley v Rutter*

- *POA 58B*

- *Crimes Act (Cth) ss3ZE-3ZI*

*[Lindley v Rutter]{.underline}*

> F: Extent of search and what can they search for and seize?
>
> H: Restricted search and seizure pursuant to an arrest to situations where the police required reasonable grounds to conduct the search (e.g. dangerous driving, hard to establish why a personal search would be needed)

- **To take items relating to the commission of the offence for which arrested;**

- **To prevent escape;**

- **To protect the safety of the accused prisoner or anyone else, such as the arrestor.**

[POA 58B:]{.underline}

allows PO to search anyone they have in lawful custody (NB: DOES NOT HAVE TO BE UNDER ARREST)

Purpose of the search under this section is similar to CL (i.e. retrieve weapon or any material)

**58B. Search of accused person in custody**

      (1) If a police officer **believes on reasonable grounds** that it is necessary to search a person who is in lawful custody, a police officer may search that person --

\(a\) for the purpose of ascertaining whether there is concealed on that person or in that person\'s clothing a weapon or other article capable of being used to inflict injury or to assist that person to escape from custody; or

(ab) for the purpose of removing into safe keeping any other articles belonging to, or in the possession of, the person; or

\(b\) for the purpose of obtaining evidence relating to the commission of the offence or preventing the loss or destruction of such evidence.

      (2) Subsection (1)(b) does not authorise a police officer to require a person to remove any clothing unless there are reasonable grounds for believing that the removal may provide evidence of the commission of an offence.

      (3) A police officer may take and retain --

\(a\) any weapon or article found as a result of the search under subsection (1)(a); and

\(b\) any article of clothing removed under subsection (2).

      (4) A police officer may use such force as is reasonably necessary for the purposes of exercising powers under this section.

      (5) Nothing in this section prevents a search of a person in lawful custody in any circumstances where it is otherwise lawful to search the person.

Note s58B(1)(ab): for removing into safe keeping any other articles belonging to, or in the possession of the person

*How invasive can it be under s58B?*

Covers frisk and strip searching (if reasonable grounds to believe that removal of clothing would assist in finding an offence)

[*Crimes Act (Cth)* 3ZE-3ZI:]{.underline}

outlines for people under arrest.

Includes:

- frisk search,

- ordinary search (the person and any accessory with them, incl. removal of outer garmets), (if RB carrying evidential material or ceaseable items)

- strip search (required to remove all of clothing, not cavity search) --SS can only be carried out when under arrest, and at a police station and only when reasonable grounds to establish they have on them a 'ceasable item' or evidential material

s3ZH: sets out guidelines for a strip search and 3ZI the rules for doing one:

Safe guards re: strip searches:

- Under arrest

- At police station

- Authorised by Superintendent or higer

- And comply with requirement's under 3ZI (e.g. privacy, same sex PO)

S58B does not have the same restrictions on strip searches as the Cth Crimes Act. Concerns in recent times re: police abuse of power to conduct strip searches.

**Tasmanian Statutory Extensions to Police Powers of Personal Search -- Non Arrest Situations**

These provisions usually require the police suspect or believe on reasonable grounds the person has committed an offence under the relevant legislation.

*Police Offences Act* 1935 (Tas) s 58B

*Police Offences Act* 1935 (Tas), ss 58 & 58A

*Search Warrants Act* 1997 (Tas) s 6(2)(a)

*Family Violence Act* 2004 (Tas), s 10(3)

*Poisons Act 1971* (Tas), ss 90A(1)(b), 90B & 90C

*Police Powers (Public Safety) Act 2005*, ss 18(2) & 22

*Misuse of Drugs Act* 2001 (Tas), ss 29, 30

**Grounds of the Search: Reasonable Grounds to Suspect or Believe**

- Most require a 'reasonable grounds for belief' that a search is necessary. Some terminology is 'reasonable suspicion'.

*Lindley v Rutter* \[1981\] QB 128

- where a person is under arrest then the CL tells us that there are a number of requirements that must be fulfilled.

- 1\) Must be on reasonable grounds to conduct a personal search

- 2\) PO can only take items from you that relate to an offence OR narrower, search for items in relation only to the offence that you have been arrested for

- 3\) PO can take items that may assist in escaping or may injure the arrestee or arrestor

*George v Rockett* (1990) 170 CLR 104

H: 'suspcion' and 'believe' refer to quite different states of mind. Applied UK *Hussein*, where suspicion 'refers to a state of mind that is one of conjecture or surmise, 'I suspect, but cannot prove', But need to point to facts that sufficiently arouse the suspicion. The objective circumstances must be sufficient to ground the suspicion.

'Believe' needs the obj circumstances must point to it even more clearly, but don't need to reach the level of proof.

'an inclination of the mind towards acsenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition.

*Nguyen v Elliott* (unreported, Supreme Court, Victoria, Hedigan J, No 7540 of 1994, 6 February 1995)

*How the definitions have been applied*

Court considered how they are applied in the context of a personal search

F: PO observing accussed sitting on park bench, and suspected he was in possession of drugs. Many people approached and talked to A, and he was looking suspicious. PO approached him and ask questions, A responded abusively that he was sick of them. PO then invited him to be searched in a private alley. A refused and resisted the search (assaulted the PO trying to search him, and bit the other PO when he came to PO1's aid).

I: was resistance by the A lawful in these circumstances:

Trial Judge: PO did not have a lawful authority to search him --only approached him out of curiosity, no reasonable suspicion or belief. Therefore, the search was unlawful, and his resistance was lawful (thus successfully defended charge of assault)

\*Evidence needed to establish reasonable suspicion or believe:
