- Entirely dependent on the facts of each case, but for interest re: Sniffer Dogs:

Sniffer Dogs: The Right to Security of the Person Chapter 7 in Gans et al, *Criminal Process and Human Rights* (Federation Press, 2011) pp 268 -- 270; 273 - 275

- Legislation provides that drug dog behavior is a sufficient ground for suspicion to prompt a search

- Problem: drug dogs are inherently unreliable. At most, accuracy is only 26% of all cases where sniffer dog behavior has prompted search

- For this reason, legislation that allows this could be a breach of HR

- Can depend on the type of dog (reknowned dog in Canada with an almost perfect hit rate)

**Search of Premises**

GR: an occupier may deny access to anyone. There must clear legislative authority to allow someone to override that.

CL Starting point: **(narrow)**

search of premises only permitted when police enter on to premises pursuant to an arrest, or with the consent of owner/occupier, pursuant to a search warrant. Originally at CL, SW only permitted in order to retrieve stolen property.

Extent of search permissible at CL: Dillon and O'Brien

At Genreally extends power s to search premises pursuant to a warrant or pre or post arrest.

*Coco v R* (1994) 179 CLR 427 at pp 435-6 (see also *New South Wales v Ibbett* (2006) 229 CLR 638 *and Kuru v New South Wales* (2008) 246 ALR 260 at \[40\]-\[55\])

No clear unambiguous authorization for police to go on to private premises and install a listening device, therefore the legislation did not allow them to do so.

*Ibbett*: if police do trespass onto private premises, aggravated damages are unacceptable.

*Dillon v O'Brien & Davies* (1887) 16 Cox CC 245 -- warrant for arrest

Where police enter premises to arrest, can search an arrestee and the area immediately around the arrestee (e.g. table/desk) for the same purpose (to find evidence of the offence, secure any items that may be used to effect an escape or cause danger to themselves or someone else)

*Jeffrey v Black* \[1977\] 3 WLR 895

F: police arrest accussed outside own premises, can they then take the accussed to his home to search?

H: That search could not be considered to be a search 'incidental' to an arrest (they should have obtained a search warrant)

So...cant arrest in one location and then take to their home to search it (not considered a search incidental to an arrest)

*Ghani v Jones* \[1970\] 1 QB 693

Vastly increased police power of search from *Dillon v O'Brien* when effecting an arrest or under a search warrant. Why? Probs, parliament was being very slow to expand police investigative tools in this area. Very different to that in Australia (which has always had quite extensive search warrants and search provisions).

Court of Appeal: If the police enter premises lawfully in order to affect an arrest or pursuant to a search warrant, they may search the premises to find evidentiary material in relation to the offence that they are arresting for/search warrant for. If during the course of the search find material about another offence, they may cease those items as well, provided they behave reasonably and don't retain the items ceased for an unreasonable period of time. (confirmed in *Rottman)*

**CL Re: searching a premises when police have entered NOT pursuant to an arrest or search warrant**

In *Ghani* the PO had entered with the consent of the occupier. H: they could search in this circumstances, not only to the offence they were investigating, but any other items they reasonably believed to afford evidence to another arrest. But court imposed 5 pre-conditions on the search and procedure:

1.  RB it is a serious offence evidence

2.  Property was the fruit of the crime , or the means of its commission, or material evidence of its commission

3.  That the person in possession was involved in the crimes as a principle or accessory

4.  The police must keep it for no longer than necessary

5.  The lawfulness of the police conduct is to be judged at the time they conducted the search (not with the value of hindsight)

> (and see *Spyke* but cf with *Challenge Plastics*)

*R (on the application of Rottman) v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis* \[2002\] 2 All ER 865; \[2002\] 2 WLR 1315 - arrest warrant for extra-jurisdictional offence.

H: where the offence has been committed outside the jurisdiction, this case said the powers under *Ghani* still apply.

*Spyke & Alexander* Tas Unreported Serial No 2 of 1981 BC8171022

Applied *Ghani* and also in s5 and s6 of *Tas Search Warrant Acts* (the aspect of allowing police to cease items of other offences when entering pursuant to a search warrant under that Act) --not a great deal of reason why they are applying *Ghani* (cf. Challenge Plastics which considered the law carefully)

**Declined *Ghani v Jones***

*Challenge Plastics Pty Ltd v Collector of Customs* (1993) 115 ALR 149 per Heerey J at 157-157

Good analysis of the CL (tried to argue the dissent in *Rottman*)

Heery J: police search did not comply with the statutory provision (went beyond this). Then argued that *Ghani* applied to extend powers beyond the statute.

Declined to apply *Ghani* and gave good analysis of CL in UK and Aust and difference between the 2 jurisdictions.

Re Ghani: No search warrant, arrest warrant, arrest, did not involve stolen goods, the extention of the norm made by Ghani is perhaps even more suprising given denning in some other case. The requirement of a warrant is a statutory safeguard to avoid arbitrary interference with personal property. The State wishing to cease property must swear on oath of the need to cease it (protective safeguard between investigators and citizens). Requires a degree of specificity --and courts will scrutinize warrants very carefully to ensure warrants have been complied with. *Ghani* really makes the process of obtaining a search warrant pretty irrelevant, and will no longer apply the safeguards they were designed to do. If *Ghani* is right, state officials are better off without a warrant at all.

*Laurens v Willers* (2002) 131 A Crim R 281 per Heenan J at \[44\]

H: Ghani v Jones giving far too unconfined powers to the police to conduct search, thus preferred to confine the power of search at CL to that of article in relation to the offence for which the police had entered, and the immediate vicinity (CL approach basically)

Consider the *Search Warrant Act* carefully

## Search and seizure of post

Per s 90N Australian Postal Corporation Act 1989 (the APCA)

**Non-Negotiables (Hard Rules)**

**1. Police can RECEIVE mail without a warrant --- but not open it**

- Australia Post can lawfully **hand over** suspicious mail if it's evidence of an offence.

- **Taking possession ≠ searching or opening.**

- Opening **always** requires a warrant (Search Warrants Act s 5).

**2. Illicit drugs are NOT "dangerous or injurious" for postal law**

- You **cannot** rely on APCA s 90Z to open mail just because it contains drugs.

- Courts have been explicit: drugs ≠ explosives / immediate danger.

- This is a **repeat failure point** in prosecutions.

**3. You must form YOUR OWN prescribed belief**

- A Post employee's suspicion **is not enough**.

- You must personally meet **s 29 Misuse of Drugs Act** threshold.

- "Prescribed belief" = more than suspicion, less than proof (George v Rockett).

- Common lawful method: **drug dog indication** you personally observe.

**4. Even with prescribed belief --- you STILL need a warrant to open**

- s 29 MDA allows **seizure**, not opening.

- Opening without a warrant = unlawful search, high exclusion risk.

**What Australia Post CAN and CAN'T Do**

**They can:**

- X-ray, sniff, scan (non-intrusive examination).

- Disclose the article to police if it's suspected evidence.

- Act under APCA protections if done lawfully and in good faith.

**They cannot:**

- Open mail unless very specific exceptions apply (undeliverable, quarantine, etc.).

- Create lawful grounds for police just by saying "we suspect drugs".

**Practical Workflow (Defensible Path)**

1.  **Confirm permitted person**

    - Verify the AusPost employee is lawfully authorised.

2.  **Document how the parcel came to attention**

    - Random scan vs targeted activity matters (Melick issue).

3.  **Independently form prescribed belief**

    - Drug dog, leakage, weight inconsistencies, smell, etc.

    - You must be able to articulate this **personally**.

4.  **Seize but do not open**

    - Secure chain of custody.

5.  **Obtain a warrant**

    - Telephone warrant acceptable.

    - Can rely on AusPost info + your own observations.

6.  **Open only after warrant**

    - Anything earlier risks exclusion.

**Key Risk Triggers (Red Flags)**

- Opening mail "because Post said so"

- Treating drugs as "dangerous goods"

- No record of *how* belief was formed

- Post employees exceeding their authority (x-ray misuse, disclosures)

- Assuming urgency removes warrant requirements

**Bottom Line**

- **Mail is one of the easiest ways to lose an otherwise strong drug matter.**

- If you shortcut belief formation or opening authority, **the evidence dies**.

- This policy is conservative by design --- follow it and prosecutions survive.

**Search Warrants**

**Common Law**

Purpose of a search warrant: Search warrant operates as a control design --imposes an independent judicial officer between official and citizen. That's why at CL you see the initial conservative approach to issue search warrant (and the jealously the CL protects rights of occupiers)

At CL: need to ensure that goods ceased during the search were covered by the search warrant (if not, could be sued for trespass to private property)

CL rejected the idea of general warrants (i.e. unspecified warrants to search anytime/anywhere) --because they had been used by the Star Chamber in a very oppressive way.

**Statutory extensions**

Have expanded to any offence at all (s5 SWA), not just re: stolen property.

*George v Rockett* (1990) 170 CLR 104

HC explaining the purpose of a search warrant

*Search Warrants Act 1997* (Tas)

*Crimes Act 1914* (Cth), s 3E

*Police Offences Act* 1935 (Tas), s 60

General warrant provision, but confined to searches for stolen property (issued to all PO by the commissioner of police, not issued by an independent judicial officer). They are issued for 6months (and allow police to enter any premises where they have a reasonable believe there may be stolen property).

\*Not HR's compliant (as no safeguard of judicial oversight)

General warrant, couple with *Spyke* gives PO a pretty free hand at searching shit in Tas. All they need is reasonable belief

***Granting a warrant***

***George v Rockett (1990) 170 CLR 104***

F: PO had given an application for a warrant to a Qld Magistrate. The application just stated that reasonable grounds for believe that there were documents on premises re: ex-premier.

HC held: this was completely legit. Just need sufficient evidence placed before the magistrate or JP so that they themselves have a RB/Suspicion that the items were on the property.

Purpose of a search warrant: Search warrant operates as a control design --imposes an independent judicial officer between official and citizen. That's why at CL you see the initial conservative approach to issue search warrant (and the jealously the CL protects rights of occupiers)

This gives an indication of what a JO should do when decided whether to issue a warrant.

HC said warrants can only work effectively if the JO brings an independent mind to the application

***MacLeod (1991) 61 A.Crim.R. 465***

Applied George v Rockett (Slicer J)

What is required for a valid warrant: not the mind of the applicant, but the mind of the issuing authority. Justice cannot rely on the suspicion/believe of the applicant, must form it themselves.

Applications are usually made on oath, or by affidavit, and must put before the authority sufficient evidence to enable them to form a reasonable believe/suspicion

This case is an example where warrant had no technical defect, however court could not be satisfied on the evidence it had (JPs notebook), that the JP actually did have sufficient evidence before him to form reasonable suspicion/belief.

***Disclosure -- what is required of the party seeking a warrant?***

*Karina Fisheries v Mitson* (1990) 26 FCR 473

Full disclosure by the applicant is required that might effect the making of a decision

*Lego Australia v Paraggio* (1994) 52 FCR 542

Only fraud or misrepresentation will invalidate a warrant (sooo mixed authority on this point)

Probs best if PO do disclose all material knowledge they have to the issuing authority, and then if it is issued, unlikely to be challenged in future for non disclosure

***Wording of a Warrant***

*NSW v Corbett* (2007) 230 CLR 606

F: Warrant made reference to a section in legislation that had been repealed at the time it had been applied for

HC: The fact the warrant referred to now no-existent statute didn't matter, as it was quite clear on the warrant what the PO were searching for (unlawfully possessed firearms), didn't matter that the wrong provision had been listed in the warrant.

Sooo....don't need to be technical, but people need to know what the PO are searching for, and that they do have the power to search (which they did in that case)

***What is the extent of power granted under a warrant?***

*Challenge Plastics v Collector of Customs* (1993) 115 ALR 149

Scrutinize legislation to establish what conduct it authorizes.

In Tasmania, we have applied *Ghani v Jones* and *Spyk*

s 6(1)(e) *Search Warrants Act 1997* (Tas)

Must behave reasonably

**Legal Professional Privilege**

Kirby J describes it as a fundamental CL right. And thus it should not be the subject of forced disclosure at the cost of anything. Thus where does it stand when police have a warrant (can they seize items subject to LPP?)

HC authority suggests they can't, and thus LPP trumps a search warrant. Thus, police unable to seize documents covered by LPP from a firm or home. LPP applies pre hearing and at hearing.

Thus a quandary when someone claims LPP in relation to items the police wish to seize. If police look at them, they breach LPP (which is unlawful). This in *Oke*, a number of formal rules were set down for when someone claims LPP:

The rules provide that the document should be bundled up and sent to 3^rd^ party for safe keeping (police not to look). Then the person claiming LPP has a specified time to establish LPP on them on interlocutory proceedings before a court (i.e. proceeding before the proceeding).

***Execution of a warrant***

*Baker v Campbell* (1983) 153 CLR 52

HC decision: legal professional privilege trumps the search warrant (unless the legislation under which the search warrant derives allows it) --courts don't like legislation to trump LPP

*Crowley v Murphy* (1981) 34 ALR 496

Set out the principles behind search warrants, and relies heavily on the principle of legality and the right to privacy. Search warrant can't be used by police to go on a fishing expedition. Thus can't search whole filing cabinet to cease items in it --must be sensible about identifying items that are covered by the warrant. Or 'wreck revenge on people'

*Oke v Commr Australian Federal Police* (2007) 168 A Crim R 503

See notes directly above re: LPP rules/guidelines

Therese reckons this case shows the bravery of lawyers standing up to police

*Larsson v Commr of Police for NSW* (1988) 16 NSWLR 173

Warrant can only be executed in one hit, unless legislation gives them the power to return (however may have separate searches under general warrant)

s 9(2) *Search Warrants Act 1997* (Tas).

Does permit temporary cessation and return to searching under a warrant (but only allows one hour-so basically a lunch break), unless they have consent of the occupier

**When is a warrant executed?**

When police have done everything they have power to do under the warrant.

*Larsson* says police do not have power to execute a warrant after it has already been used

\*\*Look at chapter on search --see what can result from police execution of warrants and search of private premises (police can rip up carpet and walls etc and leave it exactly as they have finished with it) --thus need to be careful when thinking about what is legitimate

**Interrogation**

*Williams* (1986) 161 CLR 278 (**CR**)

**H**. Police cannot arrest for the purposes of furthering the investigation or simply asking questions.

However, note the above discussion regarding s4 of the police the *Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995* (Tas) regarding the power to detain people for reasonable time after lawful arrest.

**Other Protections for Detainees**

**Video recording**

s85A of the *Evidence Act* *2001* (Tas) requires video recording of admissions; if not complied with evidence will be inadmissible (unless exceptions apply). Designed as a protective mechanism to prevent 'verballing'.

*R v Kelly* (2004) 205 ALR 274 (**CR**)

**F**. Kelly didn't want to participate in video recording and instead made admissions on paper. He then made a video recording stating that the admission was only made because the police were harassing him. He then made another statement saying that he was sorry for the video and his own performance while being video taped (stating that he was 'playing the game').

Admissions had to be taped if they were in the course of 'official questioning'. So what is official questioning? Accused argued that it was during official questioning therefore admission not admissible.

**H**. HCA held that this was not official questioning. Majority took a very narrow, textual and specific analysis of the provision while the minority took a far more purposive approach.

Kelly still stands as the law, though we have had differently constituted High Court's interpret different (yet similar provisions) much broader (i.e. *Nichols v Coates*).

*R v Challender* \[2007\] TASSC 58

**F**. Accused arrested early in the morning and was still in detention in the afternoon. Before interview, officer took her outside for chat and smoke. Told her what was going to happen in the interview (i.e. kinds of questions etc) and that she was going to be charged with drug offences.

She said during the chat that all she had was a couple of pills. When he took her up to the interview room he asked her what she had said earlier and asked her to confirm it. Accused became confused and reluctant and wanted to speak to lawyer. Then confirmed she had done it. So the question was, was the earlier chat 'official questioning'.

**H**. Applying Kelly, the court held it was not official questioning. Blow J held that this was not a good approach though. Had complied with the letter of the law but not the spirit. Here they were confirming the previous admission (but it wasn't a very ethical approach -- i.e. be friendly to start with to get them to confess and then trap them). So Blow J held unfair, and excluded the evidence on that basis.

Exceptions to the video recording provisions are in s85 of Evidence Act. May be unreasonable in some situations to require recording. See for example Cox CJ in *Browne*.

*R v Browne* \[2000\] TASSC 105 (**CR**)

**F**. Accused charged with 3 counts of sexual assault. Reasonable mistake as to age was raised as defence. Case considered the meaning of the exceptional circumstances exception in s85 of the *Evidence Act*.

**H**. Cleary lays down the proposition that a court will only find the circumstantial exceptional if the circumstances are ***truly exceptional***. The police should be able to take equipment with them when they know that they are going to make an arrest. Not a basis to excuse the making of the video tape that the police didn't have video equipment with them when they knew they were going to be making a video.

There is also an exception where the accused doesn't consent to the taping (e.g. *Kelly* and also *Nicholls v Coates*). This may take it out of the required area for video taping. Does this mean that the video must be consented to? What about video taping without knowledge? These questions were both considered in *Carr*.

*Carr v Western Australia* (2007) 239 ALR 415

**F**. Admissions made following a bank robbery. Banter outside of the lockup area was going on and the accused wasn't aware that CCTV was recording everything he was saying.

**H**. HCA took a purposive approach. Purpose was to ensure formality. Considered both the consent issue and meaning of interview raised in Browne:

\(1\) *Consent*: As long as the interview was still formal then it was ok. Majority believed that you did not have to give consent. Nothing in the wording of the legislation which imposes a consent requirement on the police. Note, Kirby J dissented and believed that consent was required.

\(2\) *Meaning of interview*: accused argued that the discussions were not an interview. Majority held however that the discussion did constitute an interview (note Kirby J's consent -- needs to be degree of formality to protect right to silence). There had been ***no trickery or collusion***. Majority recognised the purpose of the provisions (to protect the accused) and it is necessary to give it a wide interpretation in order to apply the protection of video recording. Thus, what has occurred here (information conversation outside of the interview room) also constituted an interview.

See also *Crimes Act 1914* (Cth) s 23C-W cf *Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979* (Cth), s 34ZP.

## Provision of interpreter 

Provided for under s5 of the Criminal Law (Detention & Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas).

See also Tas v Stojakovic \[2008\] TASSC 48 about it and the Victorian case of: DPP v Viel & Vocaj \[2024\] VCC 336 (21 March 2024)

Tas pol "interviewing guidelines" state that interp must be obtained.

Also see VtV v R \[2012\] VSCA 213 about hearsay implications and requirement to summons interp

**Police Powers of Search**

*International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights;* Article 17

**Personal Search**

The common law recognises the right to privacy should not be interfered with except for in extreme circumstances.

Thus, at common law, a lawful search and seizure can only

- occur pursuant to a warrant;

- incident to a lawful arrest; or

- with consent.

*Lindley v Rutter* \[1981\] QB 128 (**CR**)

**F**. Involved a woman being searched including the removal of underwear etc.

**H**. Held that the police had not basis for a lawful search, particularly to remove underwear. Appeal thus allowed and convictions quashed. Must not simply act on orders to search or seize, must consider the circumstances of the actual case to determine if the accused is doing anything that indicates a search may be necessary. Police argued that 'standing orders' required all females in custody had to remove bra. But she had done nothing to indicate she may self harm.

Restated the common law position (above). Further, they held that the powers of search in relation to arrest only extend to:

(1) obtaining evidence

(2) Prevent the destruction of evidence

(3) to locate items that may assist the accused to escape or commit further crime (i.e. to find a weapon that may help them escape)

(4) to search for items that may be used to inflict self harm.

So police powers of search are restricted to situations where they have a specific reasons to search a particular individual. Policies or 'standing orders' for particular classes of arrestee will not be a sufficient reason.

Note that we these principles now have statutory force:

*Police Offences Act* 1935 (Tas.), s 58B.

***Search of accused person in custody***

***(1)** If a police officer believes on reasonable grounds that it is necessary to search a person who is in lawful custody, a police officer may search that person --*

***(a)** for the purpose of ascertaining whether there is concealed on that person or in that person\'s*

*clothing a weapon or other article capable of being used to inflict injury or to assist that person to*

*escape from custody; or*

***(ab)** for the purpose of removing into safe keeping any other articles belonging to, or in the possession*

*of, the person; or*

***(b)** for the purpose of obtaining evidence relating to the commission of the offence or preventing the*

*loss or destruction of such evidence.*

***(2)** [[Subsection (1)(b)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=44%2B%2B1935%2BGS58B%2FGs1%2FHpb%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS58B@Gs1@Hpb@EN) does not authorise a police officer to require a person to remove any clothing unless there are reasonable grounds for believing that the removal may provide evidence of the commission of an offence.*

***(3)** A police officer may take and retain --*

***(a)** any weapon or article found as a result of the search under [[subsection (1)(a)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=44%2B%2B1935%2BGS58B%2FGs1%2FHpa%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS58B@Gs1@Hpa@EN); and*

***(b)** any article of clothing removed under [[subsection (2)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=44%2B%2B1935%2BGS58B%2FGs2%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS58B@Gs2@EN).*

***(4)** A police officer may use such force as is reasonably necessary for the purposes of exercising powers under this section.*

***(5)** Nothing in this section prevents a search of a person in lawful custody in any circumstances where it is otherwise lawful to search the person.*

*Nguyen v Elliot* 6^th^ February 1995 VicSC

**F**. Police watching group of Asian youths in park (were known to have been involved in other offences). When they saw the police they behaved suspiciously (i.e. looking around etc). Eventually approached one of the boys and said they wanted to search. He objected. Police persisted and eventually he kicked a police officer (bit one during scuffle). Continued to resist and was then handcuffed and charged him with numerous offences relating to the resistance. No drugs found on him.

**H**. Accused defended the charges on the grounds that the police were not in the execution of their duty. They had no reasonable basis for the search. TJ and appeal judge agreed. Held that the police had no right to search the young men as there was no reasonable basis for the belief that the youths were in possession of drugs. Accused could thus not be prosecuted for the offences.

But, the only reason the Court of Criminal Appeal upheld the decision was that they said the TJ had a better basis to judge the demeanour of the witnesses. Clear they were reluctant to do this. The whole tenor of the decision is that they wanted to agree with the police.

Note the inroads into these principles; for example the *Misuse of Drugs Act* 2001 (Tas) ss29 & 30 provides that police can stop and search a person provided they have reasonable grounds for suspecting someone possesses a controlled substance. Search is thus made permissible without an arrest.

Also note the *Crimes Act* 1914 (Cth) ss3ZE-3ZI (covers frisk searches, ordinary searches and strip searches).

**Medical Examinations, Fingerprinting and Photographs**

Uniform legislation (*Forensic Procedures Act 2000* (Tas)) in this area gives the police very wide powers to obtain forensic samples from suspects and people charged with offences. Deals with two kinds of procedures; ***intimate*** and ***non-intimate***.

Non-intimate procedures can be performed with consent or on order of senior police officer (i.e. Sergeant or above).

Intimate procedures require informed consent or order of judicial officer.

***Non-intimate:***

- Taking blood

- Swabs (saliva)

- Hair samples (non-pubic)

- Nail clippings

- Scrapings from under nails

- Chemical washes

- Finger prints

- Hand prints

- Foot prints

- Photographs

- Etc

***Intimate:***

- Examinations of genital areas

- Cavity searches

- Pubic hair samples

- Genital impressions.

See also *Crimes Act* 1914 (Cth) ss.3ZJ-3ZL.

**Search of Premises**

The common law protection of property is extensive. For example held in *Coco v R* (1994) 179 CLR 427 at 435-6 the court held that every unauthorised entry to private property is a trespass and it is the right of property owners to exclude (this is a fundamental common law right).

Courts will therefore narrowly construe statutory authority to enter premises. Also, any statutory authority in that regard must be clearly expressed (*unmistakable and unambiguous language*). The presumption is that in the absence of express provision to the contrary the legislature did not intend to permit otherwise tortuous conduct. Therefore at common law, search of premises without consent is usually confined to searches pursuant to a ***search warrant*** or ***incidental to arrest***.

*Family Violence Act 2004* (Tas), s 10

**Power of police to enter certain premises**

**(1)** A police officer may, without warrant, and using such force as is necessary, enter and remain on premises for such period as he or she considers reasonably necessary to prevent family violence --

**(a)** at the request of a person who apparently resides on the premises; or

**(b)** if the officer reasonably suspects that family violence is being, has been or is likely to be committed

on those premises.

**(2)** A police officer who enters premises under [[subsection (1)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=67%2B%2B2004%2BGS10%2FGs1%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS10@Gs1@EN) may --

**(a)** arrest, without warrant, any person on those premises to facilitate the issue of a PFVO, or the

making of an application for an FVO, in respect of that person; and

**(b)** remain on those premises in order to conduct a risk screening, safety audit or forensic examination

with such assistance as is necessary and reasonable in the circumstances.

**(3)** A police officer who enters premises under [[subsection (1)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=67%2B%2B2004%2BGS10%2FGs1%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS10@Gs1@EN) may, without warrant --

**(a)** search any person on those premises whom the officer reasonably suspects of having in his or her

possession any object which the police officer reasonably suspects has been used, or may be used, to

commit a family violence offence or has been created in the commission of a family violence offence;

and

**(b)** search those premises for the presence of any such object; and

**(c)** seize and retain any such object.

**(4)** If a police officer reasonably suspects that a person has committed family violence and in so acting has used or created an object, the officer may, without warrant and using such force as is necessary --

**(a)** enter any premises on which the officer reasonably suspects that the object may be found; and

**(b)** search those premises for the object; and

**(c)** search any person who the officer reasonably suspects has possession of the object; and

**(d)** seize and retain the object; and

**(e)** arrest, without warrant, any person on those premises to facilitate the issue of a PFVO, or the

making of an application for an FVO, in respect of that person.

**(5)** Where a police officer reasonably suspects that --

**(a)** a person has committed, or is likely to commit, family violence; and

**(b)** the person is in possession or control of a firearm --

the police officer may enter premises, without warrant and using such force as is reasonably necessary

in the circumstances, in order to search for and seize the firearm.

**(6)** On an application made by a police officer or by any person who claims to be the owner of an object seized and detained under [[subsection (3)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=67%2B%2B2004%2BGS10%2FGs3%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS10@Gs3@EN), [[(4)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=67%2B%2B2004%2BGS10%2FGs4%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS10@Gs4@EN) or [[(5)]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=67%2B%2B2004%2BGS10%2FGs5%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#GS10@Gs5@EN), a court may order that the object --

**(a)** be forfeited to the Crown; or

**(b)** be destroyed; or

**(c)** be returned to the owner; or

**(d)** be otherwise disposed of in such manner as the court thinks fit.

**(7)** If a police officer reasonably suspects that a person has committed family violence, the officer, without warrant and using such force as is reasonably necessary in the circumstances, may --

**(a)** enter any premises on which the officer reasonably suspects the person may be found; and

**(b)** search those premises for the person; and

**(c)** arrest the person.

**(8)** In this section --

**\"object\"** means any matter, material or thing and includes firearms.

*Misuse of Drugs Act 2001* (Tas), s32

**Power to pursue suspect onto private property**

Where:

**(a)** a police officer reasonably believes that a person has committed an offence under this Act; and

**(b)** the person flees from the police officer\'s presence while the police officer, or another police officer,

is trying to arrest the person or exercise another power under this Act in relation to the person; and

**(c)** the police officer, or another police officer, immediately pursues the person and the pursuit

continues without interruption; and

**(d)** during the pursuit the person enters a private residence or another place that is not a public place

the pursuing police officer may, ***without warrant*** and using no more force than is reasonably necessary for the purpose, enter the private residence or other place notwithstanding that it is not a public place.

*New South Wales v Ibbett* (2006) 229 CLR 638 (above)

**H**. This case demonstrated the lengths the court will go to in order to protect the above principles. If the police enter private premises without consent the courts will not be reluctant to award aggravated damages to send a clear message to the executive.

*Kuru v New South Wales* \[2008\] HCA 26 at \[40\]-\[55\]

**H**. High Court re-stated a number of the key principles in this area.

(1) A person who enters the land of another must justify that entry by showing either that the entry was with the consent of the occupier or that the entrant had lawful authority to enter.

(2) Except in cases provided for by the common law and by statute, police officers have no special rights to enter land.

(3) Authority to enter land may be revoked and that, if the authority is revoked, the entrant no longer has authority to remain on the land but must leave as soon as is reasonably practicable.

(4) A police officer may enter premises to *prevent* a breach of the peace, but not to investigate whether a breach of the peace has or will occur.

The common law originally confined search of premises (without consent) to searches pursuant to warrant or incidental to lawful arrest. *Dillon v O'Brian* shows that the personal search of an arrestee extends to the immediate area around the person (table in front of them or car they are in). The question is; how far can that search extend?

*Ghani v Jones* \[1970\] 1 QB 693

*This case represents the 'high water mark'*

**H**. Per Lord Denning: it is settled law that where the police enter premises pursuant to search warrant or pursuant to power of arrest in relation to serious offence; they are entitled to search for and take any goods in relation to that offence. If during the search they find other goods relating to other events they are entitled to seize them as well provided they behave reasonably and detain the goods for no longer than is necessary.

Police can gain lawful entry, so long as they reasonable believe:

(1) that a serious offence has been committed;

(2) that the property was the fruit of the crime or the means by which it was committed, or material evidence of its commission;

(3) that the person in possession was involved in its commission either as a principal or accessory;

Further:

(4) the police must keep it for no longer than necessary to complete their investigations or preserve it for evidence;

(5) The lawfulness of the police conduct must be judged at the time of the seizure, not by what happens later (i.e. hindsight and subsequent events cannot justify the seizure).

To *some* extent *Ghani* has been enacted in Tasmania.

*Jeffrey v Black* \[1977\] 3 WLR 895

**H**. police cannot arrest someone in one location and then trot them off to another location to search that location. This is not a search incidental to arrest.

**Search Warrants**

Search Warrants Act 1997 (Tas)

A search warrant is a document which authorises an invasion of private premises without permission of the owner or occupier. Seeks to interpose a judicial officer between the executive officer who will conduct the search and the private citizen. So they act as a control against excessive executive action. The police must justify or provide a basis for the search.

Original common law approach was to only grant search warrants for stolen property. Judicial officer had to be satisfied that particular stolen property was on the premises; both had to be described with particularity. Also couldn't seize goods not covered by the warrant; i.e. the common law rejected 'general warrants': see *Entick v Carrington* (1765) 19 St.T 129.

However, this position has now been wound back (see *Ghani v Jones*) provided the five conditions are met (see *Search Warrants Act*). Also, s60 of the *Police Offences Act* abrogates the common law regarding general warrants.

*Police Offences Act 1935* (Tas), s60

**Commissioner\'s warrant**

**(1)** It shall be lawful for the Commissioner to issue to any police officer a general warrant, in the appropriate form set forth in [[Schedule II]{.underline}](http://www.thelaw.tas.gov.au/tocview/content.w3p;cond=;doc_id=44%2B%2B1935%2BJSII%2FEN%2B20081016000000;histon=;prompt=;rec=;term=#JSII@EN), authorizing such officer to search for stolen property.

**(2)** Every such warrant shall be signed by the Commissioner personally, and shall remain in force, unless sooner revoked, for 6 months from the date thereof, or for such shorter period, if any, as may be specified therein, and shall authorize the officer therein named to exercise the powers therein set forth.

**(3)** The Commissioner, at any time, may revoke any general warrant issued by him.

**Duties of the applicant and issuing officer in relation to search warrants**

*Spyke* Tas Unreported 2/1981

*George v Rockett* (1990) 170 CLR 104 (**CR**)

**F**. Rocket obtained a search warrant to search George's office (solicitor). Wanted documents (bundles of pages). Argued it was unlawful as the belief wasn't held by the justice at the time that any evidence was held which would actually assist at trial.

**H**. Need to show supporting facts that the evidence will assist at trial. The judicial officer cannot just 'rubber stamp it'. Need reasonable grounds to grant the warrant (not the justice himself though, just that it could be held on the facts).

So you have to make the search warrant mean something. If it is to interpose a judicial officer between police and individual then you will only achieve this if the Justice actually brings their mind to the question of whether or not to grant the warrant.

Won't be a lawfully issued warrant unless the judicial officer is ***satisfied that there are grounds for issuing the warrant***. This requires the police to produce information which is capable of satisfying the judicial officer.

HCA held that a suspicion is more than a mere idle wondering. It is a positive feeling or apprehension amounting to a slight opinion. So must be afforded evidence that provides reasonable grounds for giving this belief.

*MacLeod* (1991) 61 A.Crim.R. 465

**H**. *George v Rockett* was applied by Slicer J here. See para 8-9 for a summary of the law in this area:

- it is the state of mind of the issuing officer that is relevant.

- issuing officer must satisfy himself or herself that the necessary criteria are present

- issuing officer cannot rely merely on the suspicion of the informant

- need for particularity in relation to object and the offence; mere reference to the relevant section is not enough.

*Beneficial Finance Corp v AFP* (1991) 31 FCR 523

**H**. The issuing officer doesn't have an obligation to go behind the warrant and investigate further (even if incomplete). However, there is an obligation to clear up ambiguities or make enquiries if they have concerns. Also see *Jackson v Mijovich* (1991) 23 ALD 470.

*NSW v Corbett* \[2007\] HCA 32

*Degree of precision required in identifying the offence*

**F**. Warrant issued to search for firearms. The particular provision relied upon in the warrant had been repealed though. At first instance it was held that this misstatement of the offence invalidated the warrant. Upheld by the Court of Appeal (NSW) then appealed to HCA.

**H**. Appeal allowed. Statement of offence in search warrants ***does not have to have the precision that is required in an indictment***. Only require a statement of the ***nature of the offence*** so that you can clearly see what the search is for and establish the boundaries of the search.

Here the warrant was clear in stating the nature of the offence and all other details. Repealed provision had been replaced by a very similar provision so you couldn't say that this statement could possibly have given rise to any misapprehension of the police power to search for firearms. So here the similarity of the provisions was crucial.

There is conflicting authority as to the duty of disclosure on the part of the applicant for the search warrant. *Karina Fisheries v Mitson* (1990) 26 FCR 473 held that ***all matters must be disclosed*** that would affect the issuing of the warrant. However, in *Lego Australia v Paraggio* 25/8/94 it was held that only where there has been fraud or misrepresentation will a warrant be struck out for non-disclosure. Most prosecutors believe though that everything relevant should be disclosed in the interests of best practice and conservativeness.

*Crowley v Murphy* (1981) 34 ALR 496

**H**. Police cannot go on a '***fishing exercise***' looking for documents when they don't really know what they are looking for.

*Baker v Campbell* (1983) 153 CLR 52 (**CR**)

*Search warrants and their application to documents protected by legal professional privilege*

**F**. D (police) attempted to seize documents protected by privilege.

**H**. HCA held that the search warrant did not cover the documents as they were covered by LPP. Legal professional privilege is not simply a rule of evidence; rather it is a ***fundamental common law human right***. So the HCA treat LPP in a very important fashion. Search warrants thus cannot apply to documents covered by LPP. This causes some significant problems in practice.

After Baker, AFP and law reform commission developed new approach to deal with privileged documents. Settled on an approach that results in all documents being sent to independent person for safe keeping while the person claiming privilege then has four days to show that the documents are truly covered by privilege.

*Oke v Commr Australian Federal Police* \[2007\] FCA 27

*Demonstrates the difficulties with the above process.*

**F**. Oke contacted solicitor about police going through documents (including client/solicitor information). Solicitor then spent the rest of the day trying to negotiate with police to not look at documents he alleged were covered by privilege. Persuaded the police to desist in search and entered into written agreement. This solicitor really stood his ground. AFP hadn't exercised the warrant properly and tried to get out of the whole thing by returning the documents. He refused so they dumped them on his doorstep.

**H**. The warrant was invalid.

A warrant is executed when everything that can lawfully be done, has been done: *Larsson v Commr of Police for NSW* (1988) 16 NSWLR 173, Also see s9 of the *Search Warrants Act* which deals with cessations.

See also *Crimes Act* 1914 (Cth), s 3E - 3UK.

# Extradition:

Seek a remad order under service and execution act

Look at originating jurisdiction for presumptions for/against bail as this can be used to support opposing bail

See one drive for examples and forms.

Notes from court:

Warrant in first instance as less persuasive effect to oppose bail then a warrant for failing to appear (fleeing the jurisdiction)

Bail question?

Be sure to check there is no impediment or restrictions that might frustrate or prevent his extradition from the state of Tasmania.

Priors

Introduction of the existence of warrant

the charges that gave rise to the substantial matter

Reading of the alleged facts

Circumstances of which the warrant was issued including

Service and Execution Act CTH -- does not require the tas court to have regard to original jurisdiction principles -- NEED TO look at that Act (Tas procedufre is to be followed! Not interstate principles of bail - -- could probably sill submit there is no obligation to do so, but that there may be a higher threshold to be considered before bail is granted.

**Court notes for extradition couple:**

**IDENTIFY THE PARTIES**

Your honour both parties were apprehended today by officers of Tasmania Police. During their investigation, it was revealed that the parties are both the subject of interstate arrest warrants:

Mr. Clayton Tierny having failed to appear at the Local Court of NSW at Sutherland on the 8^th^ of May,

Ms. Jedde Maloney, having failed to appear at the Local Court of NSW at Parramater on the 4^th^ of April. Addtionally, Ms. Maloney was on Parole with a balance of 3 months and 2 days remaining of her sentence, at the time, and I understand, that her failing to appear amounted to an offence in that State, and a subsequent breach of her parole order . As a consequence, there was a revocation order made, and a warrant for apprehensions and detention was issued on the 18^th^ of April by the NSW Parole Authority

Pursuant to the Commonwealth ***Service and Execution of Process Act 1992*** I tender copies of those warrants, executed earlier this evening.

> **TENDER COPIES**

You honour, the exaction of interstate warrants is provided for under **PART 5** of that same Act.

Section 83, sub-s (14), paragraph (a) empowers a justice of the peace to adjourn these proceedings and remand the defendants on bail, or in such custody as the justice of the peace specifies, for the period of the adjournment.

It is my application that these proceedings be adjourned until 14:15 tomorrow, that the defendants be held in remand, to appear by video link at that time.

The defendant's will be the subject of extradition hearings

**Ensure:**

There needs to be a full detailed itinerary of movements from within Tas, out of Tas, and every step until in another jurisdictions court. Flight details and names of interstate officers etc**.\**

**Section 3: Interpretation**

***magistrate***,

except in sections 57 and 67, includes:

\(a\) a justice of the peace who has power to issue warrants under a law of the State in which the justice holds that office; and

\(b\) a person who is appointed under section 120 of the Magistrates' Court Act 1989 of Victoria as a bail justice or is a bail justice because of holding a prescribed office within the meaning of section 121 of that Act.

***person under restraint*** means a person who:

\(a\) is on bail; or

\(b\) has been conditionally released from prison (whether on parole, licence, work release, home detention or otherwise) before the end of a term of imprisonment to which he or she has been sentenced; or

\(c\) is subject to the supervision of another person under a probation order; or

\(d\) is serving a period of home detention or a term of imprisonment by way of periodic detention; or

\(e\) is subject to:

\(i\) a community service order; or

\(ii\) a community based order; or

\(iii\) an attendance order; or

\(iv\) a work and development order; or

\(v\) any other restriction on his or her movements, imposed by law or by order of a court, that is inconsistent with the person complying with a subpoena served on the person under this Act;

but does not include a person who is in prison.

## Documents to provide court

The attached is self-explanatory and Jodie sets it out below. I think it is a good idea which will make extraditions that little easier. I have saved it to the Forms folder, please use it for future extraditions.

I have attached a copy of the Memorandum of Order used for applications for Extradition that may be of use to your Staff bringing these types of matters to court. In the past Prosecutors have handed up a typed copy of the order component of this document which has been of great benefit in expediting the matter in court. Just thought it might help, give me a call if I can help with anything else.

**[Below is the written order you should prepare and give to the court]{.underline}**

**[MEMORANDUM OF ORDER.]{.underline}**

In the Court of Petty Sessions held at Devonport.

DEFENDANT: JOHN BOB SMITH

**That** John Bob SMITH (DOB ...........), a prisoner in your custody and upon an application for Extradition to the State of Queensland

**[ORDER OF THE COURT]{.underline}**

Under Section 83(8)(b) of the *Service and Execution of Process Act,* IT IS ORDERED

That the said John Bob Smith is to be taken into the Custody of Tasmania Police and Detective Sergeant Mark John Corry, Criminal Investigation, and conveyed from the State of Tasmania on 25^th^ June, 2008 to the State of Queensland, to appear at Court at Caboolture Magistrates Court, 30 King Street Caboolture at 2:15pm on Friday the 27^th^ June, 2008.

[DATED]{.underline} this 25^th^ June, 2008

[MAGISTRATE]{.underline}

[DEVONPORT]{.underline}

# Police as Prosecutors 

**JUSTICES ACT**

**38.**   **Representation of parties by counsel, &c.**

(1)  A complainant may, in person or by the complainant\'s lawyer, conduct his or her case and examine and cross-examine witnesses.

(2)  A defendant must, in person or by the defendant\'s lawyer, be admitted to make his or her full answer and defence to the complaint and to examine and cross-examine witnesses.

(3)  If the complainant is a public officer, the complainant may, in person or by the complainant\'s lawyer or another public officer, conduct his or her case and examine and cross-examine witnesses.

(4)  With the consent of the justices, a police officer may conduct the case of any complainant, and examine and cross-examine witnesses.

# Court Procedure 

## Challenging Adjournments

### Where defendant doesn't have a lawyer and keeps seeking an adj

Dietrich is the HC case about adjournments and miscarriage of justice if serious/ complicated matters proceed without legal counsel through no fault of the accused. Its been considered in a number of by TASSC. There is a good line in Robinson v Lane \[2011\] TASSC 70 "would result in the legal system grinding to a halt". Robinson v Lane 2011 TASSC 70 has a good summary of what accused would have to show and in that case Magistrate was not held to have made appealable error.

***Robinson v Lane 2011 TASSC 70***

Crazy woman insisted on a hearing despite a precious contest mention saying shed just get a fine. Unable to get a lawyer after that as LA funding didn't extend to fine only likely outcomes. There were a number of adjournments. She commenced the hearing and then found some challenges in the course of CX and EIC but Magi Mollard said no adj, crack on. Sha appealed because representing herself and claimed unable to run a case to same standard as a lawyer and she had mental health issues. TASSC ruled the magi was ok in doing what he did.

\[22\] *"It is not incumbent on a magistrate, in my view, where there is no apparent impediment to an unrepresented litigant conducting their case, to make exhaustive enquiries as to possible impediments before a hearing is even embarked upon. If such an impediment becomes obvious during the course of a hearing, the magistrate might then adjourn to address it. In this case, that is what the learned magistrate said he would do if necessary."*

\[24\] *"As to her suggestion that a normal lay person would have difficulty presenting the same sort of defence as a lawyer, were any court to decline to hear a matter simply because a litigant was unrepresented and might not present a case in the same manner as a lawyer, would result in the legal system grinding to a halt."*

## Adjournments

Basic principle is that where a party seeks an adjournment which will create no injustice to the other side and which can adequately be remedied by an appropriate order for costs, then it seems to me that unless there are compelling reasons, such as manoeuvring or deliberate dilatoriness or inaction, ordinarily the discretion of justices to grant an adjournment ought not be exercised adversely to the party applying for it.

***Hammilton v Litteljohn \[2006\] TASSC 109***

*21 James v Williams; Ex parte James \[1967\] Qd R 496 was a case of appellate review of a refusal to grant an adjournment of the hearing of a complaint in circumstances very similar to the present matter, except that in James, the defendant sought an order that the case be dismissed when the prosecution was not ready to proceed. Hart J said, at 501 - 502:*

*\"If a defendant who has been served with a summons to appear on a certain day attends at Court on that day ready to answer the complaint, and the complainant is not then ready to proceed, then, if nothing else appears, the prima facie order to be made would be to adjourn the hearing and to require the complainant to pay the expenses of the defendant - the costs thrown away. If justice can be done by adjourning the case and ordering payment of costs, there is no justification for dismissing the complaint where the bona fides of the complainant is not challenged.\"*

*22 As Bowen LJ observed in Cropper v Smith (1884) 26 Ch D 700 at 710, \"Courts do not exist for the sake of discipline, but for the sake of deciding matters in controversy \...\". It is apparent that in the present case that is just what the learned magistrate was attempting to do by refusing the application for an adjournment and by dismissing the complaint, namely, discipline the prosecution. It is clear that his discretion miscarried when he refused to grant the application for an adjournment and this miscarriage resulted in the order of dismissal being made. He fell into error in that he failed to take into account, or adequately take into account:*

*(i) the public interest in the prosecution and trial of charges such as those brought against the respondent;*

*(ii) the reason for Mr Miller\'s inability to proceed with the hearing was due to inadvertent error;*

*(iii) the application for an adjournment was not opposed by the respondent;*

*(iv) the respondent did not claim that he would suffer any prejudice if an order of adjournment was made, nor was any such prejudice apparent;*

*(v) the order of dismissal was not sought by either of the parties to the litigation before the learned magistrate.*

*23 The learned magistrate also erred in placing too much weight upon:*

*(i) what he described as \"case management\";*

*(ii) the right of the courts to \"properly run\" the business in the courts; and*

*(iii) the need to achieve timely disposition of the complaint*.

## Costs

[See **Department of Police and Emergency Management v Jones \[2007\] TASSC 107**]{.mark}

Cost orders in criminal matters

***Costs in Criminal Cases Act 1976, Section 4***

**4. Costs of successful defendant**

      **(1)** Subject to this Act, where a person having been charged with an offence is discharged from the proceedings in respect thereof, that is to say, where --

**(a)** he is acquitted of the offence;

**(b)** the complaint charging him with the offence is dismissed or withdrawn; or

**(c)** he is discharged upon an indictment for the offence --

the court having the conduct of the proceedings may, upon the application of the defendant, order that he be paid in respect of his defence such costs as it thinks just and reasonable.

      **(2)** The court, in deciding whether to grant costs and the amount of any costs granted, shall have regard to all relevant circumstances and in particular to the following:

**(a)** Whether the proceedings were brought and continued in good faith;

**(b)** Whether proper steps were taken to investigate any matter coming to, or within, the knowledge of any person responsible for bringing or continuing the proceedings;

**(c)** Whether the investigation into the offence was conducted in a reasonable and proper manner;

**(d)** Whether the evidence as a whole would support a finding of guilt but the defendant is discharged from the proceedings on a technical point;

**(e)** Whether the defendant is discharged from the proceedings because he established (either by the evidence of witnesses called by him or by cross examination of witnesses for the prosecution or otherwise) that he was not guilty.

      **(3)** No defendant shall be granted costs by reason only of the fact that he is acquitted of an offence, the complaint charging him with an offence is dismissed or withdrawn, or he is discharged upon an indictment.

      **(4)** No defendant shall be refused costs by reason only of the fact that the proceedings were properly brought and continued.

      **(5)** No defendant shall be refused costs by reason only of the fact that in the investigation of the offence with which he had been charged he remained silent or refused to assist in respect thereof.

Costs in Criminal Cases Act 1976, Section 5

**5. Costs of convicted defendant**

Where a defendant is convicted but the court is of the opinion that the charge involved a difficult or important point of law and that in the special circumstances of the case it is proper that he should receive costs in respect of the arguing of that point of law, the court may order that he be paid such costs as it thinks just and reasonable.

***R v Freshney* TSR 126 \[1977\]**

Cost in Criminal Cases

The *Cost in Criminal Cases Act 1976* by Section 4 provides for coast to be awarded in the courts discretion to defendants not prosecuted to conviction and for some matters to be considered and others not, to be bars in exercising that discretion

- The discretion is not to be fettered

- None of the matters considered is to be conclusive

- The court should refrain as far as possible from expressing or hinting at any view as to the guilt or innocence of the applicant

- The grant of power to award costs is to be construed in the context of the enactment against the background of the prior law

- The rules that criminal trials are for many reasons not to be equiparated to civil trials and that acquitted persons are not entitled to costs remain subject only to the act

- To award cost a positive reason must be shown, other than an acquittal, whether or not based on an error, inefficiency or irregularity, to give an applicant a just claim on the public for his costs

## Stay of proceedings:

Stay of proceedings defined as: ***The permanent or temporary discontinuance of an action, by order of the court.*** Magistrates do not have an implied power to stay proceedings. Tasmania authority ***Lillico v McKenna (1995) 5 Tas R 147. They can dismiss them however. Which is not the same thing***

It is the accepted position that the Magistrates Courts do not have an inherent jurisdiction to stay proceedings, (*Visser v Hodgetts *[\[2002\] TASSC 44](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2002/44.html), [10 Tas R 422)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=10%20Tas%20R%20422)

## Course of hearings (course of proceedings)

Defendant need not give evidence first. ***RPS v The Queen \[2000\] HCA 3 3 February 2000 S116/1998***

*\[84\] Hunt CJ at CL in the Court of Criminal Appeal stated what the true position was in these terms: \"Before turning to the appellant\'s argument ... it should be pointed out that the law does not say that the accused person must be called to give evidence before any other witnesses. It has often been said that it is desirable that he be called first, as he perforce will be present when other witnesses give evidence before him and the comment may be made that he has tailored his own evidence to fit in with theirs. The judge was correct when he said that it is customary to call the accused first (if he is to be Callinan J 32. called at all), but he was wrong to say that he must be.\" (emphasis in original*

### Closing submissions 

Rule 35 of the Justices rules says that:

If defendant gives evidence then they make first closing.

If they don't, pross goes first.

# Giving evidence by phone or audio/ visual link

Can be done!

Section 67 of the Justices Rules.

Is to be done in accordance with Evidence (Audio and Audio Visual Links) Act 1999 ;

10B of that act explains process for putting documents to a witness remotely.

# Mental states/ intent

12.5 In Australia, criminal offences are generally characterised in one of three ways:

• mens rea offences---the prosecution must prove a physical element (actus reus) and a mental element (mens rea);

• strict liability offences---the prosecution is not required to prove fault, but there is a defence of reasonable mistake available;5 and

• absolute liability offences---proof of fault is not required and no defences are available.6

*R v Hodgson* \[1985\] TASSC 40

***\"\... in law it is clear that the word \'intention\' like the word \'malice\' covers all consequences whatever which the doer of the act foresees as likely to result from it, whether he does the act with an actual desire of producing them or only in recklessness as to whether they ensue or not.\"***

### Voluntary and Intentional:

Intention -- Relates to the **act** not the outcome of the act. the act must exist in idea before it exists in fact.

Prescribed in s 13 of the Code

**13. Intention and motive**

\(1\) No person shall be criminally responsible for an act, unless it is voluntary and intentional; nor, except as hereinafter expressly provided, for an event which occurs by chance.

\(2\) Except as otherwise expressly provided, no person shall be criminally responsible for an omission, unless it is intentional.

\(3\) Any person who with intent to commit an offence does any act or makes any omission which brings about an unforeseen result which, if he had intended it, would have constituted his act or omission some other offence, shall, except as otherwise provided, incur the same criminal responsibility as if he had effected his original purpose.

\(4\) Except where it is otherwise expressly provided, the motive by which a person is induced to do any act or make any omission is immaterial.

### Arguments in support of voluntary and intentional:

**Based on facts of case:**

Where defendant is clear, confident, deliberate in their denial/ communication with police about their version of events, it is arguable on the facts that they were not presenting as someone who commits an involuntary act

**Strict Liability:**

There is a common law presumption that knowledge of an unlawful act is an ingredient in every offence.

A strict liability offence is one where "intent" does not need to be proved. However, the defence of honest and reasonable mistake is still available. Need only prove that the defendant performed the prohibited act.

Driving without a licence is a strict liability offence. There is a defence if the driver has no notice of the fact that their licence has been suspended (case law and in defence prescribed in the Act).

**Absolute Liability:**

Need not prove that the defendant knew the action was an offence, only that he committed it.

**The test of absolute vs Strict**

The words of the statute itself

The subject matter in which it is concerned

Whether "absolute" liability will resolve the mischief concerend4

**Specific intent:**

A crime which an intent to bring about a particular result is upon the true construction of the provisions of the code or statute creating the offence

**Subjective recklessness:**

Foreseeing the possibility of the application of force but was heedless of such consequences when they acted.

Wilfully in the charge of arson (imported by section 267(2)) includes recklessness -- arson may be committed if D deliberately does a willed act aware that setting fire to a building is a likely consequence of his act .

*B v Visser* \[2000\] TASSC 141

*2 Under the Criminal Code, s268, any person who unlawfully sets fire to any building is guilty of a crime, which is called arson. By virtue of the Code, s267(3), an act causing injury to property cannot constitute the crime of arson \"unless it is done wilfully\". The word \"wilfully\" includes \"recklessly\", so that arson may be committed if the accused deliberately does a willed act, aware that setting fire to a particular building is a likely consequence of that act, and if he or she recklessly does that act, regardless of that risk: R v Hodgson [\[1985\] TASSC 40](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1985/40.html); [\[1985\] Tas R 75](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1985%5d%20Tas%20R%2075); R v Gardenal-Williams [\[1989\] TASSC 29](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1989/29.html); [\[1989\] Tas R 62.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1989%5d%20Tas%20R%2062) *

## Strict Vs Absolute liability 

The law recognises three categories of statutory offences -- (1) those in which the prosecution must prove some state of mind, often referred to as mens rea, as an essential ingredient; (2) offences of \"**strict liability**\", as to which the prosecution need not prove any mental element unless the defendant adduces or elicits evidence suggesting an honest and reasonable mistaken belief in a state of facts that, if true, would render his or her acts or omissions innocent; and (3) offences of \"**absolute liability**\", as to which the prosecution does not ever need to prove any mental element, but need only prove the objective ingredients of the offences: He Kaw Teh v R \[1985\] HCA 43; (1985) 157 CLR 523.

In *Proudman v Dayman* \[1941\] HCA 28; (1941) 67 CLR 536, the High Court was concerned with a charge of permitting an unlicensed person to drive a motor vehicle on a road. It was argued that the prosecutor needed to prove either knowledge that the driver was unlicensed or indifference. It was held that proof of knowledge or indifference was unnecessary. Rich ACJ and Dixon J (as he then was) held, on the facts, that no question of an honest and reasonable mistaken belief arose in that case. Dixon J suggested that that would have been a defence. At 541 he said:

\"The burden of establishing honest and reasonable mistake is in the first place upon the defendant and he must make it appear that he had reasonable grounds for believing in the existence of a state of facts, which, if true, would take his act outside the operation of the enactment and that on those grounds he did so believe. The burden possibly may not finally rest upon him of satisfying the tribunal in case of doubt.\"

The question of what the Crown needs to prove as to the state of mind of a defendant in prosecutions under ss7, 8(1) and 9 has to be determined in accordance with the ordinary principles of statutory interpretation. There is a common law presumption that mens rea is an essential ingredient in every offence: Sherras v De Rutzen \[1985\] 1 QB 918 at 921; He Kaw Teh v R (above) per Gibbs CJ at 528. Mens rea was described in Sherras v De Rutzen by Wright J at 921 as \"an evil intention, or a knowledge of the wrongfulness of the act\". As Gibbs CJ made clear in He Kaw Teh at 529, one must consider the words of the statute creating the offence and the subject matter of the statute in order to determine whether the common law presumption has been displaced.

Intersction FVA act app and 94 sentencing act

Defence on brief -- on app for fvo -- agree to variation - interim variation. Finalsiation

More:

In broad terms, statutory offences fall into three categories if distinguished on the basis of the mental element involved in them.

\(1\) Offences which include as an element that the accused had guilty knowledge or intent.

\(2\) Offences which include as an element that the accused did not have an honest belief on reasonable grounds that the circumstances were such as to make his or her behaviour innocent. These are offences of strict liability.

\(3\) Offences which do not include either of the above elements. These are offences of absolute liability.

## Implied mental element in criminal offending

Taken from: **Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018\] TASSC 55 **

56. The general principles relating to the state of mind implied as an element of an offence such as this are well settled. Having regard to s 4(3) of the *Criminal Code*, the principles of criminal responsibility under the Code are not applicable to an offence punishable summarily, except as provided by [s 36](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s36.html) of the [*Acts Interpretation Act* 1931](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/), that is, where the act or omission constituting the summary offence also constitutes a crime punishable on indictment. In the case of an offence under [s 35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s35.html) of the Act,* *there is no cognate indictable crime. Accordingly, the principles of criminal responsibility will be determined in accordance with the common law: *Gow v Davies* [\[1992\] TASSC 8](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1992/8.html); [\[1992\] 1 Tas R 1.](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1992%5d%201%20Tas%20R%201)

<!-- -->

57. Under the common law, it is presumed that mens rea is required before a person can be held guilty of an offence: *He Kaw Teh v The Queen* [\[1985\] HCA 43](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1985/43.html); [(1985) 157 CLR 523.](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281985%29%20157%20CLR%20523) However, this presumption can be rebutted by express provision or necessary implication under the terms of the statute creating the offence. In *He Kaw Teh, *Gibbs CJ noted three considerations relevant to the question of whether the presumption has been displaced. They are the express words of the statute, the subject matter with which the statute deals and the question of whether displacement of the presumption will assist in the enforcement of the relevant provision.

<!-- -->

58. In the same case, Brennan J cited the following propositions from the judgment of the Privy Council in *Gammon Ltd v A-G of Hong Kong *[\[1984\] UKPC 17](http://www.bailii.org/uk/cases/UKPC/1984/1984_17.html); [\[1985\] 1 AC 1](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1985%5d%201%20AC%201) at 14:

\"(1) there is a presumption of law that mens rea is required before a person can be held guilty of a criminal offence;

\(2\) the presumption is particularly strong where the offence is \'truly criminal\' in character;

\(3\) the presumption applies to statutory offences, and can be displaced only if this is clearly or by necessary implication the effect of the statute;

\(4\) he only situation in which the presumption can be displaced is where the statute is concerned with an issue of social concern, and public safety is such an issue;

\(5\) even where a statute is concerned with such an issue, the presumption of mens rea stands unless it can also be shown that the creation of strict liability will be effective to promote the objects of the statute by encouraging greater vigilance to prevent the commission of the prohibited act.\"

His Honour went on further to comment on these propositions as follows:

\"The first three propositions correctly emphasise the strength which contemporary authority gives to the presumption that mens rea is an essential element of an offence. The fourth proposition, if I may say so with respect, seems to be too categorical an approach to what is, after all, a question of statutory interpretation. It is not possible to decide that mens rea can be excluded only where the subject matter answers a given description (even so general a description as \'an issue of social concern\'), without regard to the whole of the statutory context.\"

### Defence of involountary action:

1.  The principles applicable to the sort of defence raised by the applicant were set out by Mason CJ, Brennan and McHugh JJ in *R v Falconer *[\[1990\] HCA 49](http://www7.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/49.html); [(1990) 171 CLR 30](http://www7.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281990%29%20171%20CLR%2030) at 40-41, as follows:

*\"The presumption that the acts of a person, apparently conscious, are willed or voluntary is an inference of fact and, as a matter of fact, there must be good grounds for refusing to draw the inference. Generally speaking, grounds for refusing to draw the inference appear only when there are grounds for believing that the actor is unable to control his actions. Although the prosecution bears the ultimate onus of proving beyond reasonable doubt that an act which is an element of an offence charged was a willed act or, at common law, was done voluntarily, \... the prosecution may rely on the inference that an act done by an apparently conscious actor is willed or voluntary to discharge that onus unless there are grounds for believing that the accused was unable to control that act.\" \[Case reference omitted.\]*

## Subjective knowledge as a mental element

I.e. knowing or believing property to be stolen for receive or possess. Belief is enough, need not be proven fact.

Voluntary intoxication can undermine and is relevant to the question of subjective knowledge.

Under the code the court refers to knowledge in two ways: Knowledge is referred to knowledge of an actual fact. Other times it refers to a belief. This belief dimension that is considered in stolen property as the mental element (subjective knowledge -any evidence of intoxication can be relied upon to deny subjective knowledge (*Weiderman*)). Certainty is not needed, just a real belief *Quillerat* & *Martin*

***Quillerat* \[1962\] Tas SR (NC 16)**

This case makes it clear that specific knowledge is a subjective test. One need only believe, knowledge to the extent of certainty is not necessary. Closing ones mind to knowledge is not counted as knowledge, but it may be evidence of it.

***Martin* Serial No 58/1980**

Belief is enough to constitute. It need not be knowledge of an actual certainty, but one needs a real belief that the goods are stolen

Any evidence of intoxication can be relied upon to deny subjective knowledge (*in Attorney General's Reference No 1 of 1996(Weiderman)* )).

# Pleading 

## Double jeopardy

**Barnes v Omant \[2019\] TASSC 38 (30 August 2019)**

Pros laid a fraud charge that had no amount \$ stipulated and was a rolled up in a single charge.. Before hearing they notified defence of intent to adjourn sine die and lay fresh indictable complaint with proper particularisation of 9 charges.

At hearing day the matter was listed. Pross sought to adjourn the hearing saying It would never go ahead. Defence objected on basis of finality. Pross argued they would be screwed on double jeopardy if the summary charge was dismissed. Magi denied adjournment and as pross had no evidence., it was dismissed.

They appealed saying it was unjust not to grant adjournment. Crown failed.

***Lusted v Jenkins* \[2010\] TASMC 12**

7\. The principle of double jeopardy is based on the notion that a person should not be prosecuted twice for the same crime. The rational for the rule was stated in the judgment of the majority of the High Court in *Pearce v The Queen* (1994) 194 CLR 610 in a passage endorsed from *Green v United States* [\[1957\] USSC 146:](http://www.worldlii.org/us/cases/federal/USSC/1957/146.html)

There is no statutory authority for withdrawal of a complaint in Tasmania. The nature of the decision made by this court to dismiss the first complaint was, consequently, a determination of nothing other than to allow a substituted complaint. Allowing the prosecution of the second complaint ... does not, in my view, offend any of the principles ... or rationale for the rule.

... the conclusion that the complaint is bad for duplicity does not oblige the court to dismiss the complaint. The alternative courses, including putting the prosecution to an election to remove any embarrassment, are referred to by Kirby J in *Walsh v Tattersall* at 110 and reflected in [s29(5).](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/s29.html) Further, if an election had been compelled on the first complaint it is impossible to determine which facts, if proved, put the defendant in jeopardy of conviction. Even if it were possible that the first complaint may have survived in some more limited form I have concluded that Mr Jenkins cannot take advantage of the dismissal of a complaint that was bad for duplicity as an answer to the later complaint.

## Means of resolution:

### Subsume and dismiss like elements:

**Murray v Tasmania Police \[2022\] TASSC 1 (24 January 2022)**

Para \[16\]

The applicant accepted the magistrate was entitled to do what he did, if it was open to construe the reckless driving element of the aggravated evading police charge as involving the same conduct that constituted the reckless driving charge. The magistrate would have been so entitled because of the rule or principle that prohibits \'double punishment\'. To the extent to which two offences contain common elements, it is wrong to punish an offender twice for the commission of the elements that are common: see *Pearce v The Queen *[\[1998\] HCA 57](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1998/57.html); [(1998) 194 CLR 610](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281998%29%20194%20CLR%20610) per McHugh, Hayne and Callinan JJ, *Wood v Major *[\[1992\] TASSC 28](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1992/28.html); [(1992) 3 Tas R 249](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281992%29%203%20Tas%20R%20249) at 254, *R v Audino* VSCA \[2007\] 318[\[2007\] VSCA 318](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2007/318.html); , [180 A Crim R 371](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=180%20A%20Crim%20R%20371) and *R v Healey* [\[2008\] VSCA 132](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2008/132.html), [186 A Crim R 433](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=186%20A%20Crim%20R%20433) at [\[27\]](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2008/132.html#para27)- [\[34\]](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2008/132.html#para34).
