- Would that give them experience as to how much they could/ could not drink?

You knew you must have been over the limit but you chose to drive anyway?

- Ask about previous disqualifications? Ask about the arrangements they used then -- how come they managed to be without it before?

Managed to keep job before how come not now?

The last time you were busted you must have known you were over the limit -- or close -- and you decided to run the risk. This is the 3^rd^ occasions you have just decided to run the risk?

What can you say to the court to convince his honour that you would comply with the restricted licence -- that you would run the risk again?

Given prior restrictions and convictions s- defendant would have known the hardship they would likely face before getting behind the wheel yet did it anyway?

**Submission**

The public interest will not be served by the granting of a restricted licence. The public have a right to be protected from repeat offenders. This is the very purpose of the RSDA -- they have a right to be protected from individual who are willing to risk it, and to risk their safety?

The defendant said they did not put their mind to it -- that's illogical -- they have been convicted before -- given his previous experiences with related matters he must have considered that he would be affected by consuming that amount.

To continue to drive he demonstrates a disregard for the law of the state and the safety of other rd users. He ought to have known there would be likely consequences for such action.

3^rd^ breach - At what stage are the public to be assured that their safety will be considered?

**Restricted Licence**

*R v Stockman*

Public Interest - When determining whether or not to grant a restricted licence to a person a magistrate should take into account the following matters;

- Is it in the public interest to grant the applicant a restricted licence?

- Is the applicant likely to commit further offences whilst the holder of a restricted licence?

- The gravity of the offence for which the applicant was disqualified.

- The circumstances surrounding the commission of the offence.

- Will the applicant be likely to be source of danger on the road whilst driving pursuant to a restricted licence?

- The public interest in seeing disqualification orders given full force in acting as a general and/o specific deterrent.

***Davies v Deverell (1992) 1 Tas R 214***

- Held, that except in the clearest cases the public interest requires that evidence in support of applications for such licences is properly tested in cross-examination.

Insofar as the public interest was concerned, the respondent's record of prior convictions and the circumstances surrounding the commission of the offences to which those convictions related were of particular significance.

There is a positive onus cast upon an applicant for a restricted licence to satisfy a magistrate that the exercise of the powers would not be contrary to the public interest.

**Morton v Scott \[2016\] TASSC 51** General and personal deterrence should have been at the forefront of the learned magistrate\'s mind. 

# Lane v Black \[2013\] TASMC 3 (17 January 2013)

> 17: Mr Black said that *"\... If I am unable to drive I will not be able to obtain alternative employment."* While this may be so, Mr Black did not give any evidence upon which that conclusion or opinion was based. Mr Black did not say that he had consulted with any job agency to assess the labour market for someone with his skills. There was no evidence that Mr Black had made any enquiries whatsoever in relation to alternative employment. Nor was Mr Black asked inchief or under cross-examination, any questions about this assertion. Much of the picture in relation to alternative employment and the applicant's financial position was left to be inferred instead of being the subject of precise evidence.

**Tasmanian RL may not be valid interstate.**

*Vanessa Limbrick and In the matter of an Application for a Restricted Driver Licence *[\[2015\] TASMC 5](https://aus01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.austlii.edu.au%2Fcgi-bin%2Fviewdoc%2Fau%2Fcases%2Ftas%2FTASMC%2F2015%2F5.html&data=05%7C02%7Cthomas.hogan%40police.tas.gov.au%7C2a4ec2d563ea4258698308de376c76e2%7Ced13a8afa76343dcb114891492b38482%7C0%7C0%7C639009136169704716%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=KohO94O62XeUvIHmmxe73wrwCPg%2FZRV1TGpZ4I696tI%3D&reserved=0)

# Misuse of drugs Act

### Section 29. 

Power to search a person where there is a prescribed belief without warrant. S. 29(5) and 30 are pretty broad power up to and including a strip search (but they must be afforded privacy)

**29(1)**

***"Prescribed belief"*** means a reasonable belief that a controlled substance or other thing in relation to which an offence under this Act has been committed is --

(a) in the possession of a person in any place; or

(b) on or in a conveyance in any place; or

(c) on an animal in any place.

Where such belief police can search and detain person or conveyance. Cannot enter private premises to search UNLESS they form the belief after having lawfully entered the premises per s 29(2A).

### Prescribed belief

**Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022) Brett J**

\[28\] *"A belief is something more than mere suspicion, but less than actual knowledge based on proof. There must be grounds for the belief, but certainty and proof are not required."*

This point was made by the High Court in *George v Rockett *[\[1990\] HCA 26](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/26.html); [(1990) 170 CLR 104](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281990%29%20170%20CLR%20104) at 116"

> *\"The objective circumstances sufficient to show a reason to believe something need to point more clearly to the subject matter of the belief, but that is not to say that the objective circumstances must establish on the balance of probabilities that the subject matter in fact occurred or exists: the assent of belief is given on more slender evidence than proof. Belief is an inclination of the mind towards assenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition and the grounds which can reasonably induce that inclination of the mind may, depending on the circumstances, leave something to surmise or conjecture.\"*

\[29\] *Further, the requirement that the belief be reasonable means that it must be based on reasonable grounds. This question is to be judged by the Court by applying an objective test: Samoukovic v Brown [\[1993\] TASSC 78](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1993/78.html), per Zeeman J.*

In ***Hodges***, the basis of the officer's belief was:

- **Intel search showing defendant had drug history.**

- **Demeanour**

- **physical presentation of the defendant -- fidgety, dilated pupils, dry mouth**

Great para at \[35\]: *"Further, I am satisfied that the basis of the belief as articulated by Constable Whelan, which include her initial suspicions, her observations of the physical indicia displayed by the accused, and the information obtained from the police database during the computer search, provided objectively reasonable grounds for her belief. In applying *![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}*** [s 29](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/moda2001184/s29.html) ***![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}*, it should be borne in mind that the provision is intended to provide police with powers, including with respect to the search of vehicles, in operational circumstances in which there may be insufficient time or opportunity to obtain a warrant. The constraint on the exercise of those powers is the possession of a prescribed belief. Because it is a power which it is intended to be used in operational circumstances, the formation of the prescribed belief is likely to be in the type of circumstances described by Constable Whelan, that is, a belief which derives from the incremental accumulation of a number of discrete pieces of information, gathered and mentally processed quickly, eventually coalescing in the relevant belief. Provided the belief is actually held, a further constraint on the exercise of the powers is that it must be held on objectively reasonable grounds, but an assessment of objective reasonableness must take account of the circumstances in which the belief is formed. Having regard to all of the circumstances, I am satisfied that Constable Whelan believed that there was a controlled substance in the vehicle, and this belief was based on objectively reasonable grounds."*

***Orr v Johnston \[2014\] TASMC 5 (27 February 2014)***

Basis of belief was based on the smell, the defendant's appearance and his past knowledge of the defendant. Accepted by the TASSC.

**Admission as to drug without analysis**

Also, accepted that admission of defendant that marijuana was as such, was enough to proive what was found was in fact marijuana despite no analysis. Def admitted it was the drug, that it was his and that he had smoked it earlier in the day and been using it on a regular basis for some time.

Section 31. Power to demand source of supply

## Evidentiary certificate provision:

Analysis certificate of drug test admissible under 177A of Evidence Act -- prima facie evidence. However, must be served on defence 14 days prior to a hearing. Does not extend to RSAD Act as that specifically excludes its operation (S. 29A).

## Quantity:

## Possession amount

Possession of minute barely detectable amount not sufficient. If microscope only then not enough. At very least, must be observable with naked eye. **Williams v R \[1978\] HCA 49**

## Possession 

Extended definition under the Act for drugs ONLY (does not extend to items used for drugs like a pipe). Onus is on defendant. See section 3(3)

## Detection times for oral samples:

**From:** Grist, Miriam (FSST)\
**Sent:** Wednesday, 23 January 2019 15:45\
**To:** Lyons, Jason (DPEM)\
**Subject:** RE: Oral Fluid vs Blood

 

Hi Jason,

 

The detection time of drugs in oral fluid is less than that in blood, so that it is more reflective of recent use. So the time frame for detection is shorter. The following are general guidelines, and there may be some small exceptions to the rule.

 

Detection times for common drugs of abuse in oral fluid:

 

·        Cannabis -- 24 hours

·        Methylamphetamine -- 3 days

·        Amphetamine -- 72 hours

·        MDMA -- 3 days

·        Morphine -- 7 days

·        Heroin -- 2 days

 

Thanks,

Miriam

Ice pipe defined in *Poisons act* section 86C

***ice pipe*** means a device that --

\(a\) is capable of being used, or is intended to be used, for the purpose of administering a controlled drug into the body of a person by the drawing or inhaling of smoke, or vapour or fumes, resulting from the heating or burning of the controlled drug in a crystal or powder form; or

\(b\) is intended to be used as such a device but requires adjustment, modification or addition before it is so used; or

\(c\) is of a prescribed class, type or description;

### Section 29 Prescribed belief 

### Section 23 Administration of 

#### Meaning of "designed to be used"

### Establishing used or designed to be used:

**Can get evidence directly from cops that they are used that way... BUT (see below)**

***Thomas v Frankcombe, Turton, Medwin and Pears \[2010\] TASMC 6 (16 July 2010)***

*\[3\] "(b) Evidence was given by Sergeant Flude, a police officer involved in seizing the relevant material. His evidence included a description of how the smoking devices, otherwise known by the colloquial name \"bong\", could be used for the purposes of smoking cannabis. His evidence was to the effect that bongs were regularly used by the users of cannabis for that purpose. His evidence also related to how the other items tendered in evidence were also regularly used by cannabis smokers for the smoking administration or taking of cannabis.*

*Such evidence was admitted without objection and was not challenged. I note that such evidence is regularly permitted by courts as direct evidence of the actual observations and experience of the officer concerned, rather than as opinion evidence (see Barker [(1988) 34 A Crim R 141).](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281988%29%2034%20A%20Crim%20R%20141)*

*It was clear from the evidence of Sergeant Flude, and seems obvious in any event from my examination of the exhibits, that the smoking devices in question could be used to smoke any substance capable of being inhaled as smoke, whether that substance be legal or illegal. Again that matter during the course of argument did not seem to be the subject of dispute."*

**Need more than just cop...**

*12. I conclude, therefore, that *![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}*** [section 23](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/moda2001184/s23.html) ***![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}* (a) relates only to items which are or have been actually used in connection with the smoking of cannabis or alternatively, have been designed for such use in the sense that they have manufactured or made or are of a design for use in connection with the smoking of cannabis.*

*13. It follows, therefore, that whilst it may not be necessary to go as far as Mr Richardson suggests, by proving the purpose for which the item was designed only by evidence as to what was in the mind of the actual designer when he designed the relevant item, there must at least be some evidence that does permit an inference to be drawn that the item was designed or manufactured for the relevant purpose. I do note that, in my view, it would not be necessary to prove that the purpose in question was the sole or even the principle purpose, of such design or manufacture, provided that the prohibited purpose was among the purposes of the design or manufacture of the item.*

*14. In this case, the evidence permits me only to conclude that smoking devices of the nature of those in evidence, can be and in practice often are, used for the smoking of cannabis. However, as Gavin Duffy J noted in Tysoe v Bucknall the fact that the items are sometimes used in this way does not permit an inference that the item in question was designed or constructed for the purpose of such use. As His Honour said \"things are often used for purposes for which they were not originally designed\". In this regard, Mr Richardson made the point in both cross examination and submissions, that items such as scissors, tobacco paper, cigarette lighters and scales are often used in connection with the administration of controlled drugs, but it would be ludicrous to suggest that those items were designed or manufactured for that purpose. I agree with that submission.*

*15. There is simply no other evidence which touches the question of the purpose for which these items were designed or manufactured. In my view, incidental matters, such as the \"cannabis leaf\" motifs do not assist in respect of this issue.*

# Poisons Act

The reference in the Tas Poison Act to the "Poison list" is reference to the Cth' Therapeutic Goods (Poisons Standard---February 2024) Instrument 2024. It is adopted by section 14 of the Act .

There is a variation to the uniform standard in relation to cannabis under the [**Poisons (Application of Uniform Standard) Order 2021**](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/sr-2021-059)

As discussed and for general reference.

Charge of 'Possess Restricted Substance' pursuant to Section 36 of the Poisons Act 1971 and;

Charge of 'Possess Narcotic Substance' pursuant to Section 48 of the Poisons Act 1971

Section 3 -- Interpretation: ***restricted substance*** means a substance that is, for the time being, specified in Schedule 4 to the Poisons List; and ***narcotic substance*** means a substance that is, for the time being, specified in Schedule 8 to the Poisons List;

The poisons list is governed by Federal Legislation - *[Therapeutic Goods (Poisons Standard---February 2023) Instrument 2023](https://aus01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.legislation.gov.au%2FDetails%2FF2023L00067%2FRepeals&data=05%7C01%7Cthomas.hogan%40police.tas.gov.au%7C9993f1d46a8445da4e2a08db50fcee90%7Ced13a8afa76343dcb114891492b38482%7C0%7C0%7C638192819479596769%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=E901cfMOCKTSwoGuagzpqDZAuHZx%2BgYR0VqJxIxRlZ8%3D&reserved=0) --* and updated February every year. In this legislation is found Schedules 1-10 -- being the poisons list.

Of note, most drugs located in a search are either going to be Schedule 4, 8 or a substance found in schedule 1 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001.

Drugs found in Schedule 4 -- the appropriate charge is ***Possess restricted substance -- s36 Poisons Act 1971***

Drugs found in Schedule 8 (if not in [Schedule 1 MODA 2001](https://aus01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.legislation.tas.gov.au%2Fview%2Fhtml%2Finforce%2Fcurrent%2Fact-2001-094%23JS1%40EN&data=05%7C01%7Cthomas.hogan%40police.tas.gov.au%7C9993f1d46a8445da4e2a08db50fcee90%7Ced13a8afa76343dcb114891492b38482%7C0%7C0%7C638192819479596769%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=EXZmb6sD9eIcWIlJLASVeQ0tli7YusGYjJoJazuss6c%3D&reserved=0)) -- the appropriate charge is ***Possess narcotic substance -- s48 Poisons Act 1971***

Obviously if the substance is found in Schedule 1 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 2001 the regular ***possess controlled plant product*** or ***possess controlled drug*** is applicable.

# Youth Justice Act 1997

Psychosocial immaturity and the prefrontal cortex is underdeveloped. That part that leads to impulse control and planning and decision making

Specifically, that those regions associated with emotions and rewards become increasingly active at a time when the brain's control centre, the prefrontal cortex, remains unable to deploy the skills required for weighing complex decisions and regulating behavioural and emotional responses ... Models therefore suggest that risk-taking behaviour during adolescence is the result of the incongruent growth of two essential brain systems, one that matures earlier and acts as a driver for these behaviours, and one that matures later and acts to inhibit them

... Thus, during adolescence emotions and the motivation to achieve rewards increase but the skills that allow young people to exercise impulse control and evaluate risks and rewards are not yet fully developed, potentially resulting in illegal or dangerous behaviours.

Under 10 there is no criminal responsibility. Between 10 and under 14 it is a rebuttable presumption that they do not understand that their conduct is morally wrong.

Youth more likely to commit crimes against property than persons. That includes aggravated burglary. Though, anecdotally that seems to be increasing. Larger proportion of physical assaults being committed.

- Males 5x6 times more likely to commit offences than females.

- Youth are less likely to offend than adults, but are more likely to reoffend.

- 50% of all youth crime in Tasmania is committed by just 50 offenders.

2024 offence outcomes.

  --------------------------------------------------------------------
  Informal Caution                109                    28%
  ---------------------- ---------------------- ----------------------
  Formal Caution                   65                    17%

  Community Conference             36                     9%

  Court                           167                    44%

  Not Proceeded With               4                      1%

                                                
  --------------------------------------------------------------------

About 50% (45-55 historically) of offences are met with diversion from the court.

## Restorative Justice:

Restorative justice is a facilitated process for people harmed by crime and wrongdoing to talk about the impact of the harm, and for offenders to take responsibility and help repair the damage, with a focus on healing and prevention rather than just punishment.

Legislative preference is to divert where possible. Bars to that are a refusal to accept guilt, or the nature or recurrence of offending make it inappropriate.

Youth must consent to a formal caution or a conference

## Limitation on Joint Charges:

**27.**   **Limitation on joint charge**

[A youth who is under 15 years of age may not be jointly charged with an adult.]{.mark}

**28.**   **Limitation on power of Court to hear joint charge**

(1)  The Court is **not** entitled to hear and determine a charge against a youth if --

[(a) the youth is 15 or more years old; and]{.mark}

[(b) the youth is charged jointly with an adult; and]{.mark}

[(c) the youth and the adult **[both plead not guilty]{.underline}** to the charge.]{.mark}

(2)  If a youth is jointly charged with an adult and the adult or the youth, or both, **plead guilty** to the charge, the Court must hear and determine the charge against the youth **unless** the prosecutor objects.

(3)  If the prosecutor makes an objection under [subsection (2)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1997-081#GS28@Gs2@EN) , the Court may hear and determine the charge against the youth or determine that the charge is to be transferred to the Supreme Court under [Part VII of the ](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#HPVII@EN)[Justices Act 1959](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077) .

## Prescribed offences:

These offences are found in the interpretation section of the Act. Qualified by the age of the offender. Note: Despite the wording used in each category of ***prescribed offences*** *(in respect of a youth who **is** less than*...), suggesting that the examination of the age of the offender should be contemporaneous with their presentation in court, it should be read in conjunction with the definition of Youth in the Act, as well. ***Youth*** considers the age at time of offending. The two definitions need to be read together.

## Responsible adult:

Section 3 -- interpretation section.

***responsible adult***, in relation to a youth, means a person who --

\(a\) is a ***nominated adult*** in relation to the youth; or

\(b\) if there is no nominated adult in relation to the youth, a justice of the peace or a person, or a member of a class of persons, approved by the Secretary;

***nominated adult***, in relation to a youth, means an adult who --

\(a\) has had a close association with the youth or has been counselling, advising or aiding the youth; and

\(b\) has not been charged with the offence in respect of which the youth has been taken into custody or is not suspected, by a police officer on reasonable grounds, of being directly or indirectly involved in the commission of that offence; and

\(c\) has been nominated by the youth;

## Penalty matrix

### Pre-Court Diversion 

Informal caution (Youth Caution Action Notice \[YCAN\]

- Not available for assaults.

Formal Caution (administered by a police officer)

Facilitated by Taspol. Have certain powers.

Community conference The Secretary of Communities Tasmania convenes a conference with an administrator, police officer, the youth / carer and the victims can elect to be involved.

COURT:

Pre-senetnce reports provide contextual information of the anticedents and circumstances of the youth to assist the magistrate

Deferal of sentence for up to 12 months

Court ordered community conference on a finding of gui;lt

## Youth Sentencing:

**Dismissal**

Dismissal is the least severe sentencing option for young offenders sentenced under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas). Dismissal means that a youth is found guilty, but the charge is dismissed and no other sentence is ordered. 453 A conviction cannot be recorded. 452F

**Reprimand**

A reprimand means that a youth is found guilty and the charge is dismissed and the youth is reprimanded. 455 This is a formal warning by the court. A conviction cannot be recorded.

**Dismissal with undertaking to be of good behaviour**

A dismissal with an undertaking to be of good behaviour is a form of conditional unsupervised release. An undertaking to be of good behaviour is an agreement by a youth

**Release and adjournment with conditions**

Under this sanction, the youth is released and sentencing is postponed for a nominated period (no more than 12 months) on conditions set out by the court. be recorded. A conviction cannot. At the conclusion of the adjournment with conditions, if the youth does everything required by the conditions, the youth is discharged from the order. If the youth does not do everything required under the conditions, the youth may have to return to court and the court may impose a new order.

**Fine**

**Probation order**

Young people on probation orders must report to youth justice 469F workers as required. 472 470F 471 The court can attach special conditions, such as attending school or other educational programs, counselling or treatment. The youth may also be required to abstain from drinking alcohol or using controlled substances, submitting to testing for controlled substances or alcohol, to live at a specified address, and/or comply with a curfew.

**Community service order**

**Detention as a LAST resort.**

General deterrence ok for serious offending.

The Supreme Court has recognised that there are tensions in the aims and purposes of sentencing under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) that can be difficult to reconcile, particularly in relation to serious offending. 390F 391 It has been consistently accepted that while rehabilitation is often a predominant factor, both general and personal deterrence can have a role to play, particularly in relation to serious offending. 392 For example, in DPP v NOP, 391F 393 392F a case involving a conviction for rape, Evan J wrote that 'whilst rehabilitation is ordinarily the primary consideration for youthful offenders, this does not mean that the Court must disregard the needs of general deterrence and denunciation'. 393F 3

Most recently, in Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP, of the Court of Criminal Appeal in TAP v Tasmania: 399 398F 398 Estcourt J quoted the comments 397F There is no need to set out those provisions \[in the Youth Justice Act\]. Those relevant to sentencing restate the relevant principles concerning the sentencing of juvenile offenders which have long been established by the courts. The principles are that rehabilitation of the offender is always an important, if not the dominant consideration, and that any sentence should be tailored with greater emphasis on the welfare of the youth; the emphasis on rehabilitation is consistent with the broader sentencing goal of community protection ... It is true that the justification for the principles concerning the sentencing of youthful offenders is that such offenders are not able to appreciate the nature and extent of their criminality. They are most likely to make ill-considered and immature decisions. ... the importance of rehabilitation of a youthful offender is usually far more important than general deterrence, but that there are cases in which just punishment and general deterrence become at least equally important. 400

## **Special Witness Applications** --

Need to include more information to support these. We should not simply rely on psych reports, we should add FVMS history, supporting affadavits etc. The Southern Prosecution website has a good aide-memoir.

See rule 5 of the *Justices Rules* -- allows reference to Criminal Rules. See rule 41A of the *Criminal Rules 2006*:

**5.**   **Procedure for matter not provided for**

In any matter not otherwise provided for by these rules of court, the practice of the Supreme Court is to be followed.

**41A.**   **Applications under [Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2023-12-17/act-2001-079)**

[https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2007-11-14/sr-2007-103](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2007-11-14/sr-2007-103#GS5@EN)For the purposes of a preliminary hearing under , a judge may inform himself or herself in any way he or she thinks fit and may accept statements and assurances of the prosecutor without hearing evidence from the child or person who is giving, or is to give, evidence.

Special Witness Applications- Information for Investigating Police

What needs to be established?

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the person is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner; or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the

proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers relevant, the person is likely ---

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

(See s8 Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2401)

The onus of proof is on us, but its only balance of probabilities because we only need to establish that its likely.

Hearsay Evidence

You can call hearsay evidence, see s75 Evidence Act (Tas); these are interlocutory proceedings.

What evidence is there of the following stress symptoms: (this can be hearsay):

• crying

• Muscle tension

• Pains in chest

• Stomach/abdominal pain

• Muscle spasms or nervous tics

• Unexplained rashes or skin

irritations

• Pounding\' or \'racing\' heart

• Sweaty palms

• Sweating when not physically

active

• \'Butterflies\' in stomach

• Indigestion and \'the gurgles\'

• Diarrhea

• Unable to sleep or excessive

sleep

• Shortness of breath

• Holding breath

Post Traumatic Stress:

• Intrusive, upsetting memories of

the event

• Flashbacks (acting or feeling like

the event is happening again)

• Nightmares (either of the event

or of other frightening things)

• Feelings of intense distress when

reminded of the trauma

• Intense physical reactions to

reminders of the event (e.g,

pounding heart, rapid breathing, nausea, muscle tension,

sweating)

What evidence is there of there being too intimidated to give evidence satisfactorily in presence of defendant (this can be hearsay)? Including any of the following:

• Refusal to make statement, give

evidence, cooperate with police for fear of reprisal ect

• Expression of fear of defendant;

Uncontrolled crying;

**Special Witness Applications: Information for Medical Practitioners**

Normally when witnesses give evidence in court it is in the presence of the defendant. The witness must retell their evidence of the offence in the witness box, within 2 to 3 metres of the defendant and in their full view. The police can apply for the witness to give evidence by video from a remote room but we need to satisfy the court of the following matters;

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the Person is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner;

Or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers

relevant, the person is likely

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

To satisfy the court of these matters we have requested that you prepare a report. It is necessary for you to cover the following matters in your report:

1.) What are your qualifications and experience?

2.) What is the subject matter of the witness\' evidence?

3.) What if any disability/ disorder (including stress disorders) does the witness

suffer? Please detail all relevant symptoms and how you have reached this

diagnosis. Please detail any treatment.

4.) Based on the witness\'s disability/ disorder or current stresses and the evidence

that they will give, please give your opinion to whether they would be able to

i\) Give evidence in the presence of the defendant without suffering

severe emotional trauma.

OR

ii\) Give evidence in the presence of the defendant coherently without

crying, shaking, losing concentration and or becoming too upset to give evidence Satisfactorily.

Please give as much detail as possible to support your opinion.

**Special Witness Applications- Information for Prosecutors**

What needs to be established?

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the person is, or is likely to b; unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner; or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the

proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers relevant, the person is likely ---

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

(See s8 Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 200.1)

The onus of proof is on prosecution, but its only balance of probabilities because we only need to establish that its likely.

Hearsay Evidence

You can call hearsay evidence, see s75 Evidence Act; these are! interlocutory proceedings.

You should consider calling recent complaint evidence from Police Officer\'s who took the victim\'s statements to establish distress and intimidation and difficulty retelling the story ect.

Expert Evidence

To establish intellectual, mental or physical disability and or that a person is likely to

suffer severe emotional trauma, you may need to call expert evidence, consider whether you need a psychologist or physiatrist, a general practitioner may be unable to give

evidence from specialised knowledge in the area if your alleging psychiatric disorder see s79 Evidence Act.

Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act is beneficial legislation which is intended to protect vulnerable witnesses and to override any common !awl right to confront

accuser ect (See A v Fitz \[2003\] TASSC 32 for a consideration of the purpose of the legislation). The rights of witnesses have always needed to be balanced against the rights of accused. See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165.

See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165 at pare 34 where J Redlich when assessing any

prejudice to defence outlines \"there is the full opportunity to observe the demeanour of the witness on a large screen with a \"close up\" view of the witness\' head and upper body\', See also DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15 at para 8.

Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276 is not authority for an unimpeachable right to confront accuser, the prosecutor in that case simply did not make the appropriate application pursuant to the appropriate legislation.

## *Evidence (Children and Special Witness) Act* notes:

### Key definitions 

***Affected child:***

- Under 18

- Giving evidence in FV matters or child sex offences

- Victim of 8(1) or 35(3) POA

***Affected person:***

- Is 18 but under 18 when victim of child sex offences.

- A prescribed witness under s 7J

***Prescribed proceedings/ offences:***

- FV offences

- Applications concerning PFVO and FVO's

- 8(1) or 35(3) POA

- Child sex offences

- Number of indictable matters under purview of the DPP

***Prescribed witness:***

- Witness declared as such under s 7J

***Specified proceedings - concerning specified offences.***

- Section 8(1A)(a) or 35(3) of the Police Offences Act 1935

***Witness intermediary order***

- Order made under s 7J

### Relevant Sections:

**Section 4:**

In **[ANY]{.underline}** proceeding concerning a child -- can have an approved support person near-by. Approved by court. Can't be another witness.

**Section 5:**

In ***prescribed*** or ***specified*** hearing the prior statement (written or recorded) of *affected child* or *prescribed witness* can go in as long as disclosed and available for CX

**Section 5A:**

A prior audio/ visual statement taken by police of a child (in ANY proceeding), a *special witness, a victim of 35(3)* or a victim of FV can be tendered as part or ALL of EIC.

**Section 6B:**

Where there is no section 7 order in place, an *affected child* or *prescribed witness* in a *prescribed proceeding* or a *specified proceeding* is to be given by remote a/v/ link.

### Intermediaries:

Only applies to ***Specified** **Proceedings***/ offences!

**Section 7I:**

Assessment report can be sought for a child witness and/or child victim OR an adult witness/ victim with suspected identified communication need in a in a *specified proceeding.*

**Section 7J:**

Seek an order under 7J -- for the use of a witness intermediary in these proceedings. This makes the relevant witness a "*prescribed*" witness.

**Section 7K:**

Ground rules hearing -- adopt recommendations of the report.

If an assessment report has been made in respect of the *prescribed witness*, in making a direction under this section the judge is to consider any matters mentioned in that report.

**Section 8A:**

Defendant can't CX in a prescribed proceeding.

### Order of events:

**At earliest opportunity:**

Petition court to consider seeking an assessment report under Section 7I and adj for that purpose to a later "ground rules hearing" under Section 7K

***Ground Rules hearing:***

Once report obtained, Petition court to formally order that a witness intermediary be used in respect o a witness in a specified proceeding under Section 7J. The result of this is that the witness becomes a "*prescribed witness*" for the purposes of the *Act*.

Petition the court to adopt the recommendations of the report (subject to anything silly in it).

Identify and advise the court of any other statutory presumption that arises by virtue of the witness becoming a *prescribed witness* (i.e. Section 5 -- prior statement as EIC).

### DPP's notes:

- Eligible Witnesses, S7I

<!-- -->

- All children (a person under the age of 18 years) who are giving evidence in relation to the specified offence. Applies equally to child complainants and child witnesses

- Adults (a person who has attained the age of 18 years) with a communication need who are giving evidence in relation to a specified offence

- Does not apply to defendants

<!-- -->

- Communication need, s 7F

<!-- -->

- A witness who is to give evidence in a specified proceeding is taken to have a communication need if the *quality or clarity* of evidence given by the witness may be significantly diminished by the witness's ability to *understand, process or express information-* See s7F(2) and (3) for more info.

### Assessment Reports

- S7I A Judge **is** to make an order than an assessment report be prepared and provided to the judge by a witness intermediary in respect of a witness in a specified proceeding if that witness is child witness/adult with communication need (see definition in previous slide)

- In determining whether to order a report the Judge may inform themselves on any matter in any manner that they think fit (7L)

- Order for assessment to be made as early as practicable before the witness may be required to give evidence. Can take 4-6 weeks for the assessment

- This requires an assessment report by intermediary which will need to be organised through the Child Abuse Royal Commission Response Unit.

<!-- -->

- Note under s7I(3) there is some discretion re ordering the assessment in instances where the Judge is satisfied that;

- \(i\) it is unnecessary or inappropriate to make the order, or

- \(ii\) the witness does not wish the order to be made

- \(iii\) the making of the order would be contrary to the interests of justice.

- Examples; 17 year old child, articulate, educated, has given evidence before, have met with them and no communication need is apparent.

### What happens after the assessment?

- A judge is to make an order that a witness intermediary will be used if, having considered the assessment report, they are satisfied the use of the intermediary will assist the proceeding.

- Once the order is made, the Judge is to order a "ground rules hearing" to discuss communication and other related needs identified in the assessment report. Prosecutor/defence lawyer and intermediary are to attend (witness need not attend). The judge will make directions that will govern how the proceedings will be conducted. Any directions will be guided by the assessment report and may include;

<!-- -->

- Directions about how the witness will be questioned/ how long the witness will be questioned/ the types of questions that can be asked/ order that prosecutor/defence provide a list of questions to the intermediary in advance of the hearing/ directions as to the use of plans, models, body maps or other aids to help communicate a question or answer

### Relevant Legislation

Evidence Act 2001-

s12 Competency; sworn and unsworn evidence

S18 Compellability of child/parent

s26 Court\'s control over questioning

s41 Improper Questions

Evidence (Child and Special Witnesses) Act 2001

Justices Rules r5

Criminal Rules 2006-

r41A

**Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001;**

S9(1); In any prescribed proceeding (or specified proceeding) in which the prosecutor who is to call the witness requires approval under s4 (support person), s6A (special hearing- child) or 8 (special witness), the prosecutor or party calling the witness is to apply for a preliminary hearing to deal with the matters before the hearing is commenced.

S9(2); A preliminary hearing in respect of those proceedings may be provided for by the Rules of Court in which that proceeding is being brought.

**Justices Rules**

Rule 5 provides that any matter not provided for by those rules, the practice of the Supreme Court is to be followed.

- **Criminal Rules 2006**

- Rule 41A of the Criminal Rules 2006 provides;

[41A. Applications under Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]{.underline}

***For the purposes of a preliminary hearing under section 9(2) of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 , a judge may inform himself or herself in any way he or she thinks fit and may accept statements and assurances of the prosecutor without hearing evidence from the child or person who is giving, or is to give, evidence***

### What to put before the Magistrate

- File an application seeking the particular support/s

- You can get instructions directly from the witness/parents and make representations to the Magistrate

- Support person; it can not be a witness in the proceeding. WAS officers are trained in what they can and cant do. WAS officers available for certain matters.

- Admission of prior statement; Submissions to the effect that; (e.g.)

<!-- -->

- The Principles in s3 of the Act relating to child witnesses are intended to reduce the child's distress/trauma when giving evidence

- That video recorded statements are taken soon after the event and are made when they are fresh in the memory of the witness, they are therefore more likely to be an accurate and reliable account of what occurred.

- It is an irrefutable record of what was said, how the questions were asked/answers elicited

- It captures gestures when child attempts to explain an action that has occurred

- Also enables the fact finder to observe their age and demeanour at the time that they are recounting what occurred. Especially important when there has been a delay in listing for hearing

<!-- -->

- For a special hearing; this may be sought when there is likely to be a lengthy delay or where the whole of the hearing can not be heard at one time, however as matters are heard by Magistrates and not Judge and Jury it is unlikely to be useful it in the Magistrates Court jurisdiction as the special hearing has to be recorded. The magistrate is more likely to just proceed with that part of the hearing without the need to conduct a special hearing.

EXAMPLE APPLICAION:

Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001

Application

Section 5 Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001

\(ii\) The video statement made by AAAAA, an affected child, on 9 April 2020 be admitted into evidence; and

\(iii\) The video statement made by BBBBB, an affected child, on 9 April 2020 be admitted into evidence

## Power of arrest

### Need to state reason

 The *Criminal Code, *s301(2), provides as follows:

\"**(2)** It is the duty of a person arresting another, whether with or without warrant, to give notice, if practicable, of the process or warrant under which he is acting, or of the cause of the arrest.\"

if it is pointless (i.e. they would not be able to hear the cops) to inform or attempt to inform an arrested person of the cause of the arrest, it is not \"practicable\" to do so within the meaning of s301(2) **Johnson v Reeves \[2004\] TASSC 110 (24 September 2004)**

### "Found offending"

**Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan, Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan, Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden, David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse Files \[1997\] TASSC 52 (20 May 1997)**

*48. The Act, s34B(3) provides the authority for a police officer to arrest without warrant a person committing certain offences, including the offences of resisting and assaulting a police officer in the execution of his duty. Some suggestion was made during the course of argument that this power of arrest is governed by the provisions of the Act, s55. I do not accept that submission because s34B is not a section in respect of which it is \"specially provided\" that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person found offending against that provision. What is provided for by s34B(3) is that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person \"committing\" an offence.*

*49. In the course of argument, considerable reference was made to Lynch v Hargrave [\[1971\] VicRp 11](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1971/11.html); [\[1971\] VR 99.](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1971%5d%20VR%2099?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) Relevantly, that case concerned the expression \"found offending\", and, in particular, whether, before it could be said that a person was \"found offending\", it needed to be established objectively that that person in fact was offending or whether it was sufficient that the person finding the alleged offender believed on reasonable grounds that the person was offending. Relying on Trebeck v Croudace [\[1918\] 1 KB 158](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1918%5d%201%20KB%20158?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), McInerney J held at 109 - 110:*

*\"Accordingly, I am of the view the power of arrest of a person \'found offending\' is to be regarded as exercisable according to the facts as then known to the member of the police force. Whether or not the person arrested was in fact and in law offending in the relevant time is a matter for subsequent determination of the court.\"*

*50. There is no reason why the same principle should not be applied to the word \"offending\". I do apply it, so that the power conferred by s34B(3) is exercisable whenever a police officer believes, on reasonable grounds, that the relevant person is committing a relevant offence created by s34B. It follows that the lawfulness of the arrests of Leigh Phegan and Hulse is not determined by whether they actually assaulted Lee. The arrests may have been lawful and, accordingly, Rowlands may have been acting in the execution of his duty at relevant times even if the obstruction of Lee was not established.*

*51. Applying Lynch v Hargrave (supra) and the well-established meaning of \"committing\" (see Simmons v Millingen [\[1846\] EngR 275](http://www.worldlii.org/int/cases/EngR/1846/275.html); [(1846) 2 CB 524](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281846%29%202%20CB%20524?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), [135 ER 1051](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=135%20ER%201051?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13); Hanway v Boultbee [\[1830\] EngR 888](http://www.worldlii.org/int/cases/EngR/1830/888.html); [(1830) 1 M & Rob 15](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281830%29%201%20Macl%20%26%20R%2015?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), [174 ER 6](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=174%20ER%206?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13); R v Smith [(1876) 14 SCR (NSW) 419)](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281876%29%2014%20SCR%20%28NSW%29%20419?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) I construe s34B(3) as conferring a power of arrest in those cases:*

*1 where a person is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act;*

*2 where the arresting officer believes on reasonable grounds that a person taken is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act; and*

*3 where a person is taken immediately and in fresh pursuit after being found in either of those circumstances.*

*52. A police officer\'s power of arrest is to be exercised by him or her personally and on the facts known to him or her. The mere direction of a senior officer provides no lawful authority for the making of an arrest by the police officer to whom the direction is given. An arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. That liberty may not be infringed except by lawful authority. The direction of a senior officer does not provide that lawful authority. Any officer who complied with the direction given by Lee, did so at his peril unless there was some other basis which authorised that officer to make the arrest.*

### Family Violence (power of arrest)

*Barnes v Crossin* \[2017\] TASSC 61

31. Under s 11, the suspicion which provides authority for the arrest is that the person being arrested \"has committed family violence\". \"Family violence\" is defined by s 7 of the Act as follows:

\"**family violence means** ---

\(a\) any of the following types of conduct committed by a person, directly or indirectly, against that person\'s spouse or partner:

\(i\) assault, including sexual assault;

\(ii\) threats, coercion, intimidation or verbal abuse;

\(iii\) abduction;

\(iv\) stalking within the meaning of section 192 of the [Criminal Code](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2013-06-20/act-1924-069) ;

\(v\) attempting or threatening to commit conduct referred to in subparagraph (i) , (ii) , (iii) or (iv) ; or

\(b\) any of the following:

\(i\) economic abuse;

\(ii\) emotional abuse or intimidation;

\(iii\) contravening an external family violence order, an interim FVO, an FVO or a PFVO; or

\(c\) any damage caused by a person, directly or indirectly, to any property ---

\(i\) jointly owned by that person and his or her spouse or partner; or

\(ii\) owned by that person\'s spouse or partner; or

\(iii\) owned by an affected child.\"

32. As is obvious from this provision, conduct which constitutes family violence may not necessarily involve the commission of an offence. This distinction is emphasised by s 4 of the Act which separately defines \"family violence offence\" and \"family violence\". By s 4, \"family violence\" has the meaning defined in s 7. However, \"family violence offence means any offence the commission of which constitutes family violence\".

<!-- -->

33. Hence, an arrest may take place notwithstanding that the police officer does not suspect that an offence has been committed and does not intend to charge the person being arrested with such an offence. It is clear that the broad powers given to police authorise arrest for the purpose of facilitating proactive intervention by police in circumstances in which there is a reasonable suspicion that the person has committed family violence. Such intervention might include removing the person from the immediate environment, often the family home, to preserve the immediate safety of other family members and to facilitate a wide range of follow-up action. That action may include investigation as to whether an offence has been committed and, if so, charging the person with that offence. The broad ambit of the purpose of an arrest under this section is consistent with the legislative scheme contained within the Act. That scheme includes the following:

    - Section 11(5) which provides that in deciding whether to arrest a person under subs (1) \"the police officer is to give priority to the safety, wellbeing and interest of any affected person or affected child\".

    - Section 11(4) which provides that after arrest a person may be detained for a range of purposes defined as follows:

\"(a) determine the charge or charges which should be laid in relation to the family violence;

\(b\) carry out a risk screening or safety audit;

\(c\) implement the measures identified by a safety audit where it is practical to do so;

\(d\) make and serve a PFVO or an application for an FVO.\"

Section 11(2) and (3) which provide for a person to be brought before justices as soon as practicable, and also apply the provisions of s 4 of the *Criminal Law Detention and Interrogation Act *1995. This section authorises detention for a reasonable time \"for the purposes of questioning the person, or carrying out investigations in which the person participates in order to determine his or her involvement, if any, in relation to an offence.\"

## Definitions

### Approach:

*Kerr v Brown* \[2013\] TASMC 30

6\. Perhaps surprisingly, the only decided judicial authority I can find as to the meaning of approach in such circumstances, is a decision of the Family Court of Australia, In the marriage of Moio [\[1984\] FLC 91-575.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1984%5d%20FLC%2091%2d575) In that case, Gee J considered the meaning of the word "approach" in respect of an allegation of breach of an undertaking given to the court that a husband would "not harass approach, molest, assault or otherwise interfere with the wife or her defacto husband". The husband was alleged to have breached that provision, in particular, by approaching the defacto husband by driving his car, for a short period, in close proximity to a car in which the defacto husband was travelling. His Honour concluded that the meaning of the word "approach" needed to be interpreted in context of other words in the undertaking. He also considered the dictionary meaning of that word, in particular, the meaning in the Shorter Oxford English Dictionary "coming near a person both in time and circumstance". He considered, in particular, that there was an element of intent involved in the use of that word. His Honour was not satisfied that in the circumstances of the case there had been a breach of the undertaking.

7\. In my view, it is appropriate to construe the word as it appears in this order not only having regard to the context of the balance of the condition, but also bearing in mind its ordinary common meaning. The order is intended to create obligations on the part of the respondent, the breach of which constitutes an offence and can result in the application of criminal sanction. It would be unfair to interpret the provision in any way other than that by which it would be understood by persons in the position of the defendant i.e. without legal training and of average experience and education. It is the defendant who is expected to comply with the obligations imposed by the condition, in jeopardy of criminal sanction, and, accordingly, it is fair that those obligations be understood according to their ordinary common meaning. The word should not be given any extended meaning.

8\. It seems to me that what is clearly conveyed by the provision is that a person is not to deliberately, and with intent, initiate action in order to bring himself into the presence of, or initiate a communication directly or indirectly with the protected person. An interpretation which limits the obligation to not initiating contact is consistent with the reference in the order to the various electronic means of communication. The word approach, as Gee J pointed out, involves an element of intent, and hence necessarily must involve the initiation of some action on the part of the defendant to give effect to that intent. On the other hand, if the obligation was intended to be wider than this, for example, that the respondent must not allow himself passively to come into the physical company or proximity of the person to be protected in any circumstances and if that occurs, then to remove himself, then it seems to me that the relevant order could easily be worded in a way which would clearly spell out that obligation to the respondent. This order was not so worded.

### Be within:

Must intend to be. Not a strict liability offence.

**Sundram v Bearman \[2020\] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)**

\[161\] The words 'be within' as they are used within the condition of the PFVO which it is alleged Mr Bearman breached (analogously with 'approach' in *Kerr v Brown*[^\[126\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/18.html#fn126)) must be interpreted in the context of the provisions of the [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) and with the other words in that order. I consider that an element of intent is involved in the use of those words.

### "Abuse"

- use (something) to bad effect or for a bad purpose; misuse.

- treat with cruelty or violence, especially regularly or repeatedly.

***K V K \[2012\] TASMC 123***

\[29\] The term "verbal abuse" should be construed in light of the other words in s7(a)(ii). A heated argument with raised voices does not, without more, constitute verbal abuse. It is difficult to characterise or define what words may amount to threats, intimidation or abuse. The same words may in some circumstances amount to a threat or abuse when in other circumstances they may not. Much depends on the background and the context. In some circumstances even the most seemingly innocent words may be highly intimidatory. The court should consider whether one of the parties is in a position of disadvantage, either physically, emotionally, intellectually, socially or economically.

**Example where not found: K & K para 32.** *She is obviously a competent and intelligent person. I do not accept that she would be in a position of disadvantage in any verbal argument or exchange with her husband or in any disagreement concerning property of the relationship.*

### "Court premises" 

Defined in Court Security Act

***court premises*** means the premises or place where a court is held and includes --

\(a\) a courtroom; and

\(b\) a forecourt, courtyard, yard, parking area, toilet facility, hall, corridor, or other area, used in conjunction with the premises or place; and

\(c\) a part of the premises or place used as an entrance to or exit from the premises or place; and

\(d\) a part of any premises, or any place, being used to enable a person, who is at a different location, to appear before the court by means of audio, or audio-visual, communication; and

\(e\) a place at which a view, hearing or the taking of evidence is being carried out in relation to proceedings before a court; and

\(f\) a part of any premises, or of any place, that are premises, or a place, used in relation to the operation or management of a court or court premises or that is referred to in another paragraph of this definition;

### Firearms

"Firearm"/ "possession" not defined in FV act and firearms Act doesn't have automatic extension to other acts like the criminal code. Need to argue that the absence of either definition in the firearms act makes it more likely it was drafted with the firearms act in mind.

**FVO expiry date incorrectly recorded by Court Registry. Defendant not in breach. Subsequent attempt to arrest was ultra vires.**

### "harassing"

Section 4 covers PFVO and FVO definetions of harras-/harassing. It is an exhaustive definition **Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018\] TASSC 55 \[para52\]**

**The only mental element is that the def intend the ACT that by virtue of the definition in s 4 constitutes harassing, not the act had any particular effect on the person to be protected.**

60. \"Harass\" in its ordinary sense involves conduct committed with the purpose of causing an effect on the person harassed, eg worry, fear or mental anguish: see *Johnson v Collier*[[(1997) 142 FLR 409.](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281997%29%20142%20FLR%20409) However, in my view, the provision of an exhaustive definition of \"harassing\", by reference to the actions of the alleged harasser, excludes the need to establish that the actions in question have had any particular effect on the person protected by the order, unless expressly so provided in the definition. It must logically follow that there is no requirement that the defendant intend or otherwise foresee the causation of such an effect.]{.mark} These conclusions are confirmed by the inclusion in some of the defined actions of an express requirement that the action occur in circumstances \"where it is likely\" that there will be some consequence relevant to the protected person. For example, in par (f), it is necessary that the offensive material be left \"where it is likely to be found by, given to or brought to the attention of\" the protected person. There are similar express requirements contained in pars (g) and (h). There would, of course, be no need to include these specific provisions, if an intention to cause or the actual causation of apprehension or fear was already implied in the definition.

<!-- -->

61. This construction is consistent with the subject matter and social purpose of the relevant legislation. The provisions in question are concerned with the establishment of firm and viable processes to ensure the protection of persons, from persons who probably have committed, and will again commit, family violence [(s 16(1)](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s16.html) of the Act). Family violence is recognised as a significant social problem and the Act is directly and expressly directed at dealing with this problem. A family violence order is the primary protective mechanism employed by the Act. [Section 3](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s3.html) provides:

\"In the administration of this Act, the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence are the paramount considerations.\"

62. The construction of the Act in respect of the mens rea required for the offence of contravention of an order, will be heavily influenced by this statutory context. However, while these considerations weigh against the presumption in favour of proof of intention to commit the offence, they do not completely displace it. Rather, they fashion and define the required mental element. Although the offence created by [s 35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s35.html) is summary, the section prescribes heavy penalties. This is also a relevant consideration, one which weighs in favour of the application of the presumption. In my view, the proper balance leads to the conclusion that the requisite mental element is that the defendant must intend to do the act, which by virtue of the definition in [s 4](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s4.html), constitutes \"harassing\". In this case, the relevant act is that defined by par (a), following the complainant. If this mental element is present, then the legislation operates to deem that the person has harassed the person protected by the order and, if the condition is in place, deems that there has been a contravention of the order. A requirement of this nature is consistent with the statutory scheme, is appropriate having regard to the subject matter of the provision, and enables and supports the viable enforcement of a family violence order. It also provides the person subject to the order with a clear understanding of the conduct proscribed by it.

**Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018\] TASSC 55 \[para52\]**

In this Act, unless the contrary intention appears --

Section 4 "harassing" means doing any one or more of the following actions in respect of a particular person:

\(a\) following the person;

\(b\) keeping the person under surveillance;

\(c\) loitering outside the residence or workplace of the person;

\(d\) loitering outside a place that the person frequents;

\(e\) entering or interfering with the property of the person;

\(f\) sending offensive material to the person or leaving offensive material where it is likely to be found by, given to or brought to the attention of the person;

\(g\) publishing or transmitting offensive material by electronic or any other means in such a way that the offensive material is likely to be found by, or brought to the attention of, the person;

\(h\) using the internet or any other form of electronic communication in a way that could reasonably be expected to cause the person to be apprehensive or fearful;

\(i\) contacting the person by postal, telephonic, electronic or any other means of communication;

### Following/ Keeping under surveillance

**Sundram v Bearman \[2020\] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)**

\[154\] Obviously the words* 'following' *or *'keeping under surveillance' *are not actually used in the particulars of count 4 (unlike counts 5 and 7 which specifically allege that Mr Bearman followed Ms Paul). Grammatically, at least, it is difficult to see how the particulars of count 4 could possibly satisfy the term '*following'. *Further, the term *'keeping the person under surveillance'* requires proof (at least) of close observation of the protected person by the defendant; and to be guilty of '*keeping*' another person under surveillance requires that the surveillance be conducted on a continued basis (as distinct from, for example, just looking at someone only once - and only for a short period). Such an interpretation gives meaning to the word *'keeping'* and promotes the purpose and objects of the [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) generally. Further, it avoids giving the word *'harassing'* too broad a meaning in the broader context within which this particular penal provision of the *[Family Violence Act 2004](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) *sits.

### Single act

**Howe v S \[2013\] TASMC 33 (29 July 2013)**

{para 13} Harassing means doing any one or more of the following actions in respect of a particular person.

*(i)* Contacting the person by post or telephonic, electronic or any other means of communication.

The reference in the definition to *"any one or more of the following actions"* suggests that a single act might be sufficient. Furthermore, it is appropriate, in my view, to interpret the term having regard to the context in which it is used in the order. The reference to *"threaten, harass, abuse or assault"* suggests that the order is to be understood as providing protection to a person from contact with the respondent which is unwelcome, and might be in various forms or have variable effect.

{para 14} This interpretation is consistent with case authority (see *Daly v Medwell* [(1986) 40 SASR 281](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281986%29%2040%20SASR%20281), where it was stated that the offence of using a telecommunications service for the purpose of harassing a person could be constituted by a single call or a course of conduct consisting of several calls, depending on the nature and intention of the relevant conduct, per White J at 284, King CJ at 296 and Prior J at 297; see also *Devlin v Cooper* [\[1991\] TASSC 115).](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1991/115.html)
