## Interaction with Family Law Act

The *Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act 2018* commenced on 31 August 2018.

A copy of the Bill and Explanatory Memorandum can be obtained at https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Bills_Legislation/Bills_Search_Results/Result?bId=s1109

**Amendment to Section 68T -- Special provisions relating to proceedings to make an interim (or interim variation of a) family violence order**

Section 68R of the *Family Law Act 1975* provides that a court of a State or Territory which holds jurisdiction under Part VII of the Family Law Act may, when making a family violence order, revive, vary, discharge or suspend a range of family law orders to the extent to which they provide for a child to spend time with a person, to ensure they are consistent with the State or Territory family violence order. Subsection 68R(4) restricts the court's power when making an *interim* family violence order to revival, variation or suspension of a family law order.

Section 68T previously provided that where the court, when making an *interim* family violence order or an *interim variation* of a family violence order, revives, varies or suspends an order, injunction or arrangement under section 68R, that revival, variation or suspension ceases to have effect at the earlier of:

(a) the time the interim order stops being in force; and

(b) the end of the period of 21 days starting when the interim order was made.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has removed the 21 day time limit in paragraph (b) of section 68T and replaced it with:

\(b\) the time specified in the interim order as the time at which the revival, variation or suspension ceases to have effect; and

\(c\) the time the order, injunction or arrangement is affected by an order (however described) made by a court, under section 68R or otherwise, after the revival, variation or suspension.

The amendments require judicial officers to consider whether the revival, variation or suspension should expire on a specific date (and if so, when), or whether it should expire when the interim family violence order expires, or when a court makes an alternative order.

These new provisions strengthen the court's ability to protect children and other vulnerable family members by allowing judicial officers to set timeframes according to the particular circumstances of the case. Relevant considerations when making such a decision may include balancing the safety risks to the child arising from having contact with a parent with the maintenance of the child/parent relationship. These changes are intended to better protect children and other vulnerable family members from violence by reducing the risk that can arise from inconsistent orders applying to a child's contact with an allegedly violent parent.

Judicial officers may only change family law orders under section 68R when they have evidence that was not before the court that made the original order (subsection 68R(3)). Evidence must support a finding that changing the family law order is in the best interests of the child concerned including because the family law order is inconsistent with a family violence order in force, or a person, including the child, has been or is likely to be, exposed to family violence as a result of the operation of that order (subsection 68R(5)).

**Amendment to Section 68P -- Obligation of court making order or granting an injunction under this Act that is inconsistent with an existing family violence order**

Section 68P of the Family Law Act sets out the obligations of a court when making an order, or granting an injunction under the Family Law Act, that is *inconsistent with an existing family violence order*. Subparagraph 68P(2)(c)(iii) requires the court, to the extent to which the order or injunction provides for a child to spend time with a person, or expressly or impliedly requires or authorises a person to spend time with a child, to explain the order or injunction to the person protected by the family violence order (if that person is not the applicant or respondent). In some circumstances, the person protected by the family violence order may be a child. Paragraph 68P(2)(d) sets out the matters that the court must include in the explanation, and notes that the explanation must be in language the person is likely to readily understand.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted three new subsections:

- subsection 68P(2A) provides that the court is not required to provide the explanation mandated by subparagraph 68P(2)(c)(iii) to a child if the court is satisfied that it is in the child's best interests [not]{.underline} to receive an explanation of the order or injunction;

- subsection 68P(2B) provides that the court is not required to include a particular matter otherwise required to be explained by paragraph 68P(2)(d) if the court is satisfied that it is in the child's best interests for the matter not to be included in the explanation; and

- subsection 68P(2C) provides that when determining what is in a child's best interests for the purposes of new subsections 68P(2A) and 69P(2B), the court is required to consider the matters in existing subsection 60CC(2), but is not required (but may choose) to consider the matters listed in existing subsection 60CC(3), despite the requirement in subsection 60CC(1).

In most cases, it will be in a child's best interests to understand how court orders apply to them and their family. However, this may not always be the case. In particular, it may not be in the child's best interests to be exposed to the conflict between their parents to the extent necessary for courts to comply with this requirement. In some cases, such as where the child is an infant, it will not be practical to give an explanation.

The provision is designed to ensure that the information children receive from a court is appropriate and does not expose them to further details of family violence which may result in re-traumatisation.

**Insertion of a new Subdivision G---Short form reasons for decisions relating to interim parenting orders**

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted a new section 69ZL - Short form reasons for decisions relating to *interim parenting orders* into the Family Law Act. Section 69ZL provides that a court may give reasons in short form for a decision it makes in relation to an interim parenting order. This provision does not otherwise affect the obligation of a court to give reasons for a decision it makes in relation to any matter arising under this Act.

This amendment implements recommendation 3 of the Family Law Council's 2015 *Interim Report on Families with Complex Needs and the Intersection of the Family Law and Child Protection Systems.* In its report, the Council identified that a practical barrier to state children's or magistrates' courts exercising family law jurisdiction is that the writing of detailed judgments is not the usual practice of these courts. The Council recognised this practical barrier applied similarly for federal family law courts.

Courts are already able to give reasons for any decision in short form, as long as the reasons are adequate. The new provision is not intended to, and should not be read, to limit the courts' existing powers in any way. For example, it should not be implied that courts cannot deliver short form judgments in final proceedings or in proceedings under other Parts of the Act.

**Insertion of a new Section 45A - Summary decrees**

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted a new section 45A into the Family Law Act, which strengthens and codifies family law judges' power to dismiss unmeritorious cases and proceedings that are frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process. While this does not significantly change the existing powers of the courts exercising family law jurisdiction to dismiss proceedings if they have no reasonable prospect of success, the provision is intended to improve court efficiency by providing greater clarity about when applications can be dismissed by the court.

Subsections 45A(1) and (2) allow the court to make a summary decree in favour of one party, in relation to the whole or part of a proceeding, if satisfied that a party has no reasonable prospect of successfully:

- prosecuting the proceedings or part of the proceedings, or

- defending the proceedings or part of the proceedings.

Subsection 45A(3) provides that, for the purposes of the section, in determining whether a defence or proceeding has no reasonable prospect of success, proceedings need not be hopeless or bound to fail.

Subsection 45A(4) empowers the court to dismiss all or part of a proceeding if it is frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process.

Subsection 45A(5) provides that proceedings or a part of proceedings are not to be considered frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process just because a related application is made and later withdrawn. This is intended to operate as a safeguard against the court misinterpreting the actions of victims of family violence who are litigants in person, who might make an application and then withdraw it for reasons other than the merits of their case, for example as a result of the power and control dynamics of family violence.

Subsections 45A(6)-(8) provide that:

- the court may make costs orders as it sees fit

- the court may take such action of its own volition, or on the application of a party to the proceedings, and

- the new section does not limit any powers that the court has apart from this section.

Section 45A has general application. Its use is not limited to matters involving allegations of family violence or other particular categories of disadvantage.

Section 118 of the Family Law Act, which deals with frivolous or vexatious proceedings, has been repealed.

**Other amendments**

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has also amended the Family Law Act such that:

- state and territory courts can be prescribed by regulations to have the same jurisdiction in family law parenting matters as that held by state and territory courts of summary jurisdiction under Part VII of the Family Law Act. The intent is to enable children's courts to make appropriate orders under the Family Law Act where there are existing proceedings before the court, particularly in child protection matters (see section 69GA).

  - The new provisions allow for the prescription of classes of proceedings in regulations, in which the prescribed court will have Part VII jurisdiction. It also allows for the prescription of rules of court in regulations, to apply when the prescribed court is exercising family jurisdiction.

  - It is a requirement that the Attorney-General consult with the relevant state or territory Minister before a court or any other matter is prescribed.

- courts of summary jurisdiction can hear contested family law property matters without both parties' consent, where the value of the property is up to a new limit to be prescribed in regulations. The new limit will exceed the current value of \$20 000, and different limits can be prescribed for different states and territories (see section 46A).

  - It is a requirement that the Attorney-General consult with the relevant state or territory Minister before a property value is prescribed.

  - Unless or until a new value is prescribed, the current limit of \$20 000 will continue to apply.

- subsection 114(2) of the Family Law Act is repealed. Consequently it is no longer open to the court to make an order relieving a party to a marriage from an obligation to perform marital services or render conjugal rights. The High Court held in *R v L (1991)* 174 CLR 379 that there is no such obligation under Australian law. Section 114(2) has therefore been effectively redundant since that time.

**JUSTICES ACT 1959 - SECT 30**

**30. Statement of offences, &c.**

\(1\) Any complaint, summons, warrant, or other document that is laid, issued, or made for the purpose of, or in connection with, proceedings before justices shall be sufficient if it --

\(a\) describes the matter of complaint with which the defendant is charged or of which he is convicted in ordinary language, avoiding as far as possible the use of technical terms and without necessarily stating all the essential elements of the matter of complaint; and

\(b\) contains such particulars as will give reasonable information of the nature of the matter complained of.

\(2\) Where an enactment constituting a simple offence or breach of duty states --

\(a\) the offence or breach of duty to be doing or omitting to do --

\(i\) any one of any different acts in the alternative; or

\(ii\) any act in any one of different capacities or with any one of different intentions; or

\(b\) any part of the offence or breach of duty in the alternative --

the acts, omissions, capacities, intentions, or other matters stated in the alternative in the enactment may be stated in the alternative in a complaint for that simple offence or breach of duty.

### Course of conduct 

**(Howe v S -- applied by MCKEE in Thomas v Stewart \[2017\] TASMC) needs more than one action: however, if they use threats like I'll kill myself if you leave, should satisfy requirement of ss 2.**

**Howe v S \[2013\] TASMC 33 (29 July 2013)**

20. Mr Doyle again submits that the difficulty with this charge is that a single conversation cannot amount to a breach of ![http://www8.austlii.edu.au/images/displeft.png](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [[s.9]{.underline}](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s9.html) **![http://www8.austlii.edu.au/images/dispright.png](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} [*[Family Violence Act]{.underline}*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) in that it is an element of the offence created by that section that the defendant has pursued a course of conduct, and that this necessarily involves more than one conversation.

<!-- -->

21. I agree with the general proposition that a course of conduct would normally be interpreted as *"conduct which is protracted or conduct which is engaged in on more than one separate occasion"*. (See *Gunes v Pearson* \[1996\] 89A Crim R 297 at 306). I note also that the definition of "*family violence*" in [[s.7]{.underline}](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s7.html), [*[Family Violence Act]{.underline}*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) includes "*stalking*" within the meaning of s.192, *Criminal Code*. That section defines *stalking* as a course of conduct. S.192(2) provides that for the purposes of that definition:

    - \(a\) A person pursues a course of conduct if the conduct is sustained or the conduct occurs on more than one occasion.

<!-- -->

22. On the other hand, s.9 itself defines *"a course of conduct"* to include ***"limiting the freedom of movement of a person, spouse or partner by means of threats or intimidation**"*. It seems to me that if a threat or act of intimidation had the effect of limiting the freedom of movement of a person, spouse or partner, that a single act of threat or intimidation could, in those circumstances, amount to *"a course of conduct"* and hence amount to an offence contrary to that section. Because of what follows, however, I do not intend to decide this question.

## Arrest and detention:

Section 12 of the FV Act says that section 34 of the justices act does NOT apply where charged for a breach of PFVO or FVO. Meaning they must taken to court.

However, where there is NO breach of a PFVO or FVO -- defendant can be released unconditionally or under police bail.

Only when there has been a charge for a breach must they be taken to court.

This means, they can be arrested for an order -- or for carry out a RAST etc, but there is no obligation for the police to lay charges then and there. They may release without charge, and review later.

Can police bail or unconditionally release for new FV charges (FVA s 11(2)). Where a def has been arrested for a breach of a PFVO of FVO, we cannot police bail them. Must be taken to a court (FV. 12(3)).

Only can release unconditionally where

## Breaches:

## Intent

**Sundram v Bearman \[2020\] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)**

156 The [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/), ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [s35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s35.html) **![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} does not create an offence of strict liability, regardless of fault. The evidence must prove that Mr Bearman "*knew all the facts constituting the ingredients necessary to make his physical acts criminal were involved in what he was doing.*"[^\[123\]^](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/18.html?context=1;query=%22fva2004158%20s35%22;mask_path=#fn123) In addition, consistently with the approach taken by Brett M (as His Honour then was) in different but somewhat analogous circumstances in *Kerr v Brown*[*^\[124\]^*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/18.html?context=1;query=%22fva2004158%20s35%22;mask_path=#fn124), the prosecution must prove an element of intent. That is, in order to satisfy the element of harassing by following, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that Mr Bearman deliberately and with intent travelled behind a vehicle which he knew was being driven by Ms Paul. Mr Bearman bears no onus to provide an innocent account of his presence on the road on that day; the prosecution case must exclude, beyond reasonable doubt, the possibility that Mr Bearman's presence and movements on the road at that time were innocent. Before convicting Mr Bearman of this count, the prosecution case must satisfy the Court beyond reasonable doubt that he intentionally followed Ms Paul for the apparently short journey between the position of the road where he stopped - and looked at Ms Paul as she passed -- and the intersection where Mr Bearman and Ms Paul drove off in different directions.

161 The words 'be within' as they are used within the condition of the PFVO which it is alleged Mr Bearman breached (analogously with 'approach' in *Kerr v Brown*[^\[126\]^](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/18.html?context=1;query=%22fva2004158%20s35%22;mask_path=#fn126)) must be interpreted in the context of the provisions of the [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) and with the other words in that order. I consider that an element of intent is involved in the use of those words.

## Collateral challenge rule 

In hearings for charges under s 35 of the FV act, defence cannot seek to challenge the validity of the underlying orders that contain the conditions.

- **McKenna v Smith \[2014\] TASMC 11 (27 March 2014)**

### Threat

Subjective recklessness (recklessly indifferent) as to whether fear is aroused in victim

R v Meek ** (1981) 1 NZLR 499 **(cited in **GARY JOHN CARTER v R No. SCCRM 94/72 Judgment No. 4498 Number of pages - 9 Criminal law and procedure - particular offences - offences against the person \[1994\] SASC 4498 (20 April 1994) -- SASC)**

*21. ... inherent in the concept of the making of a threat that the words used were intended to be taken seriously*

*by the person to whom they were being addressed; and that, if it remained a reasonable possibility that, in speaking as he did, the appellant was doing no more than merely unburden his feelings in a counselling context, the offence was not made out.*

*22 ... It seems to me that, in accordance with the ordinary meaning of that word, the notion of a threat also includes a mere declaration of hostile intent, which is made and intended to be taken seriously and thus influences the mind of the recipient, by arousing in that person an apprehension that the threat is at least likely to be carried out.*

*23. I consider that the evidence in this case fell short of establishing the existence of a positive intent to arouse the relevant fear, but it is impossible to escape the conclusion (at which the jury obviously arrived) that*

*The appellant was at least recklessly indifferent as to whether that fear would be aroused.*

## Construing condition meanings:

The general principles for construing the terms of a Family Violence Order were considered by Magistrate Brett (as he then was) in *Kerr v Brown* [\[2013\] TASMC 30.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/30.html) His honour said at paragraph \[7\]:

*"7. In my view, it is appropriate to construe the word as it appears in this order not only having regard to the context of the balance of the condition, but also bearing in mind its ordinary common meaning. The order is intended to create obligations on the part of the respondent, the breach of which constitutes an offence and can result in the application of criminal sanction. It would be unfair to interpret the provision in any way other than that by which it would be understood by persons in the position of the defendant i.e. without legal training and of average experience and education. It is the defendant who is expected to comply with the obligations imposed by the condition, in jeopardy of criminal sanction, and, accordingly, it is fair that those obligations be understood according to their ordinary common meaning. The word should not be given any extended meaning."*

## Matters arising out of relationship:

*Police v Martin *unreported, Magistrates court of Tasmania, 20 September 2021 Jago, M Cited in **Police v H \[2021\] TASMC 13 (22 December 2021)**

Magistrate Jago said in *Police v Martin* at paragraphs \[158\] -- \[160\]:

*"Here the communication sent was clearly an apology. It was an apology for having behaved in an appalling manner towards Ms Martin some five days earlier. It did not in my view involve any discussion. A discussion is a two-way process. It involves a conversation, debate or argument about an issue. It is the process of talking about something in order to reach a decision. It involves the exchange of ideas and comment. The Defendant's apology was not a discussion. It did not seek a response from Ms Martin. Did not raise any questions that required an answer. It did not seek to her to offer an alternate opinion about a matter. It did not raise any issues that required her input or opinion. It was a one-way communication which the Defendant was expressing his regret and acknowledging his wrongdoing. The communication was not sent for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship. The communication was sent for the purpose of apologising. The communication was not seeking and did not require any response from Ms Martin which is again indicative that it was not sent for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship."*

### Attempt

**ACTS INTERPRETATION ACT 1931 - SECT 34**

**34. Attempt to commit any offence to be an offence**

Where by any Act an offence is constituted, any attempt to commit such offence shall be an offence and be punishable in the same manner as the offence constituted by such Act.

### Breach evidentiary considerations 

**Section 35(3)**

Where there is a charge under s 35, the allegation in the complaint is evidence that there was an order in place.

***Bray v F Hoffman -- La Roche Ltd \[2002\] FCA 243 cited at para 24 Remess v Rabe \[2006\] TASSC 105)***

IFVO -- interlocutory -- s 75 of the Evidence Act applies as an exception to hearsay evidence. This goes to all (inclusive of 2^nd^ hand hearsay -- the protection to a respondent is still within s135)

**Burden of Proof**

**EVIDENCE ACT 2001 - SECT 142A**

**142A. Proof of exemption**

\(1\) It is not necessary in any complaint or indictment to specify, negative or prove any exemption, exception, proviso, condition, excuse or qualification.

\(2\) If a defendant or an accused relies on any exemption, exception, proviso, condition, excuse or qualification, the proof lies on the defendant or the accused.

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Note: This section does not appear in the [Evidence Act 1995](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/cth/consol_act/ea199580/) of the Commonwealth.

### Breach Sentencing considerations 

**STATE OF TASMANIA v ASHLEY LUKE HAINTZ                                     GEASON J\**
**COMMENTS ON PASSING SENTENCE                                         4 DECEMBER 2019**

"I note that some of the offences involving breaches of the police family violence order occurred with the apparent consent of the complainant.  Because of the multitude of circumstances, including emotional vulnerabilities which typically attend relationships such as this one, I do not approach breaches said to have occurred with the consent of the victim as mitigated by reason of that fact.  They are breaches of a court order designed to protect the vulnerable.  Unless enforced through the imposition of penalties for their breach, the efficacy of the system of family violence orders is eroded. I have approached the fixing of penalty on that basis, notwithstanding the way I have ultimately framed that penalty.  The failure to observe a court order intended to protect the innocent victims of abuse involves a discrete element of criminality required to be reflected in penalty, subject of course to the principles of totality which are engaged in a case like this.  The nature of the offending to which you have pleaded guilty is insidious.  It is difficult to detect because it occurs in the home and the ease with which powerful, physically strong and dominant people can inflict serious physical and other harm upon their partners in private requires sentences which deter such behaviour and demonstrate that the courts will not tolerate it.  General deterrence therefore looms large in framing a sentence.  Your conduct occurs in the face of court orders intended to protect the complainant and as I have noted, that is aggravating. Your conduct was protracted and unprovoked. It involved choking.  The complainant was unable to defend herself."

## Firearms conditions:

In the absence of a statutory definition it is necessary to give the word its ordinary meaning, and that means "the ordinary meaning conveyed by a provision having regard to its context in the Act and to the purpose or object of the Act (Acts interpretation Act 1931, 8B(3). Recourse may be made to the dictionary meaning "to confirm the interpretation conveyed by the ordinary meaning of the provision" (8B(1)(c) Acts interp Act.

# FV Sentencing

Her Honour (Magi Edwards) noted that sentencing aims relevant to family violence offences will likely include general deterrence, specific deterrence (in the case of repeat offenders) and denunciation and vindication of the victims. She also suggested that prosecution look at making greater use of Victim Impact Statements and, where relevant, BWC footage

## FV is serious as fuq 

**Brown v Jones \[2021\] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)**

25 The nature of family or domestic violence needs to be noted and steadfastly borne in mind. \"Family violence must always be regarded as a serious matter\": *Bonde v Ellery *[\[2016\] TASSC 43](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2016/43.html) per Brett J at \[27\]. In *Director of Public Prosecutions v Karklins* [\[2018\] TASCCA 6](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2018/6.html), [29 Tas R 373](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=29%20Tas%20R%20373) at [\[92\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2018/6.html#para92), Geason J (with whom Blow CJ and I agreed) said:

Domestic violence is properly regarded as a most serious form of offending, frequently hidden from view, and thus difficult to detect. The court has a symbolic function. Censure for domestic violence should be communicated through the sentences which are imposed. Community attitudes to it are changing: *R v Kilic* \[(2016) [\[2016\] HCA 48](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2016/48.html); [259 CLR 256\].](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=259%20CLR%20256) Strong denunciation is called for, and general deterrence is a primary consideration. For crimes which are difficult to detect, the consequences of discovery must be severe enough to counter the perception that their commission is a risk worth taking.\"

### Physical vs Psychological harm:

Will probs need a victim impact statement or something in the facts to support a submission in line with the following:

**Cooper v Goodwin \[2024\] TASSC 78 (9 December 2024)**

\[17\] The submission that the throwing of the telephone was reckless and did not cause long term harm focuses entirely on physical and not long term psychological harm. As is clear from cases like *Miller v McCullogh*, domestic violence is not confined to physical harm. See also *Sok v Minister for Immigration and Multicultural Affairs *[\[2005\] FCAFC 56](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/FCAFC/2005/56.html), [(2005) 144 FCR 170](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%282005%29%20144%20FCR%20170) at [\[16\]](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/FCAFC/2005/56.html#para16) per Branson J and at \[37\] per Marshall J.

## Opinion of vic in FV sentencing 

For mitigation it means nothing.

**Brown v Jones \[2021\] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)**

Porter AJ citing among others DPP(Acting) v JCN -- the attitude's of the complainant with respect to forgiveness carries little weight.

\[31\] As to the attitude of the complainant, to the extent it was suggested it was one of forgiveness, it carries little weight: *Director of Public Prosecutions v Chatters* [\[2011\] TASCCA 8](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2011/8.html), [21 Tas R 26](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=21%20Tas%20R%2026) at [\[60\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2011/8.html#para60), *Menichelli v Tasmania* [\[2009\] TASSC 111](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2009/111.html), [19 Tas R 299](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=19%20Tas%20R%20299) at [\[16\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2009/111.html#para16). In both cases reference was made to *R v Palu *[\[2002\] NSWCCA 381](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWCCA/2002/381.html), [134 A Crim R 174](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=134%20A%20Crim%20R%20174) in which the New South Wales Court of Appeal said the attitude of the victim cannot be allowed to interfere with the proper exercise of the sentencing discretion. The Court went on to say that sentencing proceedings are not a private matter between the victim and the offender, and that a serious crime as a wrong committed against the community at large and the community itself is entitled to retribution. See also *R v Burton *[\[2008\] NSWCCA 128](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWCCA/2008/128.html) at [\[102\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWCCA/2008/128.html#para102)--\[106\].

32 That matter of established principle has particular application in cases of family violence. I would respectfully agree with the following comments of Pearce J (with whom Blow CJ and Tennent J agreed) in *Director of Public Prosecutions (Acting) v JCN* [\[2015\] TASFC 13](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASFC/2015/13.html) at [\[20\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASFC/2015/13.html#para20):

\"The response to family violence is often complex. Family violence offences are not uncommonly accompanied by support of a perpetrator by a victim and reluctance on the part of the victim to assist a criminal prosecution. That is so for a range of possible factors including fear and a wish to preserve relationships, even dysfunctional and violent ones, for the sake of loyalty, affection, companionship, economic and domestic support. Sometimes those motivations are misguided but persist nevertheless. As a result, victims sometimes act in a way that seems to an objective observer to be incongruence and difficult to understand.\"

## 29A

Where declaration made, there must be an FVO, either new or extant.

### Court Considerations (as discussed by Magi Edwards). 

In relation to the declarations under s29A and the associated provisions, Her Honour noted that the statutory threshold is *not a high one*. Both sides should identify when this is triggered and be in a position to address the Court as to whether a declaration is warranted. Section 29A(3) identifies the matters to which the Court is to have regard, but gives a wide scope to that enquiry, noting that s29A(3)(d) allows the Court to look at any other matter which it considers to be relevant.

**Her Honour provided a (non-exhaustive) list of matters that she will look at, noting that other Magistrates may consider different factors to be relevant, as follows:**

- Gravity and seriousness of the matters on which the defendant is currently being sentenced.

- Risk factors (prior matters, any reports available relating to the defendant).

- Antecedents -- in particular, the gravity of any previous, like offending.

- Whether there is, or has been, more than a single complainant -- a propensity to behave violently in a relationship elevates the risk.

- Escalation in offending.

- Persistence in offending, especially if previous sentences have failed to deter the defendant.

- Personal circumstances.

- Any recognition or insight by the defendant into their behaviour.

- Any attempt to hide the offending conduct.

- A lack of remorse, e.g. if the matter proceeded to hearing.

Her Honour clarified that, from her perspective, she considers the threshold can be met by three (or more) offences being dealt with at court on the same day, even in the absence of prior matters (provided the offences themselves have occurred on different dates). Her Honour also indicated she would be receptive to submissions regarding the length of a declaration, or a further declaration by way of an effective extension (if an existing serial family violence perpetrator is being sentenced for new offending); also to deferring the question of whether to make the declaration or not, if defence are seeking the opportunity to demonstrate rehabilitation.

(Crafty submission to make when there are significant priors to support a 29A:

*"Sentences of imprisonment for offences of violence have not acted as a deterrent. As the sentencing judge observed, the appellant was not to be sentenced for his previous offences, but his record of violent offending demonstrated that the assaults under consideration were \"not aberrations committed out of character\". In addition, as previous sentences of imprisonment have not deterred the appellant from committing violent offences, personal deterrence was an important factor in sentencing."*

### Relevant cases referred to by Magi Edwards:

**Miller v McCullogh \[2024\] TASSC 61 (11 November 2024)**

**Held:** Def had taken appreciable rehabilitation steps between sentencing and offending, but given severity of physical violence in a FV context, appeal against term of imprisonment was rejected.

**Facts:** Def charged with multiple assaults -- non strangulation holding of neck, and emotional abuse. Def had priors for assault against vic, assault generally and breaching orders. Had PG before pre-set hearing date and had vol engaged in counselling to address issues before sentencing.

*\[29\] As highlighted by counsel for the respondent, the maximum penalty for the Family Violence Act offence is two years\' imprisonment. The other three all attract a maximum of 12 months. The applicant had prior convictions for violence, the most recent in April 2020 which led to a suspended prison term. When considering his earlier offending, in particular the family violence offences in 2019, it might be thought that the applicant may have been motivated to seek assistance with his mental health and alcohol abuse issues before being charged in August 2023 with the present matters, and his relationship ending as a result of his conduct.*

*\[30\] In any event, the fact remains that he did seek assistance both in relation to his mental health and specifically his alcohol abuse. Those rehabilitation attempts were pursued until he was remanded in custody on 22 April 2024. That fact and the potential continued rehabilitation efforts were not matters that had no significance in the sentencing process. Additionally, the applicant was entitled to some, but in the circumstances no great, credit for his pleas of guilty. The magistrate accepted an expression of remorse but treated it as a \"late development\". No doubt that was prompted by the initial pleas of not guilty and the very late change. Remorse seems first to have been expressed to Dr Little and given the developing insight in the sessions with him, it is reasonable to accept that it was then genuine.*

**Palmer v Tasmania \[2024\] TASCCA 6 (5 July 2024)**

## 13A Presence of children in sentencing 

the presence and involvement of the children was \"obviously\" an aggravating factor. It caused great upset and no doubt ongoing anguish. Living arrangements were altered and the children\'s routine no doubt disrupted. As to this factor, I venture to repeat what I said in 

*Allen v Kerr *[\[2009\] TASSC 10](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2009/10.html), [19 Tas R 132](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=19%20Tas%20R%20132) at [\[13\]](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2009/10.html#para13): Porter AJ

\"The reasons that the presence of children is an aggravating factor of some significance are largely self-evident. Violence witnessed by children in the domestic environment not only is distressing (usually the victim is a parent or someone in the place of a parent), but it also serves to desensitise impressionable minds to violence, and to encourage the notion that resort to violence is acceptable.\"

## FVO Applications:

### Indefinite FVO (and 18+)

LA SAH were successful in obtaining an indefinite IFVO with an adult child on the order. This was in Launceston

Matter No. 70285/2017 (Peter Geoffrey HUBBARD)

Circumstances were unusual. Below are the notes from Kate Hughs:

**The circumstances:**

- The family violence was severe (mainly physical abuse against both the applicant and the 2 children);

- The family violence against the Applicant occurred throughout the duration of their relationship -- which was almost 20 years;

- When we originally made the FVO Application, the eldest child was 17 years old, but by the time of the hearing he had turned 18. The other child was 7;

- The IFVO was originally issued in April 2017 and included both of the children.

 

**[I will provide you with some arguments / things that worked in our favour:]{.underline}**

- **The 18 year old child gave evidence** -- the 18 year old essentially provided evidence that he was terrified of his father and it causes him distress just seeing him, and that he wanted nothing further to do with him. The 18 year old also made reference to the other child (his 7 year old brother) who he said had similar views of wanting an Order to remain in place. I made submissions that there is nothing to say that an adult child cannot be included on an Order -- he was under 18 when the Application was made, and a number of FV incidents occurred when he was under the age of 18. He also gave evidence that he feels safer knowing there was an Interim Order in place. The Magistrate also asked him whether he wanted to see his father at all -- his answer was no;

- **The Applicant's parents both gave evidence** -- and they both have Restraint Orders against the Respondent;

- **The Respondent's demeanour** -- this matter went to Court on a number of occasions, before several magistrates. On almost every occasion, the respondent would "act out" in Court. The hearing was in front of Magistrate Brown and the matter had been in front of him several times previously. In his decision, Magistrate Brown commented about the Respondent's concerning behaviour;

- **Family Court Orders were in place** -- these were finalised in June 2017 -- the Applicant was given sole parental responsibility of the children (no time spent with the Respondent)- used the argument that a judge in the FCC has already decided that the Respondent spending no time with the 2 children were in the children's best interests -- and that there is an absolute restriction on the Respondent spending time with the children, and that it is unusual for a Court to order sole parental responsibility;

- **Breaching Orders**: The Respondent had previously breached Orders and been charged with common assault;

- **Breaches of the IFVO**: The Respondent was charged with breaching the IFVO. There were a few, but the most unusual method (recognised by Magistrate Brown in his decision) was that the Respondent threatened the Applicant via internet banking deposits;

- **Texts/Facebook posts**: The Respondent sent threatening and abusive text messages to the Applicant and her father, and regularly posted about the Applicant on Facebook. The threats were directed at the entire family;

- **The Respondent did not give evidence and had no witnesses**;

- **The Respondent's mental health **-- The Respondent has anxiety and depression -- Applicant believes he doesn't take prescribed mediation. She had concerns that he had undiagnosed mental health issues. Under the Family Court Order he could apply to spend time with the children upon filing an affidavit from a qualified psychologist.

 

**[The decision:]{.underline}**

In his decision, Magistrate Brown referred to s16 (2) of the FVA and the words "*any other person*" -- and that "any other person" can be protected by an Order. He noted the distinction between "any other person" and how this is different to an "affected child" or "affected person". Magistrate Brown focused on the intention & primary purpose of the Act -- to protect those affected by FV.

 

Magistrate Brown noted that the 18 year old had clearly been affected by FV, and that there is a strong established nexus between him and the Applicant -- he was an affected child when the Application was made, and he could now be considered as "any other person" under the legislation.

 

Magistrates Brown decision in making an indefinite Order was mostly based on the following:

-          The length of the relationship;

-          The nature of the relationship;

-          In light of the breaches (some of which he pleaded guilty to that morning and was sentenced accordingly);

-          The Respondent's demeanour in Court and during the hearing

 

Magistrate Brown determined that an Order was necessary until a further Order is made (indefinitely).

 

I think the fact that the 18 year old gave evidence really helped our case -- without that, I'm not sure if it would have been made indefinite or not!

### Court not obligated to make an IFVO. 

It should not be rubber stamped by mere presence of an application. **Franklin v Burke \[2022\] TASSC 59 (2 September 2022)**

### Revocation/ variation

### "Substantial change"

Section 20 of the Act prevents the court from granting leave for an application to be heard unless satisfied there has been **substantial** change in circumstances since the last order was made or varied (s 20(3)). This includes Police with respect to extensions.

Substantial defined?? Certainly significant.

The section does not permit the court to go behind the decision making of the existing order -- all facts that existed at the time should be taken to have been considered. That is simply because the defendant argues it was not considered at the time the original order is irrelevant -- there must be a SUBSTANTIAL change since the order was made.

### IFVO

Court may make an IFVO at any stage without need to recourse of the considerations in s 16.

**Section 23**

\(1\) can make an IFVO on a whim at any stage in proceedings of an FVO app

\(3\) Leave not required to vary or to extend IFVO

\(4\) Can be made or changed without the other person being there (no service requirements of IFVO or original FVO app)

Remember! When making submissions about whether the IFVO is to be made.

**3. Objects of Act**

**In the administration of this Act, the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence are the paramount considerations.**

### Ex Parte orders

**Section 31 (7)**

Court can make final order in the absence of the respondent if satisfied a sealed copy has been served, or that reasonable attempts have been made to serve a sealed copy of the application on the respondent.

They can also issue a warrant for the arrest of the respondent.

**Lacroix v Lacroix \[2015\] TASSC 42**

\[para 21\] *"must make actual attempt to serve"*

### Service of Orders 

Per s 25 - Defendant taken to be served and order taken to be of effect immediately if they were present in court, or from the moment they were personally served.

***Must be personally served!!! Acts interpretations act doesn't apply as it requires "Personal" service (i.e. is directing a type of service which is contrary to 29AB of the AI Act***

**ACTS INTERPRETATION ACT 1931 - SECT 29AB**

**29AB. Service of notices and other documents**

\(1\) If an Act authorizes, requires or permits a notice or other document to be served on a person, whether the expression \"serve\" , \"deliver\" , \"give\" or \"send\" or any other expression is used, without directing it to be served in a particular manner, the notice or document may be served --

\(a\) on a natural person --

\(i\) by giving it to the person; or

\(ii\) by leaving it at, or sending it by post to, the person\'s residential or postal address or place or address of business or employment whichever is last known to the server of the notice or other document; or

\(b\) on any other person by leaving it at, or sending it by post to, the person\'s principal or registered office or principal place of business.

\(2\) Nothing in subsection (1) --

\(a\) affects the operation of any other law that authorizes or requires the service of a notice or document otherwise than as provided by that subsection; or

\(b\) affects the power of a court or person acting judicially to authorize service of a notice or document otherwise than as provided in that subsection.

**ACTS INTERPRETATION ACT 1931 - SECT 30**

**30. Effecting service by post**

\(1\) Where any Act, including this Act, authorizes or requires any notice or other document to be given, sent, served, or delivered by post, such giving, sending, serving, or delivery shall be deemed to be effected by properly addressing, prepaying, and posting the document as a letter, and, unless the contrary is proved, shall be deemed to have been effected at the time when the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of post.

\(2\) When an Act authorizes or requires any notice or other document to be given, sent, served, or delivered by registered post, the giving, sending, serving, or delivery thereof shall be deemed to be effected by properly addressing, prepaying, and posting the notice or document as a letter --

\(a\) duly registered; or

\(b\) unless the contrary intention appears in that Act, sent by means of the certified mail service --

as provided by the postal regulations as in force for the time being, and, unless the contrary is proved, shall be deemed to have been effected at the time when the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of post.

\(3\) Except where otherwise provided any such letter as aforesaid may be addressed to the usual or last known address of the person to whom it is to be sent.

### Consent orders

**Marriage v Tasmania Police \[2020\] TASMC 4 (6 April 2020)**

This case is actually about MPES Act. But consideres the construction of a similar "consent" provision in the context of whether it is an independent power, is still rely on a "head" provision requiring the court being satisfied as to certain matters. See para 3 onwards.

**Whether consent order requires requisite belief???**

Magistrate Jago suggested in court that the power to make an FVO under s 22 is predicated on the satisfaction of the requisite belief prescribed in s 16.

Relevant legislation:

FAMILY VIOLENCE ACT 2004 - SECT 16

16. Family violence orders

\(1\) A court may make an FVO if satisfied, on the balance of probabilities, that --

\(a\) a person has committed family violence; and

\(b\) that person may again commit family violence.

     

\(2\) An FVO may include such conditions as the court considers are necessary or desirable to prevent the commission of family violence against an affected person or to protect any other person named in the order.

     

\(3\) Without limiting the nature of the conditions which may be included in an FVO, the court may require the person against whom the FVO is to be made to do one or more of the following:

\(a\) vacate premises, not enter premises, or only enter premises on certain conditions, whether or not that person has a legal or equitable interest in the premises;

\(b\) not possess firearms specified in the order or forfeit or dispose of any firearms in his or her possession.

FAMILY VIOLENCE ACT 2004 - SECT 22

22. FVO by consent

     

\(1\) A court may make an FVO in terms consented to by the parties.

     

\(2\) In making an FVO by consent, the court may record that the person against whom the order is made does not admit any of the matters alleged in the application for that order.

Argument:

Section 16 permits the court, if satisfied on the balance of probabilities that family violence has, and is again likely to occur, to make an FVO.

Section 22 permits the court to make an FVO in terms consented to by the parties, without admission of the matters alleged in the application.

If a magistrate believes that family violence has, and is again likely to occur, the consent or otherwise of the parties is, in my mind, completely irrelevant. The court is directed by the paramount objectives of the Act to ensure the welfare of affected parties by whatever conditions necessary.

Furthermore, as a civil application, if the Magistrate has reached the requisite belief under s 16 on the balance of probabilities that family violence has, and will again occur, then the admission or otherwise of the defendant is similarly irrelevant. The court has already satisfied itself that such events will/ have occurred.

For these reasons I would suggest that s 22 must be an independent power, divorced from the requirements of s 16, allowing the court to make an FVO without the need to form the requisite belief where the terms of the application are consented to by the parties.

## Jurisdiction 

Applicatrions have to be made and heard in lower court only. Only a court (court is defined as lower court -- so when it says court read magi) can make an order. Supreme court can only make s 36 orders. Cannot hear apps (section 36 says court OR judge).

Therefore a prob arises if a protected party or

## Restriction on JP's

[**Justices Rules 2003**](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/sr-2003-184)

**54T.**   **Jurisdiction of justices**

[*\[Rule 54T Substituted by S.R. 2009, No. 77, Applied:08 Jul 2009\] *](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2009-07-08/sr-2009-077#GS5@EN)The court, when constituted by one or more justices (one of whom is not a magistrate), may exercise only the following jurisdiction under the[Family Violence Act 2004](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067) :

\(a\) the granting or refusal of bail under [section 12](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS12@EN) of that Act;

\(b\) the making, variation, extension or revocation of an interim FVO under [section 23](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS23@EN) of that Act;

\(c\) the revocation (but not the making, variation or extension) of an FVO under [section 20](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS20@EN) of that Act.

## Standing in private applications

Generally none. However:

There is authority that had deemed that police were not a party to family violence proceedings and that the Act was amended thereafter to permit it. And I did not have it to hand. It was the decision of the Supreme Court in *McLean v Kemp* \[2005\] TASSC 100. Parliament responded by introducing s31 (2A) to remedy the situation.

 

An ambiguity arises if similarly, to *Lacroix v Lacroix* \[2015\] TASSC 42, *Lacroix v Lacroix *\[2018\] TASFC 8, a fresh application, rather than extension/ variation, is filed.

 

It may be that given that under s14(8) the PFVO is revoked making of an IFVO or FVO, an application under s15 is be construed in these situations to also be made pursuant to Part 3. Which would mean that s31(2A) would engage the Police as a party.

 

Otherwise the situation would be that a PFVO could be weakened significantly by the making of a basic FVO without further input from the Commissioner for Police, despite a police officer being satisfied that a PFVO on particular terms was required to protect the safety wellbeing and interests of protected party and or any affected children.

**McLean v Kemp \[2005\] TASSC 100 (18 August 2005)**

**Decision was made in the context of granting leave to hear the application \*This is not the same as a determination that the order should in fact be changed! Just a question of leave. They are not the same. They are different thresholds!**

\[23\] The learned magistrate was required to be satisfied before granting leave that there had been a substantial change in the relevant circumstances since the order was made on 26 April.

\[24\] The words \"substantial change\" and \"relevant circumstances\" are not defined in the [Act](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/). The ordinary meaning of those words would suggest that what was intended was that the Court be satisfied there had been a significant or obvious change in the circumstances involving the parties affected by the FVO since the date it was made.

28 There was clear material before the learned magistrate that neither the applicant nor Mr Kemp wanted the no contact provisions in the FVO to continue.

29 The learned magistrate took the view that he did not accept that the applicant could not have been scared of Mr Kemp if he did what she said. However, on the material before him, while he might reasonably have been satisfied the applicant had indeed been scared prior to the making of the FVO, there was no evidence apart from hers as to what had since occurred and she unequivocally said she now was not fearful. That is, apart from anything else, a clear change of circumstances.

## Family Court Orders

Family court order defined in FV act as one made under Part 7 (children) of the Family Law Act. There are other types of orders from other parts that are made, so be sure to check.

Family law act powers of inconsistency (eg. 68 onwards) are also limited to considerations of Parenting orders under part 7. So can suspend Family Court parenting order for FVO if inconsistent. Per s. 68R.

## Supreme court

## Form of applications

**30.**   **Forms of FVO, PFVO and applications**

An FVO, a PFVO or an application under this Act is to be in a form approved by the Chief Magistrate.

**Magistrates Court Act 1987**

**15AA. Practice directions**

\(1\) The Chief Magistrate may --

\(a\) issue such practice directions in relation to the practice and procedure of the lower courts as the Chief Magistrate thinks fit; and

\(b\) vary or revoke any such practice directions; and

\(c\) publish any such practice directions as the Chief Magistrate thinks fit.

\(2\) The practice and procedure of the lower courts is to be in accordance with the practice directions from time to time in force unless the provisions of any Act or of any rules or other statutory instruments made under an Act provide otherwise.

\(3\) The Chief Magistrate may issue practice directions specifying the circumstances in which initiating process must be filed in a registry.

**PRACTICE DIRECTION**

**(No. 3 of 2005)**

I, Arnold George Shott, Chief Magistrate of Tasmania, pursuant to the Magistrates Court Act 1987 section 15AA, hereby approve the following forms: -

Family Violence Order;

Interim Family Violence Order;

Application for Family Violence Order;

Application for variation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order;

Application for extension of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order; and

Application for revocation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order,

**(NOT ORAL)**

in accordance with the Family Violence Act 2004 section 30.

Direct link to forms format.

Dated this Thirtieth day of March 2005.

A. G. Shott

Chief Magistrate

**So oral applications for IFVO's are really just an invite for the MAGI to use their own discretion to make an order under s 23 (FV Act) as opposed to us formally "applying"**

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54L**

**PART 9B - Practice and procedures under Family Violence Act 2004**

**54L. Applications generally**

An application is to --

\(a\) specify the order sought; and

\(b\) include --

\(i\) in the case of an application made by a police officer, a statutory declaration of the officer that to the best of his or her knowledge and belief the information contained in the application is true; or

\(ii\) in the case of any other applicant, an affidavit of the person that to the best of his or her knowledge and belief the information contained in the application is true; and

\(c\) be filed with the clerk.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54S**

**54S. Power of justices to dispense with Part**

Justices may, upon such terms and conditions as they think necessary, dispense with the need for a party to proceedings under Part 4 of the [Family Violence Act 2004](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) to comply with any provision of this Part.

## Service of Applications:

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54N**

**54N. Service of applications**

\(1\) A person making an application is to, as soon as practicable after it is filed with the clerk, cause a sealed copy of the application to be served personally on --

\(a\) in the case of an application for an FVO or an application for a variation to, or extension or revocation of, an FVO, on all other parties to the application; or

\(b\) in the case of an application for a variation to, or extension or revocation of, a PFVO, on all other parties to the application and on the Commissioner of Police.

\(2\) Where a police officer is the applicant for the revocation of an FVO or a PFVO, service of a sealed copy of the application is not required.

\(3\) A person who serves a sealed copy of an application is to --

\(a\) endorse a copy of the application with a memorandum stating the day, place and mode of service; and

\(b\) sign the memorandum; and

\(c\) as soon as practicable, cause the endorsed copy to be lodged with the clerk for the district in which it is returnable.

**54S. Power of justices to dispense with Part**

Justices may, upon such terms and conditions as they think necessary, dispense with the need for a party to proceedings under Part 4 of the [Family Violence Act 2004](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) to comply with any provision of this Part.

## Hearings:

### FVO Hearings

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54P**

**PART 9B - Practice and procedures under Family Violence Act 2004**

**54P. Hearing of applications**

\(1\) At the hearing of an application under this Part, evidence may be given by affidavit.

\(2\) A person who makes an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1) is not required to attend the hearing of the application unless a party to the proceedings, or the justice, so requires.

\(3\) By notice in writing given to the person who made an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1), a party to the proceedings may require that the person attend the hearing for cross-examination and, if the person fails to attend, the court may --

\(a\) refuse to allow the affidavit to be used; or

\(b\) allow the affidavit to be used on any conditions the court thinks fit; or

\(c\) adjourn the proceedings until the person attends for cross-examination.

\(4\) Notice under subrule (3) is to be given not less than 10 clear working days before the proceedings.

### **Affidavits**

MAGISTRATES COURT (CIVIL DIVISION) RULES 1998 - REG 55

Division 8 - Affidavits 55. Form of affidavit

\(1\) An affidavit is to --

\(a\) specify the title of the action in which it is sworn; and

\(b\) be drawn up in the first person; and

\(c\) state --

> \(i\) the name, address and occupation of the person making the affidavit; or

\(ii\) if the person making the affidavit does not have an address or occupation, a description of the person; and

\(d\) be divided into consecutively numbered paragraphs that, as far as practicable, relate to separate portions of the subject matter of the affidavit; and

\(e\) specify any sum of money or number in figures only; and

\(f\) contain a statement signed by the person before whom it was sworn specifying --

> \(i\) the full name of the person making the affidavit; and
>
> \(ii\) the day on which and the place at which the affidavit was sworn; and

\(g\) be signed on each page by --

> \(i\) the person making the affidavit; and
>
> \(ii\) the person before whom it was sworn.

\(2\) Any annexure or exhibit filed with an affidavit is to be --

\(a\) identified by the title of the action; and

\(b\) signed by the person before whom the affidavit was sworn.

\(3\) Unless the Court orders otherwise, an affidavit may be received in evidence despite any irregularity in form.

MAGISTRATES COURT (CIVIL DIVISION) RULES 1998 - REG 56

56. Contents of affidavit

\(1\) An affidavit is to be confined to facts to which the person making the affidavit is able to depose from his or her own knowledge.

\(2\) An affidavit filed in relation to an interlocutory application may contain statements based on information received by the person making the affidavit, and believed by that person to be true, if the affidavit also contains --

\(a\) the sources of the information received; and

\(b\) the grounds for believing thatthe information is true.

MAGISTRATES COURT (CIVIL DIVISION) RULES 1998 - REG 74

74. Hearing an application

\(1\) Unless the Court orders otherwise, an interlocutory application is to be heard in open court.

\(2\) With the leave of the Court, a person may appear by telephone or video link at the hearing of an interlocutory application.

\(3\) The hearing of an interlocutory application may be adjourned --

\(a\) on no more than 2 occasions; and

\(b\) to a fixed date only.

\(4\) At the hearing of an interlocutory application, a fact may be proved by affidavit.

**Oaths Act 2001**

3\. Interpretation

In this Act --

affidavit includes --

\(a\) any affirmation, statutory declaration or other declaration; and

\(b\) any acknowledgment, examination or attestation; and

\(c\) for the purpose of section 7 , any deposition or statement made by an informant or a complainant;
