## PFVO

Leave of the court is NOT needed to make an app to vary -- extend -- revoke a PFVO in court. zIt is for a second attempt can only be made with leave (requiring evidence of substantial change) from the court as it becomes an FVO

App to vary PFVO can be made to an inspector but only with consent of both parties (s 14((7))

PFVO automatically revoked when IFVO or FVO made in realtion to both parties.(s 14((8)).

### Authority to issue:

Section 35(3) creates a rebuttable presumption that the order has been made, and that it was validly made\" in Proving authority

See **McKenna v Smith \[2014\] TASMC 11 (27 March 2014**

**\[35\] ...**

... [Section 35(3)](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s35.html), [*Family Violence Act*](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/), establishes that the allegation that the order was made is evidence that it was made and in force at the time of the alleged contravention. This can only mean that the allegation constitutes evidence that the order was made pursuant to the provisions of [s 14](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s14.html), and it would follow, therefore, that the allegation is evidence that it was made by a police officer of the requisite rank. Furthermore, although Constable Goss was clearly not familiar with the technical processes by which he came to act in the rank of sergeant, it is clear that he regarded himself, and was regarded by other officers as acting as sergeant for the shift. This, in itself, raises an inference that he held appropriate authority to perform those duties. When regard is then had to the provisions of [s 35(3)](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s35.html), it is not necessary, in my view, for the prosecution to prove the existence of the authority to act in that rank. I agree with Magistrate Bartlett\'s description of that section in *Police v Maxwell*(unreported, 9 August 2012) that the subsection *\"creates a rebuttable presumption that the order has been made, and that it was validly made\"*. In my view, the court should accept that Constable Goss held the requisite rank, in the absence of specific evidence that he did not hold that rank. The evidence does not enable a finding to be made that he did not hold the rank of acting sergeant within the meaning of [s 34](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s34.html), [*Police Service Act*](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/psa2003149/) at the time that he made the order.

**My Summary - Lack of evidence the order was validly made is not enough to displace the presumption -- they need positive evidence to support Act was not acting.**

### "Acting" SGT -- delegated power

PFVO can only be issued by an officer of the rank of Sergeant or above, or authorised by the Commissioner of Police

There is a power vested in the Commissioner of Police, under a section of the *Police Service Act*, to appoint an officer to temporarily act in a higher roll/ rank, and such officer, so appointed, is vested with all the powers inherent in that roll/ rank. (per s 34 police service act 2003

Yet another section in the *Act* allows the Commissioner to delegate any vested power. (section 8) Police service act

Finally, there is an evidentiary provision in the *Act *that allows a certificate declaring that such a delegation had occurred, to be tendered as evidence of such. (s 95 Police service act)

There is a power of delegation document on record

This doc is to Inspectors or higher (on file)

*23AA Acts interpretation Act* TAS says that reference to a rank is sufficient -- need not be a person

### PFVO Conditions:

Limited to the confines of the permitted restrictions under s 14

14 (3) A PFVO may require the person to whom it is issued to do any or all of the following:

\(a\) vacate any premises, whether or not that person has a legal or equitable interest in the premises;

\(b\) not enter any premises or only enter premises on certain conditions, whether or not that person has a legal or equitable interest in the premises;

\(c\) surrender any firearm or other weapon;

\(d\) refrain from harassing, threatening, verbally abusing or assaulting an affected person, affected child or other person named in the order;

\(e\) not approach, within a specified distance, an affected person, an affected child, other person named in the order or certain premises;

\(f\) refrain from contacting an affected person, affected child or other person named in the order directly or indirectly or otherwise than under specified conditions.

### REVOCATION OF PFVO 

Not the same as the making of an FVO -- FVO needs to be satisfied FV (not offence) has occurred and FV (not offence) will happen again.

Revocation of a PFVO has no such indicia.

Court may make an FVO at the hearing of revocation of a FVO -- then bound by those considerations -- but if not being asked to make one, or electing it's own jurisdiction, the question is only whether to revoke a PFVO.

I accept -- that is not necessarily an unreasonable basis for the question to be determined -- but that has not been provided for under the ACT --

Section 14 should be considered in light of the objects of the act -- in the administration of the FV act the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence are the paramount considerations.

That is consistent with the indicia an Inspector of Tas police must consider.

Not the convenience of the Respondent.

The order was validly made. There can be no further challenge to its validity.

Where there es recent physical violence, only, in my submission strong psychological evidence supporting the contention that the protected party's mental wellbeing would be adversely affected by the continuation of the PFVO would justify it's revocation -- and none has been submitted

## Victim Impact statements

See **Belbin v Bennett 2011 TASSC** for a duscission on the impact on the considerations for sentencing these can have.

Victim impact statements After a person has been found guilty of, or has pleaded guilty to, an indictable offence the law provides that the victim of the offence can make a victim impact statement (s81A of the Sentencing Act 1997). In respect of a summary offence, a VIS can be provided where death or serious injury has occurred as a result of the offence, or if it is a family violence offence. This is often the only opportunity for the victim to have their say. A VIS is entirely voluntary and if the victim feels it would be more traumatic to go through the process of preparing one then there is no obligation for them to do so. On the other hand, some people find the process of preparing and presenting a VIS therapeutic and want to have a role in the process. The decision whether or not to prepare a VIS can be discussed with a WAS officer who can also assist with the preparation of the document. Victim Support Services can also provide assistance with the preparation of a VIS.

Prosecutors should be aware that a VIS is not evidence to show that a victim is suffering from a psychiatric illness or disorder as a result of the crime. Where necessary, a report should be obtained from a relevant expert.

There are some limitations as to what can be included in a VIS. The statement must be limited to details elating only to the crime of which the offender is convicted. Following are some pointers to assist in the preparation of a VIS. It should include:

• details of any physical injuries caused by the crime and the impact of

these injuries

• details of emotional and psychological damage caused by the crime

• details of any changes in behaviour, thoughts, attitudes, coping skills,

social life or relationships with others (i.e. how life was before the crime

and how it has changed)

• details of the impact of the crime on employment, education or loss of

future prospects

• details of the financial impact of the crime

• relevant medical or psychological reports that support the VIS

It should not include:

• any abusive or derogatory comments about the accused

• any impact that is not the result of the crime concerned

• any conduct of which the accused has not been convicted

• any suggestion as to what the sentence should be

Presenting a VIS to the court

compuThere are three options as to how a VIS can be presented to the court. The first option is for the written document to be handed up to the judge to read to him or herself. Some people prefer this because no-one else in the courtroom will hear it. However, it must be borne in mind that a VIS is not an automatically confidential document. A copy must be given to the accused\'s lawyer so the accused will also see it. Also, judges regularly use extracts from a VIS in their sentencing comments.

The second option is for the victim to read their VIS to the court.

The third option is for the victim to nominate another person to read the VIS on their behalf

When reading out a VIS it should be addressed to the judge or magistrate, not directly to the accused. If defence counsel challenge any material in the VIS it will be necessary to discuss this with the victim. It is possible (though very unusual) that the victim may have to go into the witness box in order the prove the material.

**ENTENCING ACT 1997 - SECT 81A**

**81A. Court may receive victim impact statement**

\(1\) In this section --

immediate family, in respect of a deceased victim, includes --

\(a\) the spouse or partner, within the meaning of the [[Relationships Act 2003]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ra2003173/), of the deceased victim; and

\(b\) a parent, guardian or step-parent of the deceased victim; and

\(c\) a child or stepchild of the deceased victim; and

\(d\) a brother, sister, stepbrother or stepsister of the deceased victim;

indictable offence means --

\(a\) an offence that is punishable on indictment even though in some instances it may be dealt with summarily; or

\(b\) any other offence that is prescribed for the purposes of this section;

victim, in respect of an offence, means --

\(a\) a person who has suffered injury, loss or damage as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) a member of the immediate family of a deceased victim of the offence.

\(2\) If a court finds a person guilty of an indictable offence, or a summary offence that has resulted in the death or serious injury of a person, or [a family violence offence]{.mark}, a victim of that offence may furnish to the court a written statement that --

\(a\) gives particulars of any injury, loss or damage suffered by the victim as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) describes the effects on the victim of the commission of the offence.

(2A) If a court finds a person guilty of an indictable offence, or a summary offence that has resulted in the death or serious injury of a person, or [a family violence offence,]{.mark} the court may, if it considers it appropriate to do so, allow a person, other than the victim of that offence, to furnish to the court a written statement, in lieu of a statement under subsection (2), that --

\(a\) gives particulars of the injury, loss or damage suffered by the victim as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) describes the effects on the victim of the commission of the offence.

\(3\) A statement referred to in subsection (2) or (2A) must comply with, and be furnished in accordance with, applicable Rules of Court or rules made under subsection (6).

\(4\) If the court finds a person guilty of an offence, the court must allow the victim, or the person who has furnished a statement under subsection (2A) (the \"other person\" ), or another person nominated by the victim or the other person, to read his or her statement to the court if the victim or the other person has so requested at the time of furnishing the statement to the court.

\(5\) .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

\(6\) For the purposes of this section --

\(a\) Rules of Court may be made under section 12 of the Criminal Code Act 1924; and

\(b\) rules may be made under [[section 144]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/s144.html) of the [[Justices Act 1959]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/).

\(7\) This section does not derogate from [[section 81]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81.html) and subsections (2) and (4) of that section apply to a statement furnished under this section.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54A**

**PART 9A - Victim Impact Statements 54A. Interpretation**

In this Part --

Court means a court of petty sessions;

victim means --

\(a\) a victim within the meaning of [[section 81A]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/) or section 36A of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/); or

\(b\) a person allowed to furnish to the Court a written statement under [[section 81A(2A)]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/) or section 36A(3) of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/);

victim impact statement means a statement furnished under [[section 81A]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/), or section 36A of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/), by a victim of an offence.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54B**

**54B. Duty of victim to provide copies of victim impact statement**

\(1\) A victim who wishes to furnish to the Court a victim impact statement is to provide the original of the statement to the prosecutor, and the prosecutor is to provide a copy of the statement to -- 

\(a\) the offender; or

\(b\) if the offender is represented, his or her counsel.

\(2\) On providing the statement under subrule (1), the victim is to advise the prosecutor whether he or she wishes to read the statement to the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54C**

**54C. Provision of victim impact statement to Court**

The prosecutor is to --

\(a\) provide to the Court any victim impact statement relating to an offence which has been provided by a victim of the offence; and

\(b\) inform the Court whether the victim has requested to read the statement to the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54D**

**54D. Reading of victim impact statement**

A victim impact statement is to be read to, or by, the Court before the Court passes sentence on the offender.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54E**

**54E. Postponement of reading of victim impact statement**

If a victim has requested to read a victim impact statement to the Court but is not present in Court, the Court may postpone the reading of the statement and is to cause notice of the time fixed for the reading of the statement to be given to the victim.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54F**

**54F. Power of victim to amend victim impact statement**

Subject to [[rule 54G]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_reg/jr2003168/s54g.html), a victim who has furnished a victim impact statement may amend the statement at any time before it is read to, or by, the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54G**

**54G. Power of Court to exclude irrelevant material**

If the Court so directs, a victim is not to read out to the Court any part of the victim impact statement that the Court considers to be irrelevant to the proceedings.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54H**

**54H. Power of victim to withdraw victim impact statement**

A victim who has provided a victim impact statement may withdraw the statement at any time before it is read to, or by, the Court and, if he or she does so, the statement is not to be read to, or by, the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54I**

**54I. Requirements for victim impact statement**

A victim may furnish to the Court only one victim impact statement and the statement --

\(a\) is to be legible; and

\(b\) is to be in the English language; and

\(c\) may be either printed or handwritten; and

\(d\) except with the leave of the Court, may not be longer than 20 pages, inclusive of medical reports and other annexures; and

\(e\) may, but need not, be in a form approved by the Chief Magistrate.

## Section 37 -- Personal information 

*Personal Information Protection Act 2004*

**3.**   **Interpretation**

***personal information** means any information or opinion in any recorded format about an individual --*

*(a) whose identity is apparent or is reasonably ascertainable from the information or opinion; and*

*(b) who is alive or has not been dead for more than 25 years;*

personal information custodian means any of the following:

\(a\) a public authority;

\(b\) any body, organisation or person who has entered into a personal information contract relating to personal information;

\(c\) a prescribed body;

## Strangulation:

The following is an excerpt from the DPP guidelines. 

 

To keep in mind when reviewing charges, particularly the out-of-hours courts.

 

From pg 55:

**Assault and family violence**

Assault cases involving choking, smothering or any other form of strangulation, particularly in a family violence context, should be regarded as grave criminal conduct and even where no injury occurs a charge of assault contrary to s184 of the Criminal Code should be considered. See ***Mayne v Tasmania \[2017\] TASSC 38*** where Wood J said at \[35\]:

 

> *"A relevant consideration is whether the sentence impinges upon the sentencing range for far more serious acts of smothering. I do not consider that that is the case**. Conduct involving persistent smothering, and a loss of consciousness, should properly be regarded as a grave act of violence. It would warrant an indictable charge of assault contrary to the Criminal Code.** Without being prescriptive, it may attract a very lengthy term of imprisonment, more than the maximum penalty of two years' imprisonment as an aggravated assault under the Police Offences Act. Relevant to the statistics relied upon by the applicant, it should be assumed that the statutory ceiling of 12 months' imprisonment, or even two years for an aggravated assault, does not, and should not, have application to smothering or choking cases leading to a loss of consciousness."* (Emphasis added)

 

 

Factors to be considered in determining whether to charge an accused with summary or indictable assault in relation to strangulation or suffocation include:

 

• the length of time of the strangulation or suffocation

• whether the victim became unconscious or received any other injuries • whether there was additional violence • whether there was an accompanying threat to kill

• the history of the relationship

• whether children were present

 

Where a person chokes, suffocates or strangles a person and renders that person incapable of resistance with an intent to commit an offence or flee an offence a charge under s168 of the Criminal Code should be preferred. Of course, deciding the appropriate category of charge is a matter of judgement not science and investigators and prosecutors will appreciate that the Office welcomes discussion about charging decisions before they are made.

*\[43\] This was the sort of case where it was also entirely appropriate to give prominence to general deterrence. There is a growing appreciation by the courts and the community of the prevalence of family violence, and it is important that deterrent sentences be imposed not merely for crimes that cause grave physical or psychological harm to victims. There is a need to counter the perception that somehow violence of this kind in the home is less serious than the same kind of violence inflicted on a stranger in a public place. Also, acts of violence committed in a family or domestic context causing fear and distress to victims can have debilitating effects upon their well-being or the well-being of a family member witnessing such violence. It is not only violence resulting in visible injury that must be seen as unacceptable, and these victims, as vulnerable members of our society who have experienced fear and trauma, are entitled to the court\'s protection.*

***Mayne v Tasmania \[2017\] TASSC 38 (29 June 2017)***

7 months for a single act of smothering with a pillow for 2-5 sec. The act is dangerious -- the defendant did not stop of his own accird -- would have been terrrying.

# Restraint Orders

CAN NOT POLICE BAIL breach of restraint orders. Section 34 of the Justices ACT does NOT apply per s 106I

**Restraint Order Breach Hearings:**

Need to tender evidence of RO -- use cert of conviction/ court order under s. 178 of the evidence Act

**"Breach of the peace defined"**

As noted earlier, breach of the peace is not an offence, but instead it is the basis for the execution of the common law power which prevents public disorder. Furthermore, breach of the peace is one element of other public order offences such as unlawful assembly or riot.

In attempting to establish what breach of the peace entails, we can turn to the leading authority of the concept which can be found in *R v Howell*, where Watkins LJ in the English Court of Appeal, defined breach of the peace in the following way:

***"There is breach of the peace whenever harm is actually done or is likely to be done to a person or in his presence to his property or a person is in fear of being so harmed through an assault, an affray, a riot, unlawfully assembly or other disturbance."***

*R v Howell \[1981\] 3 All ER 383*

Cited in *Cintana v Burgoyne A Crim R 296 at para 13-19*

Broadly speaking, breach of the peace as defined in *Howell* has been generally accepted in Australian common law.

**106E. Procedure in relation to hearing and determining applications under this Part**

\(1\) Except as otherwise provided by this Act or ordered by justices --

> \(a\) an application under this Part is to be heard and determined --
>
> \(i\) in open court; and
>
> \(ii\) in the same manner, as nearly as practicable, as a complaint for a simple offence; and
>
> \(b\) a person who makes an application under this Part is to be treated, as nearly as practicable, as a complainant.

(1A) At the hearing of an application under this Part, the following persons may conduct the applicant\'s or respondent\'s case and examine and cross-examine witnesses:

> \(a\) the applicant or respondent;
>
> \(b\) a lawyer who represents the applicant or respondent;
>
> \(c\) a police officer who represents the applicant or respondent.

(1B) If a police officer could, if available as a witness, give direct oral evidence of a fact in his or her professional capacity in the hearing of an application under this Part, the statutory declaration of the police officer in relation to that fact and made in support of the application is, subject to subsection (1C) , admissible as evidence of the fact in the hearing, notwithstanding that the police officer may be available as a witness.

(1C) A statutory declaration referred to in subsection (1B) is not admissible as evidence in the hearing of an application under this Part if the justices, having regard to all the circumstances, are of the opinion that the representation in the statutory declaration ought not to be admitted without being tested by cross-examination.

(1D) The representation of a medical practitioner in a document tending to establish a fact is admissible as evidence of the fact in the hearing of an application under this Part, notwithstanding that the medical practitioner may be available as a witness, if--

> \(a\) the medical practitioner, in his or her professional capacity, could give direct oral evidence of the fact in the hearing; and
>
> \(b\) the justices, having regard to all the circumstances, are of the opinion that justice does not require that the representation be tested by cross-examination.

[(2) If the justices hearing an application under this Part are satisfied that --]{.mark}

> [(a) a sealed copy of the application has been served on the respondent to the application; or]{.mark}
>
> [(b) reasonable attempts have been made to serve a sealed copy of the application on the respondent --]{.mark}
>
> [they may proceed in the absence of the respondent and may --]{.mark}
>
> [(c) make the order sought in the application or such other order as they consider necessary; or]{.mark}
>
> [(d) issue a warrant for the apprehension of the respondent.]{.mark}

\(3\) If the parties to an application under this Part consent, justices may make an order under this Part in accordance with the terms consented to by the parties.

\(4\) At any time in proceedings under this Part, the justices may determine that it is appropriate that those proceedings be heard and determined by the Magistrates Court (Youth Justice Division) or the Magistrates Court (Children\'s Division) and may transfer the proceedings to that court.

\(5\) If proceedings are transferred to the Magistrates Court (Youth Justice Division) or the Magistrates Court (Children\'s Division) under subsection (4) , that court has jurisdiction to hear and determine those proceedings

**EIC of witnesses can be given by affidavit**

**JUSTICES (RESTRAINT ORDERS) RULES 2013 - REG 8**

**Hearing of applications**

(1)  At the hearing of an application under Part XA of the Act, except the hearing of an application under section 106GA(1) of the Act, evidence may be given by affidavit.

(2)  A person who made an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1) is not required to attend the hearing of the application unless a party to the proceedings, or the justice hearing the proceedings, so requires.

(3)  By notice in writing given to the person who made an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1) , a party to the proceedings may require that the person attend the hearing for cross-examination and, if the person fails to attend, the court may --

\(a\) refuse to allow the affidavit to be used; or

\(b\) allow the affidavit to be used on any conditions the court thinks fit; or

\(c\) adjourn the proceedings until the person attends for cross-examination.

**Oaths Act 2001**

**3.**   **Interpretation**

In this Act --

***affidavit*** includes --

\(a\) any affirmation, statutory declaration or other declaration; and

\(b\) any acknowledgment, examination or attestation; and

\(c\) for the purpose of [[section 7]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-2001-077#GS7@EN) , any deposition or statement made by an informant or a complainant;

**106I.**   **Contravention of restraint order, &c.**

(1)  Where a person in respect of whom a restraint order, interim restraint order or telephone interim restraint order, as made, varied or extended has effect contravenes or fails to comply with the order, that person is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding 10 penalty units or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding 6 months.

(2)  Where a police officer has reasonable cause to suspect that a person has committed an offence under [[subsection (1)]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106I@Gs1@EN) , he may, without warrant, arrest and detain that person and may, for the purpose of arresting and detaining such a person, enter, by force if necessary, any premises on which he has reasonable cause to believe that person is present and search those premises for that person.

(3)  [[Section 34]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS34@EN) does not apply to a person arrested and detained under [[subsection (2)]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106I@Gs2@EN) .

**106J.**   **Power of justices to make orders under this Part at hearing of complaint for offence**

Where, upon the hearing of a complaint for an offence punishable summarily, justices are satisfied on the balance of probabilities as to the matters set out in [[section 106B]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106B@EN) [[(1)]{.underline}](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106B@Gs1@EN) , they may make an order under this Part in addition to any other order which they may make.

# DVO ACT:

**Domestic Violence Orders (National Recognition) Act 2016**

Proclaimed on the **25-11-17** *(Proclamation under the Domestic Violence Orders (National Recognition) Act 2016)*

(second reading speech on file)

**For - registered orders prior to this:**

Interstate order registered under section 27 on 7-9-17 valid until 29-8-19. That was order was made on an application lodged on her behalf by Tasmania Police

Once registered, the order has the same effect as an order made under the Family Violence Act 2004 and can be enforced as if it were a tas fvo(section 28)

Section 29 states that an app to vary can be made under the prescriptions of section 20 (as if it was always a tas order).

Section 35 -- concerning breaches, states at sub-s 4, that a breach of a registered order is to be treated under the same provisions.

THIS WAS THE CASE prior to the introduction of thr National recognition act --

Meaning of "external family violence order" was once other states, now only NZ orders

Under new act -- local dvo is defined as an FVO made under the act (as opposed to definition of FVO under FV act which is only an order made under s 16. The DVO is broader. Local DVO includes registered order prior to FV and DVO intro and amendment.

CHARGE: Breach of a Registered External Family Violence Order

BREACH OF: Section 35(1) of the Family Violence Act, 2004

Particulars:

On the 7^th^ of September 2017 in the Magistrates Court in Devonport, Tasmania, an order for the registration of an external Family violence order under section 27 of the Family Violence Act 2004 was made on application 85227/2017, which included in that order

## Does not automatically apply to interstate DVOs made prior to intro of DVO Act

Under Tas legislation, enforceability of interstate orders prior to the commencement of our DVO Act is limited to orders defined as "recognised" in that jurisdiction. Unless from Victoria, prior interstate orders need to be "declared." Victoria is unique in that their definition of "recognised DVO" includes all prior ones. This is different to what amounts to a "recognised DVO" in all other states.

## Making of new order when interstate DVO exists 

Messages received

### How to make a new IFVO whilst not "revoking" interstate order. 

Effect would be that Tas IFVO supersedes interstate order. If IFVO lapses, than interstate order remains.

- Section 10 defines ***recognised*** DVOs

- Section 10(1)(b) includes ***interstate*** DVOs

- Section 6 defines types of interstate DVOs (and included as few interim orders)

- Section 13 says new ***recognised*** DVO supersedes ***comparable*** DVO

- 13(8) defines comparable DVO as one involving same parties

  - NEW DVO DOES NOT SUPERSEDE where effect would be to exclude protection for a person on original order

  - Section 14 allows local DVO to be made on application whether or not there is a recognised one in force from another jurisdiction

- Tas PFVO -- DOES NOT supersede an interstate DVO made by a court!!!!!!!! For all other court orders, the mos recent in time prevails, generally.

## No averment provision for interstate DVO

AFTER REVIEW (2 Sep 22), I now disagree with the below -- Section 17 of the DVO(NR) Act say that a non-local can be dealt with as a local. That means that a breach of non-local act is dealt with under s. 35(1) of the FVO act. Because it is now dealt with under s35(1), it enlivens s.35(3) -- the averment provision. Notice is still required, but dealt with as per below with a certificate from another state.

Section 35(3) of the* Family Violence Ac*t (Tas) says, among other things:

 

**\"*In any proceedings for an offence under s 35*** (which is what we charge interstate order breaches under), ***an allegation in the complaint is evidence that an FVO has been made against the person charged with an offence; that the order was in force at the time of the alleged contravention and that the condition alleged to have been breached formed part of the FVO.\"***

 

The point of this sub-section was to save us the hassle of having to prove a valid order in every complaint for a breach.

 

***\"FVO\"*** is specifically used in the sub-section.

 

***\"FVO\"*** is defined int the *Family Violence Ac*t (TAS) as \"***an FVO made under section 16 of the Family Violence Act (Tas)\"***

 

So, I don't think we can rely on this evidentiary provision in the *FV Act (Tas)* because this is a QLD DVO\...

 

Recourse then to the *Domestic Violence Orders (National Recognition*) Act\...

 

The relevant sections are ss 4, 10, 15, 16, 17 and 31 of the DVO Act

* *

**Section 4:** Interpretation section  

 

**Section 10(b):** Recognises interstate DVOs made in participating Jurisdiction (QLD is one such jurisdiction)

 

**Section 15(3):** Says that a recognised DVO is enforceable when the defendant is ***properly notified*** of the making of the DVO under the law of the jurisdiction it was made (QLD)

 

**Section 16(2):** Defines "***properly notified***" in the context of interstate DVOs. Basically it requires compliance with the requirements of the State's legislation under which the order was made (QLD). In Tas it would have to be served or made in the defendant\'s presence. I imagine most other States are the same. 

 

**Section 17(1):** Says that where there is a breach of a recognised, enforceable, DVO, it can be enforced as if it were a breach of an FVO under the *FV Act* (Tas).

 

**Section 31(1):** Evidentiary provision as to "***proper notification***". Basically, it says that an authorised officer under another jurisdiction\'s legislation can produce a certificate to the effect that the making of a DVO has been ***properly notified*** under the laws of that jurisdiction. The certificate is evidence of the matter contained in it.

 

**Section 31(4):** says that if the certificate is signed by a person purporting to be an authorised officer that. in itself, is evidence that they were an authorised officer.

 

**Section 31(5):** defined authorised officer in another jurisdiction as being "...a person (whether or not designated as an authorised officer) who is authorised under the law of another jurisdiction to issue a certificate certifying that the making or variation of a DVO has been properly notified under the law of that jurisdiction"

 

So, to prove ***proper notification*** of an interstate DVO we need to identify that jurisdictions authorised officers, and get a certificate from them.

 

BUT, even if we do that, the *DVO Act* only provides an evidentiary provision to the effect of proving that the interstate DVO was ***properly notified***.

 

WHAT WE ARE STILL MISSING, is an equivalent to section 35(3) of our *FV Act*. That is, an evidentiary provision as to the validity, conditions, and force of the DVO at the time of offending. 

My reading is that we would need admissible evidence from the court of the specific jurisdiction to prove this, and that we can\'t simply rely on the averment in the complaint, as we do with local orders. 

Rene576@yahoo.com

# Driving:

***Cooley v Lowe***

For it to be proven that person was 'driving' a motor vehicle the following matters are to be considered,

- The degree to which the 'driver' was operating the controls of the motor vehicle (steering, brakes etc.)

- The distance travelled.

- The road conditions, (undulations, corners etc.)

- The speed of the vehicle

- If the vehicle was being towed (as in this case), the length of the tow rope, communication (if any) between the vehicles, the manner in which the towed vehicle was steered and stopped

*... "driving"*, requires proof that the person charged *"\...had the capacity to do so, and that there was at least a reasonable possibility, if *not* likelihood, that he would attempt to drive the vehicle and so endanger the public\..." *in accordance with the Full Court decision in *Cooley v Lowe *\[1984\] Tas R 107.[^\[2^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/17.html#fn2)

\[27\] In my view, when parliament amended the extended definition of *"driving"* by introducing s 2(4) and s 2(5), it did not intend to overturn the effect of the Full Court's decision in *Cooley v Lowe*. Indeed the amended definition is entirely consistent with the approach taken in that case.* *This view is reinforced by the nature of the Full Court's reasoning in that case where the Court approved what was said by Morris CJ in *Smith v* *Westell.*[^\[14\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/17.html#fn14) In the relevant passages, the Court emphasised that the words extending the meaning of *"driving"* still require proof of a link between;

a.  The acts or omissions contemplated by the definition provisions; and

b.  The possibility or likelihood that the person will move the vehicle.

**Barnes v Dale \[2020\] TASMC 17 (7 October 2020)**

\[28\] My analysis is that despite the broad and inclusive amended definition in the Act, it remains the case that I am bound to decide this case by asking the questions posed in *Cooley v Lowe*; whether the defendant had the capacity to and that there was at least a reasonable possibility, if not likelihood, that he would attempt to drive the vehicle and so endanger the public. Whether the relationship between the defendant and his motor vehicle can be said to be *"driving"* is a question of fact to be determined in the circumstances of the particular case. I now turn to answer that question.

***public street*** means any street, road, lane, thoroughfare, footpath, bridge, or place open to or used by the public, or to which the public have or are permitted to have access, whether on payment of a fee or otherwise;

# Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000

### Evade Police

NEED TO GET EVIDENCE OF POLICE REASON FOR ATTEMPTED INTERCEPT

People never understand what it's like being cooked for when you're a chef. It's the best thing ever! We just love the fact someone is doing it for us. Even if it's fucking toast, it's amazing. Its just the gesture of it that we love. Honestly, quality doesn't even factor into it. It's just the effort that we apricate it.

**A v Roughan \[2019\] TASSC 17**, particularly at \[11\]-\[16\], Brett J dealt with the meaning of the provision creating this offence, and he set out the applicable principles which are binding on my determination of this case:

.\"Evade police

\[11\] The relevant provision of s 11A(1) is as follows:

\'(1) The driver of a vehicle must not take action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer who is exercising his or her powers or performing his or her functions under any Act.\'

\[12\] The submissions of each counsel were based on what was contended to be the proper interpretation of this provision. The starting point of statutory interpretation must be to give legal effect according to the plain and ordinary meaning of the text, read in the context of the surrounding provisions and the legislative scheme: Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority \[1998\] HCA 28; (1998) 194 CLR 355. Although the provision is inelegantly drafted, there does not seem to be any great difficulty in attributing meaning to the simple words contained in s 11A(1). Accordingly, in order to establish the offence of evade police, the prosecution must prove that:

\(a\) the driver of a vehicle;

\(b\) has taken action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer;

**(c) who is exercising his or her powers or performing his or her functions under any Act.**

\[13\] The term \'driver\' is defined by s 3 of the Act to mean \'a person having control over the steering, movement or propulsion of a vehicle and includes a person riding a vehicle\'. This definition suggests a clear nexus between the driving of the vehicle and the taking of action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. This conclusion is consistent with the long title of the Act which is as follows:

\'An Act to confer additional powers on police officers to stop and search vehicles and to obtain information concerning the identity of drivers and owners of, and passengers in or on, vehicles used in connection with the commission of certain offences, and to provide for related matters.\'

\[14\] I am not satisfied, however, that the nexus requires the evasion be constituted solely by an act of driving, nor that the evasion and the driving must be contemporaneous and co-extensive. The requirement is that the driver of the vehicle takes action to avoid apprehension or interception. Taking action can involve a single act or a course of conduct. The use of that term without qualification is consistent with a legislative intention to prohibit any form of action which is intended to achieve evasion of a police officer who is acting within the law. The focus is not on the nature or effectiveness of the action, but rather its purpose. It is immaterial that the action is, or is not likely to be successful, in achieving the evasion of the police officer. In the second reading speech which accompanied the amending Act which inserted s 11A into the Act, the then Minister for Police and Emergency Management gave examples of the type of situation in which the section might operate:

\'This provision will apply to situations where a driver becomes aware of a police presence and then takes action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. An example of such an offence includes drivers who turn their vehicle around, or pull over and run from their vehicle, when they are approaching a random breath test site, or drives in such a manner as to attempt to evade, elude and or prevent police from speaking to the driver or occupants.\'

\[15\] Accordingly, provided that the Court is satisfied that the action taken by the driver of a vehicle is for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception by a police officer, it is not necessary that the evasion occur by the driving of the vehicle, nor that the vehicle is being driven throughout the act of evasion. It is necessary only that the purpose of the action taken be to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. The required nexus between the driving of the vehicle and the taking of action to avoid apprehension or interception will be satisfied if the Court can conclude that the action was taken by a person in his capacity as a driver of the vehicle, but this does not necessarily mean that he or she must actually be driving the vehicle at all times, or even at any time, throughout the evasion. Generally, as a practical consequence of this nexus, some contemporaneity can be expected between the actual driving of the vehicle and the action in question. However, because the taking of action can be constituted by a course of conduct, it is not necessary that the entirety of the relevant action be constituted by the driving of the vehicle. Hence, as in the example given by the Minister in the second reading speech, it would be sufficient if the driver has stopped the vehicle and decamped from it in order to avoid apprehension or interception. It is also possible to contemplate circumstances in which the evasive action commences after the driving has come to an end. An example might be a driver who leaves the scene immediately after an accident, for the purpose of evading apprehension by police who are approaching or already present at the scene, but who prior to the driving coming to an end upon the occurrence of the accident was not evading police.

"\[16\] The requirement that the evasion be of a police officer performing his or her functions under any Act, imports a requirement that the taking of action must be for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception by an actual police officer[. **The prosecution must prove that there is a police officer, that he or she is exercising power or performing functions under any Act**]{.mark}, and that it is the apprehension or interception by that police officer that the defendant has taken action to evade. It would not be sufficient, for example, that the person believes that police might be in the area, when that is not actually the case. On the other hand, it is not necessary for that police officer to have formed an intention to, nor be actually attempting to apprehend or intercept the driver. **[The power or function does not necessarily need to relate to the apprehension or interception of the driver]{.mark}. **The requirement that the driver take action to avoid apprehension or interception by the police officer refers to the subjective purpose of the driver, and does not rely upon or circumscribe the intentions of the police officer. **[It must be independently shown that the police officer is exercising powers or functions under an Act and, provided that is shown]{.mark},** it is irrelevant whether the driver has correctly determined that the police officer intends to apprehend or intercept him or her. The offence is established when the driver of the vehicle takes action for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception, whether or not that prospect is, in fact, about to occur."

### Passenger:

Moving from driver's seat to passenger seat, and allowing someone else to drive off can still amount to an evade: **Police v Quinn \[2019\] TASMC 4 **

Aggravated Evade Police

Most circumstances that lead to a 2A charge must exist when driver is taking action to avoid WHILST DRIVING.

So, lets say he is driving, Cop lights go on. He pulls over and gets out of the car THEN takes action to evade by running away, it's not an Agg evade for most of the 2A cricumsatnces, as "the ACT" needs to occur WHILST driving.

Unless you can support the contention that the pulling over of the car was part of a course of conduct to evade.

The above was just comments by the chief Magistrate.

### Sentencing submissions for aggravated evade

**Murray v Tasmania Police \[2022\] TASSC 1 **

Para \[55\] The magistrate correctly identified the purpose of the law as one to protect the public against the risk involved in a police pursuit and to facilitate the enforcement of the law. When subs (2A) was inserted into s 11A of the Act, the penalties for a breach of s 11A(1) -- evading police -- were increased. In the second reading speech the Minister noted that evading police had emerged as a significant safety problem for Tasmania Police and the whole Tasmanian community, and referred to the need for there \"to be a more significant deterrent, as drivers should not see any advantage in evading police, and repeat offenders should be discouraged from undertaking a cost-benefit analysis of not stopping\".

Para \[56\] The penalties prescribed by [s 11A(2A)](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ppia2000358/s11a.html) of the *[Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ppia2000358/) *are indicative of the Parliamentary view of this type offending. For a first offence the penalty is a fine of not less than 20 penalty units and not more than 100 units, or a term of imprisonment not exceeding three years or both. For a second or subsequent offence the penalty is a fine within the same range or a term of imprisonment for not more than five years, or both. Such legislative changes and the maximum penalties set for a particular offence provide \"legislative guideposts\" to a court, and are matters to which a court should have regard: *Muldrock v The Queen *[\[2011\] HCA 39](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2011/39.html), [244 CLR 120](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=244%20CLR%20120) at [\[27\]](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2011/39.html#para27); *Banks v Tasmania* [\[2019\] TASCCA 1](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2019/1.html), [31 Tas R 342](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=31%20Tas%20R%20342) at [\[33\]](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2019/1.html#para33).

#### Illicit drugs or drunk 

Proof of drugs needs to come from blood test! Oral not enough until amendments are made (as of 2019). See Southern Pross notice below:

The creation of the offence of evade police with aggravated circumstances is found in the *Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000*. The offence came into effect on 13 September 2017. One of the conditions that meets aggravating circumstances, is that the driver had an illicit drug present in his or her breath or blood in contravention of section 6A of the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*.

Since that time, amendments to the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970* have come into that allow oral fluid to be taken for drug testing, instead of a blood sample. These amendments were written and came into effect on 3 December 2018, over a year after the amendments creating the offence of evade police with aggravating circumstances came into being. This means the evade offence still requires the driver to have an illicit drug in their breath or blood. It does not cater to an illicit drug located in oral fluid.

Until the *Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000* can be amended, members will be required to conduct a blood test if they are looking to charge a driver with Evade (Aggravated Circumstances) if the sole condition they are relying on the driver having an illicit drug in their system. Oral fluid testing can still be conducted as usual for all offences under the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*.

*Andrew Keane, Inspector, Southern Regional Prosecution Services*

\[27\] In my view, when parliament amended the extended definition of *"driving"* by introducing s 2(4) and s 2(5), it did not intend to overturn the effect of the Full Court's decision in *Cooley v Lowe*. Indeed the amended definition is entirely consistent with the approach taken in that case.* *This view is reinforced by the nature of the Full Court's reasoning in that case where the Court approved what was said by Morris CJ in *Smith v* *Westell.*[^\[14\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/17.html#fn14) In the relevant passages, the Court emphasised that the words extending the meaning of *"driving"* still require proof of a link between;

b.  The possibility or likelihood that the person will move the vehicle.

Whether the relationship between the defendant and his motor vehicle can be said to be *"driving"* is a question of fact to be determined in the circumstances of the particular case. ### DUI

See **Kerr v McLennan \[2011\] TASMC 21 (20 April 2011)**

Whether driver was under the influence to such a degree as to be incapable of exercising proper control of vehicle

**And Police v Brown \[2008\] TASMC 7**

#### Recent Magi decision

***Police v Brown* \[2008\] TASMC 7**

**The law**

\[8\]. The relevant principles applicable in this case are these:

Intoxication -- under the influence

\- The question whether a person is under the influence of intoxicating liquor is a pure question of fact. What must be proved is that the defendant was influenced by intoxicating liquor - that is - that his reactions were affected; *Richardson v Shipp* \[1970\] Tas SR 105, at 112; *R v Burnside* \[1962\] VR 96

\- It is recognised that there are indicia, being certain observable abnormalities of behaviour and certain physical signs, which evidence when a person who has ingested alcohol has become \"influenced\" by alcohol. If there is accepted evidence of sufficient manifestation of these indicia, one is entitled to conclude (in the absence of evidence to the contrary), that the indicia are the effect of the alcohol and that the subject person, no matter what his tolerance to alcohol, is \"under the influence\"; *O'Connor v Shaw* \[1958\] Qd R 384 at 386.

\- The extent of the defendant's intoxication, i.e. whether he is so much under the influence as to be incapable of having proper control of the vehicle, is a question of fact upon which the court must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt; *Pulleine v Button* \[1948\] SASR 1 at 7.

*Proper control*

\- The standard of \"*proper control\"* is an objective standard, a standard common to drivers as a whole and not to the individual driver. Whether a person is under the influence of intoxicating liquor to the requisite extent must be judged by an objective test. The standard is that control which a driver of ordinary reasonable prudence and ability ought exercise while driving his/her motor vehicle; *Lowe v Suckling* \[1983\] Tas R 116 (NC) N9 (Unrep. 23/1983 at 9), approving *Lysaght v Police* \[1965\] NZLR 405 (CA) (see 409, 414).

\- Actual direct observation of the defendant's lack of ability to control the vehicle is not essential -- the inference may be drawn from his behaviour: *Pulleine v Button (supra)* at 6-7.

\- The court may reach the requisite state of satisfaction upon inferences drawn from evidence other than actual driving; or, notwithstanding evidence of acts of apparently normal driving by the defendant; *Pulleine v Button (supra)* at 7; *Lowe v Suckling (supra).*

### Public road (driveway)

**Brown v Maxwell \[2009\] TASSC 36**

*\[24\] For all of these reasons, I conclude that s6(1) and 6 (2) do not apply only to driving on public streets, but also apply to driving on private land. It was therefore not necessary for the learned magistrate to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the applicant drove on a public street.*

## Timeline of THC

There is a good case of **De Wit v Ali \[2021\] TASMC 10 (2 December 2021),** where Craig Gardner gave evidence that to be detectable weed generally has to be smoked within 6 hours to be at a reportable level.

## Passive ingestion of THC

Craig Gardner from FFST in an email dated 3 Auguste 2018:

Review of the literature suggests that although passive exposure can cause positive results for THC in blood, this is only likely within a few hours of exposure.

THC is rabidly metabolised, an in occasional smokers is typically only detectable for 4-8 hours, dose dependant.

## Prescribed/ prescription THC

The Department of Health's Pharmaceutical Services Branch (PSB) is responsible for administering the *Poisons Act 1971* (Act) and *Poisons Regulations 2018* (Regulations). This includes the maintenance of Tasmania's real-time prescription monitoring (RTPM) systems.

The records of the supply to patients of all Schedule 8 (narcotic) substances and prescribed monitored medicines are sent to PSB by Tasmanian pharmacies. These records show who received, who prescribed, where and when these substances were dispensed. The records of all authorities to prescribe narcotic substances, issued to prescribers under the Act, are also kept on the database.

Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis is a narcotic substance and I can advise there is no record on our RTPM systems of Mr Woodfield being dispensed Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis in Tasmania from community pharmacies.

**Subject:** Prescribed medication and Meth

Good morning, Craig,

Can you please tell me whether a driver taking the below medications, alone or in combination, in any prescribed quantity, could return a positive result for the presence of Methamphetamine in an analysis of oral fluid?   

Paracetamol + Codeine

Paracetamol + Pseudoephedrine hydrochloride

Diazepam

Citalopram

Oxycodone

**From:** Gardner, Craig \<<Craig.Gardner@fsst.tas.gov.au>\>\
**Sent:** Tuesday, February 14, 2023 9:41 AM\
**To:** Hogan, Thomas \<<thomas.hogan@police.tas.gov.au>\>\
**Subject:** RE: Prescribed medication and Meth

Hi Tom,

None of the listed drugs would lead to a positive methylamphetamine result in a sample of oral fluid analysed by FSST. Pseudoephedrine may produce a roadside positive result on the Drugwipe 5S, but laboratory instrumentation used in the analysis is able to distinguish methylamphetamine from pseudoephedrine.

The presence of methylamphetamine indicates an administration of methylamphetamine.

Regards

**ALSO -- oral advice from Craig -- Lexapro (escitalopram) and Serequel (Quetiapine) will not give positive results either.**

**ALSO -- another email once sent to Ang Phipps Zoloft (sertraline) is way different. and Duromine (phentermine) will give positive lick test but not analysis.**

Also on advice from Juliette Cunningham [CLOMIPRAMINE]{.mark} wont. May lead to

## Passive ingestion of Meth

Miriam Connor from FFST in an email dated 29 May 2018:

Methylamphetamine is heated to become a vapour which is then actively breathed into the lungs. It does not exist in the air, like smoke from cigarettes or cannabis -- therefore it cannot be inhaled passively. There is no scientific literature or research into passive methylamphetamine exposure, as it is just not possible to passively inhale methylamphetamine vapour, from our understanding. It will be difficult to write a proof for this, given there is no scientific evidence, but the physical characteristics of methylamphetamine mean that it does not exist in a gas form, it has to be heated to form a vapour.

**From** Cunningham, Juliette (FSST) on 31 May 2017

ng our advice on passive inhalation of methamphetamine?

 

Further to this, the only other information that I believe could be of help to you is that methylamphetamine hydrochloride (commonly referred to as crystal meth) the form typically "smoked" has a melting point of between 170-175 degrees. Obviously it will vaporise above this temperature but this gives an indication that methamphetamine is not just floating around in the air of a room at ambient temperature when people are using it.  It will, in fact, easily condense on the cooler parts of the glass smoking devices used before it has any chance of "escaping" into a room -- at any significant/detectable level. Meth and THC differ considerably in how they are "smoked". Cannabis is typically literally burnt as per a cigarette so that the smoke is inhaled/exhaled. Meth is heated until it changes from a solid to a liquid to a gas and this is what is inhaled . Smoke from a cannabis joint can be long lasting (given the right conditions e.g. lack of ventilation), meth vapour is not the same.

 

## Drug pattern defence:

THC positive usually indactes use within 6 hours. After that usually undectable regardless of subjective charactersitcs.

Mistake of fact for timing of drug use

See **De Wit v Ali \[2021\] TASMC 10 **

Def argued 4 days had passed -- expert evidence said anything beyond 6 hours likely to meet cut off for analaysis

## Questions to be put to FFST Analyst in EIC:

[Evidence of the FSST Analyst -- if you put the report in the 'mail out' tray yourself]{.underline}

Q -- Can you please tell me you name and occupation please?

A -- My name is ........... and I am a Forensic Scientist employed by Forensic Science Service Tasmania.

Q -- Can you please tell me what your duties at FSST are?

A -- My duties include but are not limited to receiving, preparing and analysing Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 blood samples for alcohol and/or illicit drugs as well as the preparation and review of reports for Police and the Coroner.

Q -- Please tell me what qualifications you have to carry out those duties.

A -- Detail education and experience etc. and the fact that you are an 'approved analyst' pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970, such appointment being declared by the Governor in the Government Gazette on .../.../... (date).

**Just the month and year will be fine if you don't recall the exact date.**

Q -- You are aware that you have been summoned to give evidence in relation to ..... .... (this defendant) and the analysis of his/her blood sample?

A -- Yes

Q -- Do you have an independent recollection of what you did in relation to the defendant's blood sample?

A -- No I do not, as I am involved in the analysis of (....) samples of blood each year.

Q -- Do you make notes or keep records?

A -- Yes, we have instrument print-outs for each sample and keep a case file including all relevant paperwork including submitted forms and reports etc. which are subsequent used to create the 'Report of Analysis'.

Q -- Have you reviewed your notes in the last 24 hours?

A -- Yes, several times.

Q -- Has reading your notes refreshed your memory so that you can recall what you did in relation to this defendant's blood sample?

A -- No it has not.

**I will seek leave for you to be able to either use that document to refresh your memory, or read from it pursuant to section 32(3) of the Evidence Act.**

**At this point the court will allow you to read directly from your notes or the report of analysis to give your evidence about the analysis of the blood sample.**

Q - Are you aware of your obligation pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act in relation to service of the \'Report of Analysis\'?

 

A -- Yes, my obligations are that I must cause it to be served on that person within 21 days, pursuant to section 13B of the Act.

 

Q - Do you recall what you did with the report relating to the report in this matter?

 

A -- I do not have an independent recollection of what I did with the report as is was some time ago and I complete many such reports as part of my duties.

Q - What is your usual practice when you complete a report?

A -- I am one of (\...\....) scientists in FSST responsible for analysing about (\...\....) blood samples every year.  As such I do not have a direct recollection of what I did with this report.  What I can say is, it is standard practice that a colleague, in this instance that was (.... ....) checks the report (peer review). The purpose for this is checking for errors and peer review.

I know that the peer review/checking was done because the records reflect that (.... ....) did this. I then made a copy for internal filing and placed it into the 'out' tray in the administration section so it will be posted. I am certain that I would have done this in this instance as I never deviate from this procedure after completing a Report of Analysis.

[Evidence of the FSST Analyst -- if you gave the report to someone else (another analyst) who put it in the mail out tray]{.underline}

Q - Are you aware of your obligation pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act in relation to service of the \'Report of Analysis\'?

 

A -- Yes, my obligations are that I must cause it to be served on that person within 21 days, pursuant to section 13B of the Act.

 

Q - Do you recall what you did with the 'Report of Analysis' that relates to the defendant in this matter?

 

A -- I do not have an independent recollection of what I did with the report as is was some time ago and I complete many such reports as part of my duties.

Q -- How many analysists are there at FSST analysing blood samples for alcohol and drugs, and how may are completed each year?

A -- I am one of (\...\....) scientists in FSST responsible for analysing about (\...\....) samples every year.  As such I do not have a direct recollection of what I did with this report.  

Q - What can you tell me if there is a standard practice for FSST analysists when you complete a report?

A - It is standard practice that I have a colleague check the report (peer review) for accuracy and errors. If they are satisfied with its accuracy then it is their responsibility to make a copy for internal filing and places the 'Report of Analysis' into the 'mail out' tray in the administration section so it will be posted. It is my expectation that this standard practice is carried out by whoever checks my reports and it is my belief that all staff are aware of their obligations. In this instance the records show that (.... ....) was the colleague that was responsible for these duties.
