Tender certificate 

Refined updated 2022 questions:

**Speeding -- Prosecution points of proof for Radar and Laser devices**

1\. Identification of the driver

2\. The speed zone -- was it a built up area or were there speed signs?

3\. Do the speed signs comply with the legislation in size (see Rules 21, 22, 23, and 24 of the Road Rules 2019)?

How were they displayed, were they clearly visible? Describe the signs.

4\. Was the device used a radar speed analyser, or a laser speed analyser? (see regulation 6 of the *Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations* 2022

5\. Was the device tested within the last 12 months? - see regulation 8(1)(a)

6\. Has it been repaired, altered or adjusted? - see regulation 8(1)(b)

7\. Was the device sealed so its speed computing circuitry can not be interfered with? - see regulation 8(1)(c)

8\. Do we have proof of the device having been tested in the last 12 months?

\- see regulation 9

9\. Was the testing completed by an employee of

\- a school/uni etc of a registered higher education provider etc., or

\- an organisation accredited by the NATA, Australia

\- an organisation approved by the commissioner

\- see regulation 9

10\. Tender the regulation 11 certificate.

11\. When the police officer gives evidence of the serial number of the device it must match the serial number/s on the device certificate. The witness can not give the device ID number, it must be the serial number.

12\. Was the police officer suitably trained and/or experienced in the use of the device? See *Wheaton v Wilson* \[2017\] TASSC 40

13\. Tender the witness' training certificate/letter.

# Monetary Penalties Enforcement Act 2005 (MPES)

Application to set aside statutory conviction: Section 40. Need to satisfy court that there is valid reason for delay in electing court hearing and that there is a prima facie defence.

**Marriage v Tasmania Police \[2020\] TASMC 4 (6 April 2020)**

App can be consented to by prosecution. There is no need for court to be independently satisfied of anything if there is consent.

# Police Offences Act 

### Evidentiary provisions 

Age

**Police Offences Act 1935**

**67. Averment of age *prima facie* proof**

In any proceedings under this Act an averment in the complaint as to the age of any person shall be deemed to be proved in the absence of proof to the contrary.

## Failure to state name and address 

See **Arnesto v Hickman \[2016\] TASSC 26**

## Disorderly / public nuisance etc

Section 13 etc POA

**McDonald, Scott Raymond v Sherrin, Robyn Lynette \[1998\] TASSC 126 (14 October 1998)**

*When dealing with a charge involving disorderly conduct, the correct approach to the evidence is as stated in Watson v Trenerry, an unreported decision of the Supreme Court of the Northern Territory dated 3 October 1997 where Martin CJ said:*

*\"It is for the tribunal of fact to say whether matters established before the tribunal or any of them in fact constitute disorderly behaviour (per Zelling J in Samuels v Hall [\[1969\] SASR 296](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1969%5d%20SASR%20296?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) at p309, and Mitchell J pointed out in Ellis v Fingleton [(1972) 3 SASR 437](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281972%29%203%20SASR%20437?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) at 440 that \'Whether conduct is disorderly \... is to be decided by an objective test.\' It is a matter of judgment for the court drawing upon \'its own experience and knowledge of life\' per Bray CJ in Romeyko v Samuels [(1972) 2 SASR 529](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281972%29%202%20SASR%20529?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) at p564.\"*

**Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan, Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan, Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden, David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse Files \[1997\] TASSC 52 (20 May 1997)**

*60... "In order to sustain a charge under the Act, s13(1)(c) it is sufficient to show that the conduct complained of, according to the time, place and circumstances, offends against public decorum and is calculated to disturb, alarm or annoy (Kruger v Humphreys [\[1968\] SASR 75)](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1968%5d%20SASR%2075?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13)"*

**McDonald, Scott Raymond v Sherrin, Robyn Lynette \[1998\] TASSC 126 (14 October 1998)**

The word \"disorderly\" is not defined by the [Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/).

In *Hebble v Phegan* 50/1997 at 13, Zeeman J said of a charge of engaging in disorderly conduct in a public place in breach of the [Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [s13(1)(c)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s13.html) **![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} that in order to sustain the charge \"it is sufficient to show that the conduct complained of, according to the time, place and circumstances, offends against public decorum and is calculated to disturb, alarm or annoy (*Kruger v Humphries* [\[1968\] SASR 75)\".](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1968%5d%20SASR%2075?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13)

*In Barrington v Austin & Ors [\[1939\] SASR 130](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1939%5d%20SASR%20130?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), Napier J at 132 said that disorderly behaviour refers to \"any substantial breach of decorum which tends to disturb the peace or to interfere with the comfort of other people who may be in, or in the vicinity of, the street or public place\". In Melser & Ors v The Police [\[1967\] NZCA 5](http://www.nzlii.org/nz/cases/NZCA/1967/5.html); [\[1967\] NZLR 437](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1967%5d%20NZLR%20437?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13), the judges of the Court of Appeal all expressed views to the effect that conduct which is the subject of a complaint of disorderly conduct must do more than seriously offend against recognised values of orderly conduct and must be likely to cause a disturbance or to annoy others. North P at 443, Turner J at 444 and McCarthy J at 446.*

*In Reeve v Cornish 3/1973, Crawford J relied on Barrington v Austin & Ors (supra) and Melser & Ors v The Police (supra) when considering what amounted to disorderly conduct for the purposes of the [Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), [s10(1)(a).](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s10.html) The passage quoted above from the decision of Napier J in Barrington v Austin & Ors (supra) has been approved by the Full Court in South Australia and favourably cited in nearly all of the authorities I have read on disorderly conduct.*

*It is clear from the authorities I have read that the limits of disorderly behaviour cannot be sharply defined. Subject to that qualification, I proceed on the basis that to obtain a conviction, the applicant had to satisfy the learned magistrate that the conduct complained of, viewed in the light of the surrounding circumstances, was a substantial breach of public decorum which was likely to disturb, alarm or annoy others.*

### Nuisance: 

**Lusted v Van der Meer \[2012\] TASMC 8 (23 February 2012)**

\[25\] *To commit a nuisance contrary to this provision the conduct must amount to a public nuisance. To do so it must materially affect \"Her Majesty\' s subjects generally or at least a class of Her Majesty\'s subjects\"; see Richter v Risby and Others [\[1987\] TASSC 4](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1987/4.html); [(1987) Tas R 36](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281987%29%20Tas%20R%2036?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s13) per Cox J at 30-34. A public nuisance is an act or omission which materially affects the reasonable comfort and convenience of the life of a class of the public. Obstruction of a public highway may amount to such a nuisance, depending upon the degree and length of time and reasonableness of the obstruction. Wallace v Powell and Ors [\[2000\] NSWSC 406.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/nsw/NSWSC/2000/406.html) It is not necessary to have evidence of annoyance, inconvenience or discomfort to particular persons before there can be a conviction; Smith v Cornish [\[1971\] Tas SR 383 (NC](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1971%5d%20Tas%20SR%20383) 17)*

## Dangerous Article

**Police Offences Act 1935 - Section 15C -- Dangerous Article**

**Police Offences Act -- Section 3 -- Interpretation**

*dangerous article* includes --

(a) any weapon other than a firearm; and

(b) any article adapted or modified so as to be capable of being used as such a weapon; and

(c) any other article that is intended to be used as such a weapon; and

(d) a knife

#### Possession:

Not defined in POA. "possession" ordinary means "physical custody or control ***Momcilocvic V the Queen 2011 HCA 34***

#### Requisite belief:

Police officer must believe that the defendant is in possession or carries and dangerous article HELD, in the circumstances, to be based on objectively reasonable grounds.

See ***Tasmanian v Hodges 2022 TASSC***. Belief is something more than mere suspicion but less than actual knowledge based on proof. Ther must be grounds for their belief but certainty and proof are not required.

#### 'Dangerous' and 'Dual Purpose'

***Craig v Lowrey* \[2012\] TASMC 41**

12\. The Oxford Dictionary defines "weapon" as "a thing designed for use for inflicting bodily harm or physical damage".

13\. Turning firstly to the three inclusive definitions set out in section 3, it is probably the case that I would find that the machete is a weapon, despite its dual purpose. Its purpose as a weapon worldwide is well known. Terrible injuries and death have flowed from machete attacks. The blade is known to be long, strong and sharp.

14\. However authorities have held that where there is a **dual purpose**, prosecution is required to prove the intended use as a weapon. See *Chadbourne v Ansell *[\[1975\] WAR 104](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1975%5d%20WAR%20104), *Threfall v Panzera* [\[1958\] VicRp 87](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1958/87.html); [\[1958\] VR 547](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1958%5d%20VR%20547), *Coleman v Zanker* [\[1991\] SASC 3121](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/sa/SASC/1991/3121.html); [(1992) 58 SASR 7.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281992%29%2058%20SASR%207)However these cases deal with different statutory provisions relating to "offensive weapons". This appears to be a higher test than required for part (b) of the definition in this state's Act, where an item is modified so that it is only *capable* of being a weapon. This interpretation would also seem to defeat the work of the "lawful excuse" provision. I do not need to decide this point. The definition is an inclusive one, and therefore the overriding requirement is that the court must be satisfied that it is "a dangerous article" independently of whether or not it falls into the category of a "weapon". This would at face value cover a wider array of items.

15\. The Macquarie dictionary defines "dangerous" as "full of danger or risk; causing danger; perilous; hazardous; unsafe". The Oxford dictionary defines dangerous as "able or likely to cause harm or injury".

#### Dangerous Article (Lawful Excuse)

***Craig v Lowrey* \[2012\] TASMC 41**

20\. The overall effect of the defendant's explanations was that he kept the machete for recreational or farming purposes unrelated to attack or self-defence.

21\. In *Taikato v R* [\[1996\] HCA 28](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1996/28.html) the majority of the High Court in relation to a similar charge discussed the meaning of "lawful purpose". They held that as a general rule interpreting "lawful purpose" in a legislative provision to mean a purpose that is not forbidden, rather than positively authorised by law, is usually preferred. This is because statutes are interpreted in accordance with the presumption that parliament does not take away existing rights, unless it does so expressly or by necessary implication. In this matter it was not argued by prosecution that using the machete for the purposes of a tool, as the defendant describes, was in itself unlawful. It is not prohibited by law and I find that such use would be a lawful activity.

22\. Two decisions of the South Australian Supreme Court assist with the principles to be applied in proving "lawful excuse" in the context of this type of charge. I will set out relevant passages from those decisions.

23\. In *Holmes v Hatton *[18 SASR 412](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=18%20SASR%20412) Wells J stated at p418:

It seems to me that the expression "without lawful excuse" obliges a Court to examine all the circumstances that characterize the purpose and reasons for which the weapon is carried, and to determine, as a matter of fact and degree whether, reasonably appraised, those purposes and reasons constitute lawful excuse or not.

His Honour further stated as p419:

> *Where an offensive weapon is carried knowingly, lawful excuse or its absence is ordinarily to be judged by a consideration if the purpose or purposes, or the reason or reasons, for which the weapon is being carried. (If it should be -- and I leave this point for consideration when it directly arises -- that a man may "carry" a weapon within the meaning of the section even where he is unaware that he is carrying it -- for example, where he is helping to carry another's luggage which, unknown to him contains a gun -- absence of purpose or reason and an inadvertent carrying may amount to lawful excuse).*
>
> *The purpose or reasons for which an alleged offender was carrying a weapon are to be examined and assessed having regard to the united force of all the circumstances put together. If the sole purpose of reason for which the weapon is carried is plainly unlawful or is other-wise contrary to the section, the carrier cannot claim lawful excuse.*
>
> *Where the sole purpose or reason is lawful, there will be lawful excuse. Where there is a duality of purpose or reason, no hard and fast rule can provide a solution. The court, in determining the issue, must, in my opinion, decide a question of fact and degree, and must, in particular, ask whether that which, ex facie and taken in isolation, may be regarded as an unlawful purpose or reason, was, in the circumstances, so transient, so remotely related to the defendant's conduct, so speculative, so unlikely to become translated into action, so subsidiary -- in short, so unimportant or insignificant, as to leave the alleged offender with lawful excuse.*
>
> *The foregoing discussion, it will be observed, has omitted any reference to onus of proof. The application of the provision as to onus of proof should cause little difficulty in practice, but occasional cases, in which dual purpose is under examination, may arise where it will be necessary to keep steadily in mind what the defendant is obliged by [s. 15](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ea200180/s15.html) to prove.*
>
> *Plainly, given that all other elements are proved, if only a single purpose or reason is in issue, and if the defendant proves on the balance of probabilities primary facts that, in law, amount to lawful excuse, he will be acquitted. If proof falls short of that, he must be convicted. If the evidence as a whole raises a question of dual purpose or reason, the matter will, to my mind, stand thus. Where the evidence as a whole includes evidence suggesting an unlawful purpose or reason for carrying as well as of a lawful purpose or reason, in order to acquit the defendant the court must find itself persuaded that, having regard to the cogency with which the relevant facts and circumstance are proved by the evidence as a whole, the unlawful purpose or reason was so unimportant and insignificant that there probably was lawful excuse.*

## CLAMPING & HOONING

### Prescribed offences:

Prescribed offences are prescribed and can be found in Regulation 6 of the *Police Offences Regulations* 2014:

[Section 13](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/vata1999150/s13.html) of the [Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/vata1999150/) (drive disqualified) or [section 19A](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/rsada1970264/s19a.html) of the [Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/rsada1970264/) (drive was disqualified) so long as it is a subsequent offence for either. And a speeding offence above 45kmh

Further prescribed offences in s 37K of the ***POA***

**14B. Unlawful entry on land involving use of vehicle**

**15B. Dispersal of persons involving use of vehicle**

**37J. Excessive noise, smoke, &c., from vehicles**

**48. No motor-vehicle race to be held without a permit**

**32. Reckless driving TRAFFIC ACT 1925**

**11A. Evading police POLICE POWERS (VEHICLE INTERCEPTION) ACT 2000**

### Power to clamp and confiscate vehicles:

Power to do so where there is a prescribed offence or UP TO 14 days after the offending (*POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37N)*

*The period a vehicle can be clamped or confiscated depends on the offence and how many previous offences. They are stipulated in the subdivisions.*

### Mandatory penalty 

Prescribed offences including a vehicle that are "clamped or confiscated" are subject to a "Special Penalty" (s 37MA POA). There is no discretion and it is above any other penalty found in the ***Sentencing Act***.

The amount of the special penalty is found in Regulation 7 of the *Police Offences Regulations* 2014 **[OR]{.underline}** 2024 Subject to the date.

Offending post 12 December 2024 (when the new regs came in) is charged as 4 penalty units./

Offending pre that is \$600.

### Motor Forfeiture Application.

Where third offence -- app can be made *POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37Y)*

**Look at *Perkins v Douglas* \[2010\] TASMC 8 for relevant considerations of the court.**

When preparing for a hearing, get certs of convictions for relevant prescribed offending. Get FFP for this so def can be cross examined on previous driving history in support of the position that the vehicle ought not be returned. Confirm the penalty that was imposed on the conviction that led to the conviction. Not the circumstances of the relevant offending that led to confiscation. The more basic, the less likely that forfeiture will be granted.

If app made by owner (not offender) cx about their knowledge and acquiescence to letting the offender use the car. Did they know that def was unlicenced/ unregistered etc.? Is there a history or pattern of owner allowing def to use car? Did they know they were offending/ likely to offend?

## 37J. Excessive noise, smoke, &c., from vehicles

See **Lane v Kelly \[2010\] TASMC 20**

## Motor Vehicle Stealing -- 

**37B Police offences Act.**

Not like "stealing" in the Code. Need not intend to permanently deprive.

**37B.**   **Motor vehicle stealing**

If serious can be "made a code offence" 37(4)(b)

12 month limitation

- drives or [uses]{.mark}

- a motor vehicle

- without the consent of the owner or registered operator of the vehicle or of some person who is lawfully in charge of the vehicle and has authority to give that consent

- mental element: def must have subjective knowledge that above has not given consent

***Willox v Warren \[1987\] TASSC 74 ***

Para \[4\]

*S. 37B of the Police Offences Act 1935 provides:--*

*"37B--(1) Subject to subsection (2), a person who drives or uses a motor vehicle without the consent of the owner of the vehicle or of some person who is lawfully in charge of the vehicle and has authority to give that consent is guilty of the offence of motor vehicle stealing.*

*(2) Subsection (1) does not apply to or in relation to the driving or use of a motor vehicle by a police officer or an authorized officer acting in the execution of his duty."*

*The essential external elements of this offence are, first, that the defendant drove or used a motor vehicle and, second, that he did so without the consent of the owner or person lawfully in charge of it. To these should be added a mental element, namely that at the time he did so he knew that the owner or person lawfully in charge of it had not given his consent to such use (R. v. Murphy \[1957\] VicRp 75; \[1957\] V.R. 545; Wray v. Robertson \[1970\] Tas.S.R. 253). Stealing on the other hand requires a dishonest taking or conversion with intent to permanently deprive the owner of the thing stolen. [Although the summary offence is described as "stealing", it has that clear generic difference to stealing as defined under the Criminal Code and at common law. It has frequently been said that the offence was introduced to prevent "joy riding"]{.mark} (see Wray v. Robertson (supra) at p.255 and cases there cited).*

**Abandoned?**

Motor vehicle believed abandoned

.

Ref: *Kemp v Shultz*

*Lowe v Radosavljevic*

One case, Magistrate J.P. MORRIS -- (30/3/84) 6/1984 -- Refers to 'Dishonesty'

## False statement:

44A Police Offences Act

Needs to be knowingly false -- exaggeration not enough. Must be about facts -- not law -- must be that an ACT has been done. Saying assaulted me probs not enough. As there is a range of potential meanings in law. He punched me, would be required.

## Willfully obstruct police:

Must be an intent to frustrate as opposed to help. Refusing a direction given amounts to obstruct where police officer trying to arrest or carry out other lawful duty: **Liley v Lowe \[1987\] TASSC 76 (20 March 1987)**

## Stealing:

### Quick differences:

**Stealing is when you take something, robbery is when you take with a threat or actual violence, burg is tress pass (need not take) in any place with intent to commit crime (need not be stealing), agg burg is when you trespass with weapon OR in a place ordinarly used for human habitation OR uses violence or assaults while burg**

**Stealing:**

Take anything capable of being taken with the intent to permanently deprive

***Take*** includes rick, intimidation, mistake of the owner (of known by offender), or by finding (if the owner can reasonably be discovered)

**Robbery/ Stealing by force:**

Stealing with the use of threat or use of violence to person or property, at or around the time of stealing, to get it.

Intent to permanently deprive must exist at time of violence ***Smith-Towns v Tasmania \[2019\] TASCCA 22 (23 December 2019)***

**Burglary:**

***(Not limited to property! It is about location of incident with intent)***

Trespass in a place or conveyance plus an intent to commit a crime.

I think (not 100% but think) that the intent must be a "crime" within the meaning of not a summary offence. See para 15 of **Hurst v Reidy \[2003\] TASSC 107 (30 October 2003)**

**\[15\]** *Counsel for the applicant\'s other submission was that it was reasonably possible that the applicant\'s intention was merely to drive Mr Osok\'s vehicle, which would have amounted to the offence of motor vehicle stealing under the [Police Offences Act 1935](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), *![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}* [s37B.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s37b.html) *![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}* That offence is not a crime. If the applicant entered the vehicle only with that intention, it was not his intention to commit a crime and he could not be found guilty of the crime of burglary, which requires an intention to commit a crime at the time of entry, as one of its elements.*

Ultimtely the judge rejected defence submission because the evidence was enough to say that the def broke into the car with the intent to steal items out of it. Not to just drive it.

**Aggravated Burglary**

Burglary, committed with a weapon; committed with the use or threat of violence **Or** where the trespass is into a place used normally for human habitation.

### Stealing by finding

### Abandonment

Property that has been abandoned can not be stolen (see *post ss* 18 -- 24). If property has not been abandoned, but the defendant believes that it has, under the old law he could not be convicted of theft, whether his belief was reasonable or unreasonable : see *R v White* (1912) 7 Cr App. R 266; *Ellerman's Wilson Line Ltd. V Webster* \[1952\] 1 Ll.L.R. 179, D.C. It is submitted that this is still the law. Whether or not a mistake of fact has to be reasonable (see ss 17-4 *et seq.*), there would be no evidence that the defendant was acting dishonestly.

#### Must take reasonable steps to find owner

***Maingay v Charles \[2009\] TASSC 98***

"The respondent\'s stated belief was in effect that he did not believe he could discover the owner by any reasonable means. While there is no stated requirement in s226 that the belief itself be a reasonable one, it would make a nonsense of the legislation were an alleged offender to simply be able to state that he did not hold the requisite belief, notwithstanding that that stated belief might be entirely inconsistent with the surrounding circumstances, and for that statement to be determinative. Of course, the belief referred to in s226 is one that an owner can be discovered by \"reasonable means\". (see below)"

Maingay also sites Alexander Gordon MacDonald \[1983\] 8 A Crim R 248

(The defendant found foreign currency and kept it, and made no attempt to locate an owner and claimed he believed it was abandoned)

In Alexander Gordon MacDonald \[1983\] 8 A Crim R 248, the Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales considered how the state of mind of a finder of property is to be established when the finder has volunteered no information pointing to their state of mind at the relevant time. The Court (Lee, Maxwell and Yeldham JJ) observed (at p251):

\"The guilty mind or animus furandi requisite in larceny by finding is thus the belief of the finder that the owner can be found, and unless that belief is established beyond reasonable doubt, a conviction for larceny is not open. The Crown of course carries the onus or proving that belief beyond reasonable doubt, and there is no onus on the accused in any circumstances to prove that he did not have such a belief or that he believed the goods had been abandoned, or that the owner could not be found (May v O\'Sullivan \[1955\] HCA 38; (1955) 92 C.L.R. 654). How then is the requisite belief in the finder to be established when the finder has volunteered no information pointing to the state of his mind at the relevant time, as is the case here? In Russell on Crime

(12 th edition) the learned author, at p1014, states:

"In cases of taking on finding some of the strongest circumstances to rebut the implication that such taking was felonious \...are those which show that \[the taker\] endeavoured to discover the true owner and kept the goods till it might reasonably be supposed that the true owner could not be found. The finders belief, in our view, is to be inferred from the facts and circumstances surrounding the finding and the taking of the goods, and in this respect regard may be had not only to what the finder does in relation to the goods but also what he does not do that might reasonably be regarded as consistent with the actions of an honest man finding goods. Did the finder examine it closely to see if it gave any clue to its owner by name tag or otherwise? What avenues were reasonably open to the finder to locate the owner? A person finding goods may be taken to know that the person who has lost those goods may well retrace his steps with a view to recovering them, and so the leaving of the finders name and address with someone at the place of discovery may be one means of locating the

owner. It is common knowledge in our community, and the finder would know, that the police will receive lost property handed in and take care of it and accordingly the finder would think that the loser might make an enquiry of the police in the area where he believes he may have lost his property. It is common knowledge that newspapers publish ,,lost and found columns. These and perhaps other considerations would ordinarily be present to the mind of an honest person finding property in a suburb of Sydney. Each case nmust be looked at according to its own facts, and the place where the property is found and the nature of the property will, in most instances, readily indicate the avenues that are reasonably open to

find the owner.\"

**Criminal Code Act 1924**

**PART VI - Crimes Relating to Property Chapter XXIV - Stealing Interpretation**

**226. Interpretation**

**(1)** A person who, without the consent of the owner thereof, dishonestly --

**(a)** takes; or

**(b)** being lawfully in the possession thereof, either as a servant of the owner or as a bailee or part owner thereof, converts to his own use or to the use of any person other than the owner --

anything capable of being stolen, with intent permanently to deprive the owner thereof, steals such thing.

**(2)** In this chapter --

**(a)** the term ***takes*** includes obtaining possession --

**(i)** by any trick;

**(ii)** by intimidation;

**(iii)** through a mistake on the part of the owner, if the taker knows of such mistake; or

[**(iv)** by finding, if at the time of the finding the taker believes that the owner can be discovered by reasonable means;]{.mark}

**(b)** the term ***owner*** includes any part owner or person having possession or control of, or a special property in, anything capable of being stolen.

**(3)** A servant who takes anything out of the possession of his master with intent to apply it to his master\'s use, and who so applies it, or who converts anything belonging to his master to his master\'s use, does not steal the thing, although such taking or conversion is wrongful.

### Two mental elements: 

  

  

### Dishonestly - s226(1) 

  

- The test is both objective and subjective and it is now clear that courts will apply the *Feely* test without the *Ghosh* gloss. This was accepted by the High Court in *Peters* (1998) 192 CLR 493 and *Macleod* (2003) ALJR 1047. In determining dishonesty it is necessary to: 

  

- Identify the knowledge, belief or intent which is said to render the relevant act dishonest; and 

  

- Instruct the jury to decide whether the accused had that knowledge, belief or intent and, if so, to determine whether, on that account, the act was dishonest; and  

  

- Direct the jury that, in determining whether the conduct of D was dishonest, the standard is that of ordinary and decent people? 

  

  

- There is no additional requirement that D realised he or she was dishonest: *Jovanovic.* 

  

  

  

### Intent to permanently deprive 

  

- An intent to assume dominion over the goods in such a manner as to deny (take dominion over) *Lowe v Hooke.* and not merely entrench upon the rights of the owner *Holloway* (using but not permanently depriving) 

### No limitation period on charges for stealing:

*\[4\] "Stealing is an indictable offence under s 234 of the Code and is not governed by that provision, as it deals with "a simple offence that is not an indictable offence". There is no limit for the bringing of this complaint." **Moore v Howe \[2017\] TASMC 10***

See also section 72C of the *Justices Act*!

## Notice of demand:

Need to have basis to believe occupant of car committed an offence

Can be served on owner or registered operator

"Owner" defined in section 37A of POA. It includes anyone police officer reasonably believes was the driver.

Don't need to prove actual ownership

Can run it on cop alone if they can give evidence of basis of belief (reps made by actual owner not hearsay as per Subremanium rule -- original evidence)

### 37GA POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37GA

A police officer may serve a notice of demand on the owner or registered operator 

***Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999***

***3.   Interpretation***

***registered operator*** of a motor vehicle or a trailer means --

\(a\) the person recorded in the register of motor vehicles and trailers as the person responsible for it; or

\(b\) if the registration of a motor vehicle or a trailer has expired, or been cancelled, the person last recorded as the registered operator unless that person has notified the Registrar that he or she is no longer responsible for the motor vehicle or trailer;

### Difference between requirements under s 37G and 37GA:

***Baker v Smith* \[2025\] TASSC 43**

**Facts**

- Baker's vehicle crashed (Wynyard, 18 Mar 2023). Driver left scene.

- Police served Baker (registered owner) with a **driver demand notice** (s 37GA *Police Offences Act 1935*), requiring details of driver/occupant.

- Baker did not comply.

- Magistrate convicted him (Aug 2024). Baker sought review.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Issues**

1.  Does s 37G require only an **allegation** of an offence, or officer's **satisfaction** of it?

2.  Was there sufficient proof Baker was the **registered owner**?

3.  Was the notice **invalid/ambiguous** (re: offence details; "driver or occupant" wording)?

4.  Did the officer lack **reasonable belief** an offence occurred?

5.  Did issuing the notice unlawfully abrogate Baker's **right to silence/self-incrimination** (given officer was "99.99% sure" Baker was driver)?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Decision**

- **Appeal dismissed** -- conviction upheld.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Ratio**

- **s 37G precondition:** only requires an **allegation**, not proof or officer's satisfaction.

- **Separate offences:** s 37G (demand) and s 37GA (notice) each create distinct offences with different penalties.

- **Ownership proof:** admissions and conduct can suffice; certificate of registration not essential.

- **Notice validity:**

  - "Indicate" = general statement of offence, not exact section.

  - Context showed allegation was against **driver**, not occupant → no ambiguity.

- **Reasonable belief:** Magistrate applied wrong test (belief vs allegation), but no miscarriage of justice.

- **Right to silence:** s 37G clearly abrogates it; no implied "necessity" limit.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Significance**

- Confirms **low threshold** for valid driver demand: mere allegation is enough.

- Clarifies ownership can be proved by **conduct/admissions**.

- Confirms strict but clear statutory abrogation of silence/self-incrimination rights.

- On review, findings will stand if **reasonably open**.

**Case:** *Baker v Smith* \[2025\] TASSC 43

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Issue**                                       **Applicant's Argument**                                                                                                 **Court's Reasoning / Outcome**
  ----------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **1. Preconditions (s 37G vs s 37GA)**          Proof required that officer was *satisfied* an offence occurred (s 37GA(1)), not just an allegation.                     Wrong -- s 37G only requires an *allegation* of an offence. Satisfaction standard in s 37GA not imported. Conviction can rest on allegation alone.

  **2. Ownership**                                No proper evidence Baker was registered owner. Body worn footage not enough; should have had registration certificate.   Ownership can be proved by admissions and context. Baker said "yes" when told he was the owner, nodded during reading of notice. Certificate not essential. Finding was reasonably open.

  **3. Validity / Ambiguity of Notice**           Notice invalid: (a) didn't specify exact offence provision (only Act); (b) ambiguous re: "driver or occupant".           "Indicate" (s 37GA(2)(a)) only requires general statement, not exact section. Offence described (driving under influence). Notice + officer's words made clear it related to *driver*. No ambiguity.

  **4. Reasonable Belief**                        Officer admitted he didn't know who driver was, or if under influence → no reasonable basis to issue notice.             Court: only an *allegation* is required. Even if higher "belief" test applied, magistrate's conclusion (based on CCTV, witness info, leaving scene) was open. No miscarriage.

  **5. Rights to Silence / Self-Incrimination**   Notice invalid because officer said he was "99.99% sure" Baker was driver → unnecessary, undermined rights.              Statute expressly abrogates rights in these circumstances. No "necessity" limit implied. Officer's statement = poor wording, but does not invalidate notice.

  **Overall**                                     Conviction unsafe; notice invalid; ownership not proved.                                                                 Conviction stands. Notice valid. Magistrate's findings reasonably open. Applicant's review dismissed.
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Police Offences Amendment Bill 2014 16 of 2014

Clause notes/ second reading/ fact sheet

<http://www.parliament.tas.gov.au/bills/Bills2014/16_of_2014.htm>

## Unlawful Possession of stolen property

### Elements:

- Satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant had at some point/ or was found by a Police officer in ***possession*** of the goods listed

- Satisfied that the officer, believed, on grounds that I am satisfied were reasonable, that the goods had been stolen or unlawfully obtained

### In possession or "had" in possession 

Section 39 amended by *Police Offences Amendment Act 2014*

Clause 42 of the clause notes states the following (note: no mention in the second reading)

*"Section 39 amended (Possession of stolen property) The clause amends this section so that the belief of a police officer, that a person had possession that property stolen or unlawfully obtained, may exist even after possession or control of that property by the person has passed. The amendment removes the requirement that the belief has to be held during the time the person had possession or control of the property. The belief no longer has to be concomitant with the finding of the person in possession with the property"*

### "Possession" circumstantial evidence only.

**Be careful as to assumptions of possession**

**"recent possession doctrine" does not exist -- it is circumstantial evidence.**

**Roughan v Haines \[2019\] TASMC 7 (25 November 2019)**

*\[17\] Elements (b), (c), (d) and (e) are all circumstantial evidence pointing towards guilt. The so called "doctrine of recent possession" which, if established, presumed guilt and reversed the onus of proof has long been recognised as having no place in modern criminal law: see R v Wanganeen [\[1988\] 50 SASR 433](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1988%5d%2050%20SASR%20433) at 34 to 42; see also Wallis v Tasmania [\[2007\] TASSC 4](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2007/4.html) per Evans J at paragraphs 78 to 80. Therefore, if there is evidence -- as here -- that a defendant was in possession of a lot of property stolen from Begents that is evidence which falls to be considered as circumstantial evidence.*

### Possession 

***Section 1: Interpretation CRIM CODE***

***have in possession*** includes having under control in any place whatever, whether for the use or benefit of the person of whom the term is used or of another person, and although another person has the actual possession or custody of the thing in question;

NOTE -- not a reference to the exact quite ***"have in possession"*** because that phrase of words does not appear exactly anywhere in the act. It would negate the purpose if it were to be limited to that exact phrase.

Possible need not have officer identify basis of belief for each single item listed on complaint... Look at **Unlawful Possession**

*Winwood v Burk* \[2002\] TASSC 54

1.  In the circumstances of this case, it was not necessary that each item be referred to individually before the learned magistrate could be satisfied with respect to the reasonableness of the supposition which Detective Sergeant Burk said he entertained with respect to every item on the list.

### What constitutes possession, custody etc

\"de facto possession and actual control\" and being \"wide enough to include any case where the person alleged to be in possession has hidden the thing effectively so that he can take it into his physical custody whenever he wishes, and where others are unlikely to discover it unless by accident.\")

Williams v. Douglas (1949) 78 C.L.R. 521.

***Biddle v Horton \[2013\] TASMC 1***

\[24\] ... he *had lived there for some time. He clearly controlled the question of entry into the premises in that he answered the door to the police when they knocked. In short, his relationship with the item in question was such that it made his possession of it manifest.* 

***PERNA v POLICE \[2007\] SASC 306***

Held: possession requires consideration of nature and extent of control of goods at relevant time - at relevant time, appellant had complete present personal physical control of property to exlusion of others 

Exceptions/ qualifications:

\[30\] Can be in a public place, if it is hidden or locked, or otherwise only likely to be accessible to the defendant.

\[54\] The appliances were stored in a shed to which Mr Perna had a contractual right of access during business hours. The decision in *Williams v Douglas* demonstrates that the decision in *Moors v Burke* does not mean that a person cannot be in possession of items that are in premises to which the person in question does not have an unrestricted right of access. In *Williams v Douglas* the defendant was a mere lodger, and his entitlement to enter the hotel bathroom was not unlimited. The decision in *Williams v Douglas*also demonstrates that a person can be in possession of an item that is in a place to which other persons have rights of access.

### Possession defined Code principles Apply:

Unlawful is in the Code as well as the POA, so Code principles and definitions apply

Contrast with 15C of POA which has no code parallel. POA doesn't define Possession, but "possession" ordinary means "physical custody or control ***Momcilocvic V the Queen 2011 HCA 34***

**CODE: 258. Receiving stolen property**

\(1\) Any person who, without lawful excuse, receives or has in his possession any stolen property, knowing it to be stolen property, is guilty of a crime.

Charge: Receiving stolen property.

\(2\) For the purposes of the Code, stolen property shall include anything capable of being stolen which has been obtained in this State or elsewhere under circumstances which constitute, or which, if they had occurred in this State would constitute, a crime under any of the provisions of chapters XXIV to XXVII or of this chapter, but shall not include any such thing which, after having been so obtained, has been returned to its owner, or to which any person has acquired a lawful title completed by delivery.

\(3\) In any proceedings under this section --

\(a\) the fact that the accused had in his possession other stolen property which had been obtained as aforesaid within 12 months preceding the date of his alleged crime; and

\(b\) the fact that within 3 years preceding his being charged with the crime the accused had been convicted of a crime under any of the provisions of chapters XXIV to XXVII or of this chapter --

may be given in evidence against the accused in support of the averment of guilty knowledge.

\(4\) The facts mentioned in paragraph (b) of subsection (3) shall not be admissible unless --

\(a\) 7 days\' notice in writing has been given to the accused of the intention to prove such fact; and

\(b\) evidence has previously been given that the stolen property in respect of which the accused is charged was found or had been in his possession.

Section 1 -- Criminal Code

***Interpretation***

### Reasonably Believed

evidence needs to amount to acceptance by the officer - to the exclusion of other propositions - that the property was stolen or unlawfully obtained at para \[23\] **Lane v Jones \[2012\]**

\-

This question is to be assessed subjectively. I note that the requisite state of mind is something more than mere suspicion.

* Lane v Jones [\[2012\] TASMC 11](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2012/11.html),* Deputy Chief Magistrate Daly analysed the meaning of this term as it appears in ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} [s 39](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s39.html) ![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}, [Police Offences Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/). He concluded that a belief must amount \"to acceptance -- to the exclusion of other propositions --\" that the relevant state of affairs exists. With respect, I agree with His Honour\'s analysis and adopt that test in assessing whether Sergeant Orr held the requisite belief.

***Police v Lea \[2008\] TASMC 1 (29 January 2008)***

*8. The Act, s.39(1) provides that a person must not have possession or control of any property that is reasonably believed by a police officer to have been stolen or unlawfully obtained. The Supreme Court has not dealt with any case charged under the amended provision, which came into force on 17 December 2001.*

*9. In Parker v Wood [\[1993\] TASSC 26](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1993/26.html), Slicer J dealt with the test applicable to the words \"reasonably believes\" in the Road Safety Alcohol & Drugs Act 1970, s.8. Slicer J stated in relation to that particular requirement, that a the reason that a constable must hold a reasonable belief is to prevent random and capricious exercises of police powers. The Second Reading Speech is of no assistance whatsoever. However, Slicer J's comments are apposite here in relation to police capriciously or unreasonably charging persons under this provision. Slicer J held that the test in relation to the phrase \"reasonably supposes\" is that in George v Rockett [\[1990\] HCA 26](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/26.html); [(1990) 170 CLR 104](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281990%29%20170%20CLR%20104?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39) at 112:*

*\"When a statute prescribes that there must be \'reasonable grounds\' for a state of mind - including suspicion and belief - it requires the existence of facts which are sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person.\"*

*and at p116:*

*\"The objective circumstances sufficient to show a reason to believe something need to point more clearly to the subject matter of the belief, but that is not to say that the objective circumstances must establish on the balance of probabilities that the subject matter in fact occurred or exists: the assent of belief is given on more slender evidence than proof. Belief is an inclination of the mind towards assenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition and the grounds which can reasonably induce that inclination of the mind may, depending on the circumstances, leave something to surmise or conjecture.\"*

*10. In relation to the degree of suspicion that might be required, in Winwood v Burk [\[2002\] TASSC 54](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2002/54.html), Underwood J, as he then was, said:*

*In O\'Sullivan v Tregaskis [\[1948\] SASR 12](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1948%5d%20SASR%2012?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), Napier CJ saidat 16:*

*\"It is manifestly impossible to enumerate or define all the circumstances that may reasonably attract suspicion to the property, but, for this purpose, regard may be had to the nature of the property, to the circumstances in which it has been found, and to the behaviour of the defendant, with respect to it. (Ex parte Patmoy [(1944) 44 SR (NSW) 351](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281944%29%2044%20SR%20%28NSW%29%20351?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39) at p 357; LePoidevin v Hudson [\[1935\] SASR 223](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1935%5d%20SASR%20223?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), at p 230).*

*Speaking generally, it seems to me that suspicion is commonly, and not unreasonably, incited by the impression of abnormality or incongruity in the thing being in the possession of the person who has it, or in his dealings with it.\"*

*11. In Burnett v Brown [(1929) 24 Tas LR 23](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASLawRp/1929/2.html), Clark J, at 34, said with respect to the Police Act 1905, s16, an earlier Tasmanian enactment similar to s.39:*

*\"The words \'suspicion\' and \'suspect\' convey the idea of doubt and mistrust as much as belief, a doubting and mistrusting inclination to believe. The inclination may exist in various degrees according to the cogency of the evidence, but the idea of doubt and mistrust is always present.\"*

CONTRA

***Rolls v Richardson \[2014\] TASMC 12 (23 May 2014)***

Basically -- refusing to consent to a search is not sufficient grounds, and the belief must be independatly held based on reasonbale investigation. A call from a radio room probs not enough

*\[21\] In my view, the conduct of the defendant after being confronted by police does not amount to reasonable grounds for the requisite belief. He was entitled to refuse to allow police to search him, and entitled to walk away from them. The exercise of such rights is as consistent with innocence as with guilt, and could never form the basis of the belief necessary to carry out an arrest. A contrary conclusion would be entirely inconsistent with the rights which the statutory precondition to the exercise of the power of arrest is intended to protect. Further, the defendant\'s presence in the Mall close to the scene of the alleged crime could not have meant anything without understanding the factual basis of what had occurred. Once again, as pointed out by defence counsel, this information was as consistent with innocence as with guilt.*

*\[22\] The real issue in this case is whether it was reasonable for Sergeant Orr to uncritically accept the bare statement of the radio room operator that a person matching the defendant\'s description had committed a theft, as a basis for the requisite belief.*

*In Cotton v Ramm [\[1976\] 16 SASR 107](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1976%5d%2016%20SASR%20107?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), Jacobs J dealing with a question of whether or not a police officer believed on reasonable grounds that a driver\'s ability to drive the motor vehicle was impaired, considered the question of whether the officer was entitled to rely upon information received from another officer as to the defendant\'s manner of driving. His Honour said this:*

*\"The least that the police officer concerned should do, before he forms a requisite belief, is to ascertain by enquiry as much as he can of the circumstances that might be relevant to his belief, if the information first supplied is not of itself sufficient, in terms of the statute, to justify the request for a breath test.\"*

*In Hibble v Phegan at \[52\], Zeeman J said:*

*\"A police officer\'s power of arrest has to be exercised by him or her personally, and on the facts known to him or her. The mere direction of a senior officer provides no lawful authority for the making of an arrest by the police officer to whom the direction is given. An arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. That liberty may not be infringed except by lawful authority. The direction of a senior officer does not provide that lawful authority.\"*

*In this case, Sergeant Orr was not acting in response to a direction from a senior officer. However, she did accept uncritically what in effect amounted to a conclusion or mere allegation on the part of either the radio room operator or some other police officer, that the defendant had committed the theft. She did not inquire as to the circumstances, or the first-hand information upon which the report was based. It is instructive in this case to note that had she done so, she may have been told, that there was no evidence that a theft had taken place and that the conclusion that there had been a theft was based entirely on the unfounded suspicion of Mr Badcock.*

*In my view, the simple statement by the radio room operator that the theft had been committed was not sufficient to induce a state of belief in a reasonable person in the position of the police officer. There was no detail or explanation as to the information upon which this allegation was based. A reasonable person in Sergeant Orr\'s circumstances had no basis to exclude propositions consistent with innocence, for example that the report was based on the unfounded suspicion of another. Had Sergeant Orr been told that the defendant had been seen taking the items then she may well have had reasonable grounds to form the necessary belief. On the other hand, had she been told that the allegation arose merely from Mr Badcock\'s suspicion based on the appearance of the defendant, and his refusal to permit a search, then she would have realised that there was not a sufficient basis for an arrest. Without any explanation at all, the latter proposition could not be excluded.*

*Mr Fairley submitted that Sergeant Orr ought to have investigated the circumstances herself, by going to the shop and speaking to witnesses before carrying out the arrest. I do not believe that she was necessarily required to go to those lengths. There were good reasons for her to act quickly. Belief on reasonable grounds does not equate to proof. Further, the information available would not need to satisfy her of the guilt of the suspect beyond reasonable doubt. The assent of belief is given on more slender evidence than proof. Belief is an inclination of the mind towards assenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition and the grounds which can reasonably induce that inclination of the mind may, depending on the circumstances, leave something to surmise or conjecture (See George v Rockett, supra). Had the radio room operator explained the circumstances upon which the allegation was based in a way which permitted a reasonable conclusion that the theft had been committed, then there may have been a sufficient basis for the arrest. However, in this case, Sergeant Orr had no information at all upon which to assess the cogency of the bald allegation, and accordingly did not have objectively reasonable grounds for her belief.*

## Trespass

1.  It is well established that any person who enters on private premises, even for a lawful purpose, without the consent of the owner or occupier, is a trespasser. This applies to a police officer: see *Roy v O\'Neill* [\[2020\] HCA 45](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2020/45.html), [385 ALR 187](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=385%20ALR%20187) per Keifel CJ at \[11\]. In some circumstances, a consent or licence to enter a property is implied by law. An example is in respect of residential premises, where the law implies a licence to walk to a door, knock on it and, if the door is answered, speak to the person inside. The licence can be revoked at any time at will: *Roy v O\'Neill* per Bell and Gageler JJ at \[34-35\]. **Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)**

Commercial hotel after checkout

**Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)**

13. In this case, the issue is complicated by a number of factors, including the nature of the premises and the rights, inter se, of the owners of the apartment and the accused in respect of the accused\'s occupation of the apartment. The prosecutor relies upon an assertion that the accused\'s licence to occupy the apartment had ended at 10am, in accordance with the terms and conditions of his tenancy. It is submitted that, accordingly, he had no right to still be in the room and, therefore, no right to refuse entry by police. I do not accept this argument. Firstly, as already noted, it is not clear that these terms and conditions applied to the agreement between the apartment business and the accused. Further, and in any event, the argument misses the point. Even if it was the case that the accused\'s licence to be on the premises had come to an end at 10am, and that under his contract with the apartment business, he could no longer lawfully exclude its representative from the room, this in itself does not address the authority of the police to enter the room. In the absence of any other lawful basis for entry, the authority of the police to be in the apartment building, and to enter the apartment in question, depended entirely on the express consent of the owner of the apartments, represented in this case by the management, and any lawfully authorised employee. There is no basis for any form of implied licence that would permit the police to enter the apartment, in the absence of such consent.

**Criminal Code Act 1924**

**267.**   **Injuries to property**

(1)  Every injury to property which is caused with intent to defraud is unlawful, although caused by the owner of such property.

(2)  No person is justified in causing any injury to property by the fact that he has a partial interest therein.

(3)  An act causing injury to property shall not constitute a crime under this chapter unless it is done wilfully and without claim of right.

**POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37 Offences relating to property**

**PART IV - Injuries to Property Offences relating to property**

(1)  A person shall not unlawfully destroy or injure any property.

(2)  A person shall not unlawfully and maliciously kill, maim, or wound any animal the property of any other person.

(2A)  A person who contravenes subsection (1) or (2) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding 10 penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months.

(3)  A person shall not unlawfully remove, displace, deface, obliterate, or conceal any --

\(a\) boundary mark;

\(b\) beacon;

\(c\) survey mark;

\(d\) mark used in setting out any work;

\(e\) milestone or kilometre post;

\(f\) sign post; or

\(g\) notice --

set up or posted by or on behalf of a public authority.

(3A)  A person who contravenes a provision of subsection (3) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding 2 penalty units.

(4)  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

(4A)  A person must not unlawfully damage or interfere with any work of a council or any material used or provided for that work.

(5)  A person who contravenes subsection (4A) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding one penalty unit.

(6)  A complaint made for the purposes of the [Justices Act 1959](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/) in relation to an offence against subsection (2A) in relation to subsection (1) is to be made within 12 months after the date of the offence.

### Unlawful and wilfully

**Willful:**

*Hodgson* \[1985\] Tas R 75.

 

Cox J said that wilfully is a word of doubtful import. At common law the word means an "intent" (i.e. to to set fire to the building - specific intent) or subjective recklessness (foreseeing the likelihood of setting fire to the building).
